Chapter 1
“Y ou don’t think I need the bowtie?” Cody asked. He turned halfway in the mirror, checking his
appearance from a different angle and frowning.
“Nope,” I said. “We already know Morgan’s not marrying you for your looks.”
He scowled and pounded me in the shoulder—not too hard, but hard enough. “I’m serious. You’re
my best man. You’re supposed to steer me right. It’s my wedding, so shouldn’t I wear the tie that came
with the jacket instead of a regular necktie?”
I shrugged. “I don’t know. Last time I saw you in a monkey suit like that was Mom’s funeral, and
we all looked awful.”
“Don’t remind me.” He straightened his shoulders and twisted his neck, then cocked his eye at his
reflection again. He adjusted the neck tie, tightening it and loosening it a couple of times before he
quit and frowned at himself. Then he shook his head. “This looks terrible. I’m wearing the bowtie.”
“Suit yourself. You’ll look like a penguin, but go ahead.”
“Better than looking like a banker who accidentally wandered into a wedding after work. You
know what I think? I think Luke put you up to pulling a joke on me.” He clipped the bowtie under his
chin and smiled at the mirror. “There. What do you think?”
Luke chose that moment to stick his head in the door. “The minister says it’s almost time. You two
ready?”
Cody turned around and spread his arms wide, with a grin big enough to light the whole outdoors.
“Ready? I’m just wondering what’s taking so long. Is Morgan here yet?”
“Yeah, they just pulled up and I guess she’s fixing her dress or something.” Luke frowned. “You’re
not wearing that tie, are you? You look like a penguin.”
“Told you,” I mumbled.
Cody fired me a look that could have heated a frozen lake. “It’s a little late to change wardrobe
now! I’m getting married in like five minutes and nobody’s going to be looking at my tie.”
Luke slanted a brow at me, as if to say, “You’re the best man. Fix this.”
I rummaged through some of the castoff coats and stuff we’d carried in and found one of my dad’s
extra bolo ties. It was two sterling silver horse shoes linked together, and it looked a lot more like
Cody’s style than that stuffy black silk thing he was wearing. I tossed it at him. “Put that on and go out
there and get married.”
The bowtie hit the floor, and a few seconds later, my best friend turned around with my father’s
silver glittering at his throat. “Well?”
I nodded. “That’ll do.”
Cody topped his outfit off with a black hat. Then he tugged at the front of his jacket and shuffled his
boots a few times, rolling his shoulders and sucking in a few deep breaths like he was getting ready
for a fistfight. “Okay. Okay, I’m ready. Let’s do this.”
He walked toward the door, but I stepped in his way and pushed on his chest. “Hold on there. You
look like you’re walking into a dungeon. Everything okay? Not getting cold feet or something, are
you?”
He closed his eyes and shook out his hands. “No. I want to spend the rest of my life with Morgan.
She’s like air to me, the best thing that’s ever come into my life. Guess I’m just a little eager to get
started!”
I dusted off his shoulders and acted like I was smoothing down the jacket, even though there was no
need. Cody was a little smaller than the rest of us except Dusty, but wiry and tough as all get-out. To
be quite honest, I’d never look as good as he did. Didn’t matter whether he was dandied up or
wearing grubby coveralls, he was all class. Whether or not I wanted to admit it, I looked like a burly
farm hand next to him. But I wasn’t jealous. Not of his looks, anyway.
“You’re getting an amazing girl,” I told him. “Don’t know what she sees in you, but—” Cody
started laughing, and I couldn’t finish what I was going to say. I just met his eyes and gave him a tight-
lipped smile. I think he understood.
He smiled back and grabbed my forearm, like we did when we were kids and swore to be blood
brothers. “You know, Marshall, I’d probably be under a bridge somewhere if it weren’t for you.”
My face got hot. I hated it when he talked like that, like he owed me something. I never had figured
out what to say, so I just shrugged and brushed it off with my usual smart aleck reply. “Nah. You
wouldn’t have made it that far.”
I felt his hand tighten around my forearm. “Right.”
My throat was choking up, so I just clapped him on the shoulder with my free hand. “Go on, don’t
keep Morgan waiting.”
“You’ve got the rings, right? You better be right behind me,” he warned.
“Sure, sure, right behind you,” I promised, but his double meaning didn’t escape me. We’d had a
lot of talks these last few nights about… well, about a hundred things, but the one that kept coming up
was… was walking down the aisle right in front of the bride. I clasped my hands to keep from
twitching and tried to look anywhere but the maid of honor.
Kelli Mason. The girl I couldn’t stand to be in the same room with, and also the girl I’d never been
able to forget. And now my best friend was marrying her best friend, so I’d have to see her a lot.
I was doing a good job of keeping my attention on the bride, I thought. Cody was getting a stunner,
that was for sure. Morgan practically glowed when she locked eyes with Cody, and I don’t think
either of them even knew the rest of us were there. She was easy to admire, so radiant and happy. But
then, before I realized what had happened, I went from looking at the bride’s lacy white veil to the
maid of honor’s wavy black curls.
There was something just deliciously addicting about getting an eyeful of Kelli Mason. Guys had
always called her cute, maybe sweet and bubbly, but to me, she was like looking at the main course
and dessert all at the same time. Other women could turn the head, but Kelli just had a flavor and a
flair no one else could match. She made me itch all over just thinking about her, because I couldn’t
figure out whether to take her picture so I could stare at her longer, or grab her and kiss her dizzy.
Too bad she hated my guts.
The other bridesmaids got to the front of the church and started spreading out on the other side of
the minister’s podium, and Kelli was turning to take Morgan’s bouquet. That was when she slipped
and glanced at me.
My heart stumbled all over itself and I had to swallow so my tongue wouldn’t fall out. She was
wearing more makeup than usual, and her thick black lashes lowered like seductive veils. Her silky
hair was studded with pearls and coiled up in some intricate and delectable mess, and there was the
faintest shimmer to her skin. Not that Kelli needed any help knocking my socks off. I was usually a
speechless idiot if she just looked at me in passing.
Apparently, looking at me wasn’t high on her list, because I only got a glance. She took Morgan’s
bouquet and went to her place, and refused to meet my eyes again. Even when the minister
pronounced Cody and Morgan man and wife and it was my job to escort the maid of honor back down
the aisle, Kelli just hooked her arm through mine and stubbornly waved at the bride’s side of the
church.
Right behind you, huh, Cody? I growled under my breath as I glared at his back. Only as long as it
took to get out of this church and get home. I sure wouldn’t be following in his footsteps in any other
way. I’d much rather be out bucking hay or shoveling stalls.
Except now I had to survive the reception.
M arshall Walker didn’t say a word to me throughout the entire ceremony. We had to sit at the
same table and stand together in photos. We took turns toasting the bride and groom, and
while my toast made everyone smile and say, “Aww,” Marshall had everyone in the room laughing till
they cried. Except for me.
We stood beside each other in the greeting line and Morgan’s wedding colors left no doubt about
who was meant to be paired up with whom. Marshall and I were both in dark blue, while the rest of
the party wore a lighter shade. He was supposed to be my escort through the whole wedding. But
when the couples’ dances started, he didn’t even get out of his seat. Just stared at his glass like a
crusty old grump.
At least Dusty was polite. I was pouting with my arms crossed, unable to even look at the happy
couple on the dance floor, so I stared at Morgan’s bouquet on the table instead. Dusty had to clear his
throat to get my attention, but he asked so sweetly that I couldn’t ignore him.
“Would you like to dance, Kelli?”
I smiled and let him take my hand. “Sure, but aren’t you supposed to have another partner? Where’s
Jess?”
He looked puzzled, then his eyes widened in understanding. “Oh, the wedding party thing? I think
she has another date. Maybe two or three.” He tipped his head to the floor, where Luke Walker was
cutting in on Jess and some guy I’d never seen before.
“I see.”
He shrugged and smiled. “Come on, let’s have some fun.”
Dusty turned out to be a pretty good dancer. Funny how I never knew that before. I guess I’d just
never paid attention to the youngest Walker brother, because I’d always been looking at a different
one. But Dusty could two-step and dosi-do and twirl with the best of them, and he had me laughing so
hard that I almost forgot about the muscular cowboy in the corner who wouldn’t look up from his
phone. Almost.
When the song ended, Dusty gave me his arm to walk me off the floor. That was a cute gesture, I
had to admit. He was kind of fun to be around, really. Not exactly one to make my heart go pitter
patter, but maybe a friend. The kind of friend who would save a girl from a bad date.
We reached the edge of the room and I looked up to thank him for the dance, but his attention wasn’t
on me. He was watching Jess again, who was being led back to the dance floor by that guy I didn’t
know. Dusty’s expression looked like a forgotten puppy.
And suddenly, it all made sense.
“Hey, cowboy, how about another dance?” I asked, hoping to cheer him up.
He looked back at me like he was surprised I was still there, and he had to think a minute before
answering. “Sure, Kelli. Sounds like fun.”
“You don’t sound very convinced. Look, if you’d rather go have a drink or something, I get it.”
His forehead creased, and he looked down at his boots. “Actually, I was going to take off early.
Cows need fed, wedding or no, and I got the short straw.”
“Ah. Well, thanks for the dance anyway.” I tried to smile at him, but everyone was spinning and
stomping around on the dance floor behind him, and he had to duck out of the way or get run over. I
saw him glance over his shoulder as one particular couple twirled by, and I wasn’t surprised to see
where his gaze landed.
“Who’s the guy dancing with Jess? I’ve never seen him before.”
“Austen Conrad,” he said, without even looking back at me. “Just bought the old Finney Ranch.”
He watched the couple for several more seconds, then shook himself like he was waking up. “I’d
better go start chores. Hope you enjoy the rest of the night.”
I watched Dusty stop to congratulate Cody and Morgan one more time, then he said something to
his father before he left. And I was alone again. I tried the innocent “check out the room” scan, hoping
I didn’t look like I was begging for a dance—even though I totally was—but everyone seemed to be
having too good of a time to notice me.
Except for Marshall. He’d finally left the wedding party’s table and was standing around with
Evan. Neither of them were talking to anyone else, but their faces didn’t match. Evan looked
thoughtful—maybe a little sad or nostalgic. But Marshall looked like a bull fit to charge. His chin was
lowered, his arms were folded over his chest, and he was sweeping the room with a resentful scowl.
Until he got to me.
The back of my neck tingled, but I refused to look away. Instead, I stared right back at him. He
didn’t back down—it was like a stupid game of chicken, trying to see which one of us would blink
first. My eyes were actually stinging, but I wouldn’t flinch, so I widened them and beamed a defiant
smile back at him.
Finally, he looked away. And he never looked at me again.
An hour later, I was back in the bridesmaids dressing room, stuffing my frilly gown into a duffel
bag and pulling on my sweatshirt, when Morgan poked her head through the door.
“Hey.”
I looked up briefly. “Hey, yourself. You guys about to take off?”
“Pretty soon. I thought you’d be part of the bubble blowing party when we leave.”
I winced. “Oh, man. I was supposed to wear the dress and be in the pictures, wasn’t I? I’m sorry if
I ruined the photo op.”
Morgan shook her head. “It’s alright. Hey, are you okay?”
I sniffed and bent to finish stuffing the duffel bag, just in case she might suspect I was crying. Which
I most definitely was not. “Sure, why wouldn’t I be? I just have to work early tomorrow. You know,
the five-a.m. shift.”
“Yeah. Okay.” She said no more, but she didn’t leave, either. Of course, she wouldn’t leave. She
knew me too well. I kept looking for things to cram in my bag—makeup, shoes, that glittery jewelry
all of us girls had bought for the wedding. I’d probably never wear it again, so it went into an
unceremonious tangle with everything else.
“You want to talk about it?”
“Hmm? Talk about what?” I asked, still trying to look busy.
“Marshall Walker, that’s what. You two have been glaring daggers at each other all night.”
I felt that weird sensation when all the heat floods from my face. Fortunately, my complexion
usually hides most of that, but I couldn’t fool Morgan. I just stared at her with that stricken, helpless
look. Before I could say anything, she was hugging me, and I was glad she was looking over my
shoulder. I wouldn’t want her to see me blinking back tears on her special day.
“I’m sorry I put you two together,” Morgan whispered. “I didn’t realize it would be that awkward
for you.”
I sniffed and shrugged and pulled away. “What else were you supposed to do? Tell Cody he
couldn’t pick his own best man? And you didn’t pick me for your maid of honor; I just volunteered
myself, because we all know I’m the awesomest friend you’ll ever have.”
She grinned. “It’s true. I’m just sorry it worked out like that.”
I screwed up my face in a half-laughing denial and shook my head. “I’m over it. He can act like an
ape buttoned in a suit all he wants. I’m a big girl.”
Morgan sighed and shook her head. She didn’t believe me, but she was a good enough friend to act
like she did. “Okay. Well, Cody’s waiting for me. I just wanted to see you before we left.”
“It’s that time already, huh? Wow.” I hugged her. “You look gorgeous, you know. I wish your aunt
Nancy had been here to see you today.”
Her face glowed, and she gave a small sigh. “I think maybe she was, in a way. I know I was
surrounded by people I love, and that means a lot.”
“No one can ask for better,” I agreed. “And you deserve it. I’m happy for you guys.”
Her eyes got watery, and she hugged me again. “Love you, girl,” she said as she squeezed me hard.
Then she was gone.
I should have followed her out. That was sort of my job, wasn’t it? But I would have to stand
beside Marshall to see them off, and besides, I was already out of my gown. I guess I wasn’t much of
a maid of honor.
After the Walker family and the guests left for the night, I stayed to help clean up. There wasn’t
much to do, since the staff were supposed to take care of most of it, but at least I made sure some of
Morgan’s things got packed in the right totes. Then I walked outside.
It was getting cold at night now. My breath froze on the air, and I was grateful for my big cozy
sweatshirt. I fumbled for my keys and went around back to where the wedding party had parked all
those hours ago.
My car wasn’t the only one there.
There was someone sitting in his truck, pointing right at my car. My heart jumped in my throat. I
was alone out there in the dark, my cell phone was buried in my duffel bag and the event staff were
running a noisy vacuum cleaner inside. No one to hear me if I had to call for help.
That was when I recognized that silver Ford dually. And who the driver was, with his features
partially lit up by his instrument panel.
Marshall Walker.
He acted like he didn’t notice me until I stopped right in front of his truck. When he finally looked
up, his face was expressionless. He just held up his phone, as if to inform me he was sitting there
texting or something. Who texts in an empty parking lot in the middle of the night?
I just shrugged and walked to my car. “Night,” I said, and I didn’t particularly care whether he
heard me.
“Night,” he called back through the glass. But he didn’t leave.
Still watching him, I popped open my car door and turned over the engine. I cranked up the heat
and had to wait for the fog to clear from my windshield. Maybe Marshall was waiting to see me
safely home? That would be a gentlemanly thing to do, wouldn’t it?
That was when his truck lurched forward and rumbled into the night. I sighed, trying not to admit to
myself what a letdown that was. What did I expect, anyway?
Besides, it wouldn’t do for me to get any ideas. It wasn’t like we had a thing, or like people
expected us to… you know. I tightened my hand on the wheel and put my car in drive.
And I tried to ignore the fact that Morgan’s bouquet had somehow found its way to my passenger
seat.
Chapter 2
“Y ou’re not charcoaling the steaks, are you?” Luke closed the slider to the patio and came to
lean over my shoulder. “Shouldn’t they be done by now?”
“It’s freezing outside. The grill took forever to heat up. Oh, and you’re welcome.”
“Tails is tails. You lost the toss. How long are those going to be? My belly button is rubbing a hole
in my backbone.”
I prodded the nearest steak to test its firmness. “You’ll live five more minutes. Go get me a platter.”
Luke went back into the warm house, and I shrugged a little deeper into my coat as I watched the
steaks. Most folks probably quit grilling once the snow caps hit the mountains, but not us Walkers.
The colder it got, the hotter Evan mixed his homemade barbecue sauce. Heck, our Christmas cook-out
was a firmly held family tradition of twenty years or more. Cold weather didn’t stop us from grilling
outside.
Last week, Cody had been out here with me. Even if it wasn’t his turn to do the grilling, he’d
usually keep me company when I got stuck with it. He’d lean on one porch railing, I’d hold up the
other, and we’d shoot the breeze. Before we knew it, Dad would be after us for overcooking his
steak, but we would have been just getting started.
Time just passed more quickly with Cody around, but he wasn’t over at the bunkhouse anymore. I
glanced at the sad, darkened windows across the yard and wondered what he was up to now. He
probably wasn’t freezing his nads off cooking for a bunch of bachelors. Nope, he was probably
having a lot more fun.
Not that I’d begrudge him that. Cody deserved his share of happiness, and Morgan seemed like a
stand-up kind of girl. The kind who would throw in and be a partner, and to a rancher, that’s solid
gold. I guess that was what had always stopped me from getting serious about anyone. I was happy
with my life and my family as they were, and even if I could find that kind of woman, bringing her into
my world would change it.
Maybe it was time for a little change, though.
Luke stepped back out, this time brandishing a platter, and I loaded him up with steaks. Evan had
the potatoes and the green beans ready, and Dusty had whipped up a hefty bowl of what he called
“cowboy salad.” Basically, it was crumbled lettuce and whatever fresh vegetables he could find in
the fridge, speckled with cheddar and bacon bits, and served with some kind of chipotle dressing. I
think the bacon bits were originally there just to train Dad to eat the salad, because Dusty seemed to
be getting more and more stingy with them lately.
We all pulled out our chairs and waited for Dad to say the blessing, then dove in like ravenous
wolves. As always, the steaks disappeared first, and none of us were talking. We were too hungry
after a hard day’s work. But by the time we’d all had our second helping of potatoes and were
slowing down on the rabbit food, we were a bit more civilized.
Dad leaned back in his chair—his salad mostly untouched—and tipped his head at Evan. “Did you
get that roof patched on the hay barn?”
Evan nodded but was still chewing, so he just pointed at Dusty.
“Yeah, I got that done,” Dusty volunteered. “Evan did the gutters on the cow barn.”
Dad nodded and glanced at Luke. “What about the sprinkler lines on the last hay field? Did you
finish winterizing them?”
“Yeah, last week,” he mumbled between bites. “I was helping install the backup pump on the well
in the lower eighty today.”
“Good, good,” Dad grunted. “We won’t have to truck water down to those cows if the power goes
out this winter.” He looked down at his plate, and I didn’t miss the faint curl to his lip as he
considered the rest of his salad. Instead of eating it, he found something else to talk about.
“The colts looked good this afternoon,” he said to me. “Cody was telling me to expect big things
out of that little roan gelding. What did you think of him?”
I swiped up the last of my dinner and tossed my napkin on my plate. With Cody on his honeymoon,
the task of keeping the green horses going under saddle had fallen to me. I didn’t have his finesse with
the polished show horses, which was fine with me, because those horses could handle a break,
anyway. But I’d grown up starting ranch colts with him, and I still say I could stick to a bronc longer
than he ever could. Not that I’d needed to. These days, the horses we were raising were so good-
minded that an easy ten-minute ride was plenty to keep them fresh for when Cody got back.
“He’s nice. Smart and pretty agile. I thought he was a little on the lazy side, though.”
Dad shrugged. “Cody thinks he’ll outgrow that. Besides, at my age, I’ll take a quiet, lazy one over a
runaway any day.”
“Oh, speaking of runaway,” Evan said, “Brandon had a horse spook and take off with him this
afternoon. Dumped him off and he twisted his arm when he fell. His wife took him in to get checked
out.” Evan lifted his phone to show a text that had just landed. “I guess it’s broken.”
Dad sprang up from the table, probably happy to have an excuse not to finish his salad. “I’ll head
over to see if he needs anything. We’ll have to hire someone on for a few weeks to take over his
chores. Any ideas?”
“Not that kid we kept hiring all summer,” I grumbled. “When he bothered to show up, he had his
phone in his hand all day. I’m through giving him chances.”
“Maybe I’ll call the local 4-H club and see if they have any teens looking for work,” Dad mused as
he grabbed his hat and went out.
Luke got up and started collecting stuff off the table, since it was his turn to clean up. He snagged
Dusty’s plate almost before Dusty could get the last bite off of it and carted the whole stack off to the
kitchen. That left three of us still sitting at the empty table. Evan had his phone out, squinting at the
screen as he sent off a few texts. Dusty was staring out of the window. And suddenly, I felt Cody’s
absence all over again.
No, I was missing more than just Cody. It was the idea of something different. Something other than
my boring brothers—much as I loved them—and talk of the ranch to distract me in the evenings.
Maybe Cody really did have the right idea in getting married and starting something new. It might be
worth considering…
But there was only one girl I’d ever gotten stuck in my head, and that door was firmly closed. I
sighed and pushed out of my chair. “Well, you guys are a laugh a minute. I’m going to crash on the
couch and watch TV.”
Evan slowly got up after me, still involved in whatever conversation he was having on his phone
as we wandered into the living room. “I’ll have to have to sell that horse,” he mumbled, more to
himself than to me.
I plopped on the couch and fished around for the TV remote. “Oh, yeah? What’s wrong with him?”
Evan made an unhappy face and tossed his phone on the side table. “It’s a mare.”
“There’s your problem right there.”
He shook his head and cupped his chin in his fist. “Shouldn’t be. She’s a heck of a mount. Big and
rangy, can drag a steer half a mile and outrun a deer. But she’s spooky, and when she takes off, it’s
like she’s shot out of a cannon. That’s the third cowboy she’s dumped. I can’t have her putting all my
guys in the hospital.”
“Is she worth keeping as a broodmare?”
“Maybe for somebody, but not for us. She’s the old running type we’re going away from with our
newer blood lines. Besides, if I can’t use her on the ranch, I don’t need to make more like her.”
“So, sell her,” I said with a shrug. “There’s a sale coming up next week, isn’t there? Maybe there’s
some fool out there who likes crazy mares.”
“N o more food, Ma, please,” I groaned. “I’ve already eaten ten lumpia rolls, a side of garlic
fried rice, a pile of roasted beets, and half a bowl of adobo.”
“But you haven’t had your flan,” she complained. “I thought you liked my caramel pumpkin flan.”
My eyes crossed, and my stomach gave a heavy lurch. “Oof. I do, but Daddy will have to roll me
out to the car with a wheelbarrow as it is. Can I just take it home with me?”
My mother sighed and looked mournfully at her antique ramekins and the delectable pudding she’d
probably slaved over. “It just isn’t as good leftover. Are you sure you’re eating right? You’re looking
pale lately.”
I rolled my eyes. That was this week’s complaint. Only last week she’d told me I shouldn’t be
drinking so much of my own coffee because my cheeks were getting pudgy.
“Oh, leave her alone, Maria. She’s an adult.” My dad shot me a wink as he helped himself to the
flan. “How’s your business going, honey?”
“Busy. I can hardly keep up with supplies. Who knew a sleepy little cow town could drink so much
coffee?”
“That’s my girl. You’ll have to open another location over in Custer.”
“Oh, no,” I told him, shaking my head vehemently. “I have my hands full enough. I’d have to take on
a partner or something, and I’m not in a hurry to ruin any friendships over business.”
“Smart. Hey, what’s the news with the therapy center? Are they really moving out to that acreage
south of town?”
I shrugged. “That’s what Morgan says, but I don’t see it happening until next summer. Cody and
some guys got a few fences up before the wedding and I guess they managed to get some trusses
ordered for the barn, but they’ll probably be building for a few months. They don’t even have a well
yet, and no one wants to start all that in the middle of winter.”
“You spend too much time with that program,” my mother tsked, shaking her head and plopping a
dish of flan in front of me, whether or not I wanted it. “You’re going to wear yourself out, what with
your coffee shop and all.”
I winced but picked up my fork because that was what I’d been trained to do. In my mom’s kitchen,
if there’s food on your plate, you eat it. And then you hear about how much junk food you’re eating.
“I love helping out over there. It’s fun work, and I believe in what they do. Besides, Morgan’s my
best friend.”
Dad set his silverware down and gave me a long, steady look, with one of his wistful smiles. “You
never could turn down time around horses.”
I blinked and fastened my eyes on my plate. Here we go again. Dad was going to start apologizing,
saying how bad he felt that we hadn’t been able to keep my horse. For pity’s sake, it was ten years
ago! I’d only had him for my freshman and sophomore years; just long enough for me to train him as a
crack barrel horse and try a bit of rodeo queening. Then Dad lost his job, and that was the end of that.
I had no regrets, really. Sure, maybe I was a little jealous of all the kids who’d been weaned on
horseback and could rope a calf before they could walk. In this town, that was pretty much everyone
but me. But my brother had to sell the car he’d worked so hard for, so it was only fair that I sold the
horse. We did what we had to do, and we pulled through. I didn’t need to go back now to rebuild
some teenage fantasy.
“Do you think Morgan would let you keep a horse at White Pines?” Dad asked out of the blue.
I jerked my head up. “Uh… well, no! I mean, it’s never come up, but the barn’s full and so are all
the temporary shelters. She just got like five more horses donated, and until they move to their new
facility—no way. I guess if she had the space, and I asked…”
I wrinkled my forehead and poked my flan. “No, I’m actually not comfortable asking. If she let me
keep a horse there, it might make things awkward with other people, and I wouldn’t want to put her in
that position.”
He crossed his arms and grunted. “I see. Well, it was just an idea. I always figured you’d be buying
another horse as soon as you got some money saved up. You’ve got your own place now, and you
seem to be doing well…”
“Forget it, Daddy.” I shrugged, blushing, and crammed some flan into my poor stomach so I didn’t
have to keep trying to convince him I wasn’t unhappy. It was hard convincing him when I wasn’t so
sure myself.
I knew he just wanted to see me get what I wanted out of life. He was proud of me. I hadn’t gone to
college like my brother, which had caused my mother no end of anxiety, but I’d worked my tail off for
eight years. Four years ago, I’d bought my first house; a cute little two-bedroom ranch a block off of
Main Street. And last year I’d closed my eyes and taken the plunge into my own business, with all the
sleepless nights and back-breaking work that went along with it. Not long after that, I’d reconnected
with Morgan, and new corners of my world had opened up, in ways I’d never expected.
I was getting my horse fix, at least once a week. And this week I was house-sitting for the
honeymooners, so I was fully immersed in the barn life. Sometimes I wondered what people saw in it,
especially at this time of year. You get up when it’s still pitch dark outside, plunge yourself out into
the frozen pre-dawn to defrost waterers and plow through crusty mud, all to carry hay to horses that
are going to make a mess out of it that you have to clean up later. In the dark, right before you go to
bed, so you can do it all over again the next day.
But yes, I loved it. I loved just being part of something so natural as the cycle of life. I loved
steamy breaths blown in my face by friendly animals and communing with the barn cats that prowled
around the haystack. I loved the sound of rain slapping a tin roof, while all the creatures were snug in
their stalls. And I loved the people you get involved with; down-to-earth, good-hearted people who
would give you the shirts off of their own backs.
Most of them, anyway, I thought, remembering one particular grumpy cowboy.
But as much as I enjoyed my time at White Pines, there was one thing missing. I didn’t get to ride
any of the therapy horses. Morgan would have let me. She’d even invited me to because sometimes
she hopped on one of the program horses for a bit of retraining, or she’d even saddle up for a relaxing
trail ride. But it just wasn’t the same as when it was my own horse to fuss over, my own goals to
smash. So, I never took her up on it.
“Paul Turnbridge was in the office today,” Dad said.
I took the excuse to set my fork down. After getting laid off from the big real estate company ten
years ago, my dad had had two options. Find another career or strike out on his own. He was now the
busiest… actually, the only independent real estate broker in town, dealing in farmland, ranch land,
residential houses, commercial properties, vacation homes, you name it. He knew everyone within a
hundred miles, and all their business, so when he brought up a name at the dinner table, it usually
meant fresh news.
“The cow seller?” my mother asked.
I hid a smirk and pretended to eat my flan again. My mother had never been too interested in the
farming lifestyle that surrounded us. Born in the Philippines, she’d met and married my father when he
was stationed over there, and then followed him back to the States to his quiet little hometown.
Ten years went by before my brother and I arrived as a dual surprise package, but except for that
brief spell when Dad was out of a job, Mom had never worked outside of the house. She had friends;
she was active and incredibly social, but the business side of living and working in a ranching
community sort of escaped her. I thought she did it deliberately half the time, because I’d caught hints
over the years that she knew more than she cared about.
My dad smiled patiently. “He’s called an auctioneer, my love. Yes, he sells cows, but also farm
equipment, trucks, hay. And once a quarter, they have horses at the sale, too.”
I stuck my fork into my pudding and peered up at my dad through the hair that fell into my eyes. He
was glancing sideways at me but trying to act casual. “Supposed to be some high-class consignments
at this next sale. Registered, sound, ‘good using horses’ he called them. Some of the big ranches
always do a fall dispersal, so…”
“Daddy, I can’t buy a horse right now.”
“Okay, I know. I just thought of it, that’s all.”
“I wish I could, but there’s no way.” Or… was there?
I had the money. I’d been saving up for a “someday” kind of dream that I never really planned to
cash in on. What if I finally did? I started twirling my fork around my fingers in thought.
I could maybe find the time. Yes, I was busy with work, but I could squeeze in some time for
myself, couldn’t I? It wasn’t like I had a lot going on with my personal life, so since I wasn’t spending
my time on a man, why not spend it on a horse?
Oh, and I’d just lost my go-to girl. Morgan was married now, and though neither of us would admit
it, that meant less time she’d be available to hang out with me. What if I did something else with
myself?
I’d just need to find some place to keep a horse. But really, that would probably be the easiest part
of the whole thing. Practically everyone I knew lived on a farm or something, and surely someone
would have a corral or a field they’d rent to me. Jess and her dad had a barn not too far away, so she
topped my list.
“Actually…”
My dad looked up, his face eager with interest, when I started tapping my fingers on the table.
“You know, maybe I’ll think about it. Just thinking, though. No commitment.”
He grinned at my mother, then back at me. “Yes, think about it, honey. I don’t think you’ll regret it.”
My heart was already beating faster, but I played it cool. “Well, I’ve got to get going. That buckskin
horse of Morgan’s will tear his stall down if I’m late with his dinner.” I got up and went to kiss my
parents on the cheeks. “Love you both. Thanks for dinner, Ma.”
They waved me out, and I got into my car and stabbed my key into the ignition. Why was my hand
shaking so much? I hadn’t trembled all over like this since I was fourteen and buying my first horse.
And now I was thinking about jumping into that brilliant, exasperating, thrilling money-pit all over
again.
I was either a genius or a fool, but whatever I was, I was going to be a happy one.
Chapter 3
Marshall
“E van, do you have the vet clearance for these horses?” I waded through the sea of cowboy hats
crowding the entrance to the sale building. Up ahead, Evan was threading his way back out
after checking in. He didn’t say anything, but he pulled the folded papers from Doc Burns out of the
inner pocket of his coat.
I tugged them out of his hand and went back to the trailer. Horses brought to the sale were required
to show health paperwork to prove they didn’t have anything contagious, and this sale took it a step
farther by requiring a soundness exam as well. It was a good policy, because it protected both the
buyers and the horses. It meant prices were higher, but this sale had a reputation for good horses
going to good homes. We’d had a lot of success here.
Evan had selected four of the ranch horses to bring today. Besides that hot-headed chestnut mare,
we had brought three steady young geldings that would make excellent working mounts for any
cowboy, or even the weekend warrior who wanted a reliable horse. That was how Walker Ranch had
built such a solid reputation. We bred high-quality horses, and local horsemen paid a premium to get
their hands on our stock.
Cody took the best of them to the show circuit, and the rest we put two years of training and ranch
work on before trickling them out to the public. Our horses brought top dollar anywhere they went,
and just about every cowboy around figured the Longhorn W brand on his horse’s hip was a status
symbol worth bragging about. Especially now that Five Iron, our youngest herd stallion, was a
national champion.
I took the lead ropes of two of the geldings and headed for the livestock entrance, flagging my
paperwork at the lady who was supposed to check it at the door. I settled the geldings in the stalls
assigned to their hip numbers, then went back for the other two.
Evan was nowhere to be found by the time I was done. Probably off jawing with some of his
friends. I tipped my hat to a few folks who were wandering the barn aisles, checking out the horses
before the sale. Our stalls always drew a crowd, so I stuck around to answer questions or take a
horse out of his stall if someone asked me to.
“Who was the sire of this bay gelding?” one man asked.
“Iron Ridge, which makes him a half brother to our top stallion now.” I pointed out the horse’s
pedigree in the sale catalog and he took it back to his wife, nodding and squinting over it like a
bloodline expert.
“Could I get a look at the roan’s front legs?” a middle-aged woman asked. I slid open the stall, let
the lady feel down the gelding’s legs, and mentioned that every horse at the sale had already passed a
soundness exam. Not only that, but I assured her that we Walkers prided ourselves on raising horses
with good bones and feet, as well as good minds. The lady smiled and thanked me, and by the way
she was clutching the sale catalog and eyeing the roan gelding, I had a feeling we’d be hearing more
from her.
She was just walking away when I felt a tug on my sleeve. “Have you been calf roping off this
mare?” an older man asked. “Looks big enough for it.”
I frowned. “I hear she’s powerful on the end of a rope and fast as a gazelle, but I’ll be honest with
you. She tossed off one of our riders when a grouse popped up in front of her. I wouldn’t recommend
her for just anyone. Evan was thinking she’d be a dynamite broodmare for someone with a good rope
horse program. Cross her on something even-tempered, though. She’s jumpy.”
“What do you mean when you say she’s jumpy? Do you mean she’s just a bit nervous, or is she a
runaway?”
I shrugged. “I’m not sure. They say she’s got speed to burn. Maybe she’d be good for someone who
rode in a controlled environment all the time. Lots of them don’t take to the open range, but an arena
is different and they’re fine there. Evan said she wasn’t working out on the ranch, so that’s all I
know.”
“Huh.” The man nodded. “Thanks for the information.”
I watched him go, then turned a sour look on the doe-eyed mare in the stall. She was a dark
chestnut, almost liver, with tall white socks and a blaze that covered most of her face. Her ears were
pert and sharp, and she had a wary look in her eye as she stared back at me.
I didn’t like the looks of her. Oh, she was put together right. Tall and long-strided, built like a brick
outhouse, with hints of flaxen in her mane and tail. She was certainly one to turn the head, but I guess
I’m a bit old-fashioned. I just didn’t like chestnut mares. And I especially didn’t like ones that looked
like they were ready to explode any minute.
Maybe it’s just old wive’s tales that make horses that look like that out to be crazy, but we ranchers
thrive on our share of superstitions. When your livelihood depends so much on nature, things out of
your control, you start looking for odd little things to blame your luck on. Like the color of your horse.
Yeah, it was probably a little ridiculous, but I knew one thing for darned sure. I always rode bays if I
could help it.
A few more people wandered by, some asking questions and some pretending not to see me
standing there. A little while, and everyone would be making their way to the sale ring. I finally
spotted Evan talking to a bunch of our neighboring ranchers. Actually, he was probably just listening
to the rest of them talk. Either way, he looked content enough, and in no hurry to come spell me at the
stalls.
Well, fine. I didn’t need to babysit the horses, and Evan would have to come over soon anyway
because he wanted to be the one to lead them into the ring. I latched the stalls and headed off toward
the stands to see if I could find someone I knew to talk to.
That was when I passed her.
I swung around for a double-take as she walked by. I didn’t know Kelli Mason came to livestock
sales, but there she was, strolling along beside Jess Thompkins and chatting up a storm. With a sale
catalog in her hand.
Forget talking to people. I’d never be able to hold down a conversation if I knew Kelli was in the
building. Fresh air sounded good right about then. I pounded my way to the door and went back to the
truck.
“W hat about that one?” Jess asked, pointing to a cute little paint mare in the first row of stalls.
“She looks sweet.”
I checked her hip number in the sale catalog. “Nine years old, says she’s kid-gentle. What does
‘bombproof’ mean?”
Jess laughed. “That’s a good sign! It means she’d be a safe horse to learn on.”
I had to swallow my pride a little. I wasn’t a total novice, just rusty, but Jess kept steering me
toward horses you’d buy for a five-year-old. Maybe it had been a mistake to ask her to come with me,
but Morgan had just gotten back from her honeymoon, and I hadn’t even seen her yet. Besides, I
wanted to surprise her with my new horse the next time I saw her.
“I’ll keep this one in mind,” I lied. “She’s kind of short-legged, though.”
“Well, maybe that’s okay. I’m sure she’s athletic enough to do what you want. It’s her mind that
matters, right?”
“Sure,” I echoed, but I wasn’t looking at the paint mare anymore. “Hey, what about that big black
horse over there?”
We went over to take a look, and I matched his hip number up with his description in the catalog.
“His name is Jasper,” I read. “He’s been trained as a rope horse. Owner’s illness forces sale.”
Jess shook her head. “No, I know this horse. He’s bucked off about sixteen people. That ‘illness’ is
because his owner has both arms in a cast.”
I slapped the catalog closed with an exasperated sigh. “I thought this was a select sale! Only high-
quality horses, that’s what everyone told me. So far, we’ve looked at three broncs, two on the edge of
retirement, and one that couldn’t outrun my mom’s old flea-bitten cat.”
“There’s always a dud or two. That’s why you brought me, right? It helps to know a bit about the
horse’s background. They’re not all as bad as you’re making it sound, you know. Just about every
horse here will be an awesome fit for the right person. You just have to find the one that fits you.”
“Well, where am I supposed to do that? We’ve seen almost all of them.”
Jess pointed. “The Walkers brought a few horses. You can always count on theirs to be—”
“Nope. Not buying a Walker horse.”
Jess blinked, her perfect pink lips round in surprise. “Well... okay, then. We could go back and look
at that sorrel gelding.”
“The one that’s pushing twenty? Jess, I’ve waited a long time to get a horse again. I don’t want to
have to retire it as soon as I buy it.”
Her brow creased, and I realized that I’d finally managed to frustrate her. “He was only sixteen, but
if you don’t like him...” She took the catalog from my hands and flipped through it, occasionally
holding up one listing or another for me to either nod or shake my head at.
“You don’t have to buy a horse today,” she reminded me.
“I know that, but I thought this was the best possible place to start looking, with all the supposedly
good horses available here.”
“Yes,” she agreed, a line still creased between her eyes. I’d actually done it—I’d irritated her
perfect smile right off her face. There was probably a world record for that somewhere and I’d
somehow broken it.
“Why won’t you look at the Walker horses? You’ve turned down almost everything else, and they
have the best reputations of anything around here. Cody probably trained them, so what’s wrong with
that? Is it the cost? Their horses will draw a lot of bids.”
“No, I have the money. It’s... complicated.”
She sighed. “Well, let’s go sit down in the stands and look through the ones you marked. Maybe
we’ll notice something we missed before.”
I stopped her. “Jess, I’m really thankful for you helping me. I don’t mean to sound persnickety, but
I’ve wanted to do this for so many years. I just want it to be the perfect horse, you know?”
Jess’s smile returned. “I get it. We’ll find the right one for you, even if it takes a while. Oh, look,
there’s your dad coming over.”
I turned around and saw my father hurrying up to us, his face pink with exertion and his grin as big
as the whole outdoors. As soon as I’d announced my plan to come to the auction, he had moved his
schedule around so he could tag along. He had a catalog of his own, all bookmarked with colorful
tabs and creased in about a dozen places. “Have you found anything you like, honey?”
“Maybe. Jess and I were just headed to the stands to look over the ones we marked. What did you
find?”
He held up his catalog and tapped one page. “A real beauty. They just took her out of the stall, so
it’s too late to go back and look at her there. Let’s get to the stands and I’ll show you her picture.”
We sat down, and I got my bidder’s card ready. Then it was a waiting game. Dad wouldn’t take no
for an answer—he insisted I look at the horse he was so excited about, even though I hadn’t seen
anything in the sale worth writing home over.
“There,” he said, folding the back of his booklet over and shoving it in my lap. “Feast your eyes on
her.”
I squinted and then rubbed my eyes. The horse in the picture reminded me of the ones you see in
paintings, with a graceful head and neck, long legs poised for flight, and chiseled muscles
everywhere. She was more than beautiful. She was handsome. Breathtaking, even. I’d never seen such
a powerful-looking mare.
I scanned down to the first line of her description. “Five-year-old chestnut mare,” I murmured. “No
name? Who’s selling her?”
“Oh, it’s one of the Walker horses,” Jess said, leaning over my shoulder. “She’ll bring a lot of
bidders.”
I gulped. She was a Walker horse. No wonder I hadn’t seen her in the stalls. “I can’t, Daddy,” I
said, pushing the catalog back to him.
“You just wait till you see her in person. You won’t care how much she costs.”
Jess had her own catalog open to the mare’s page now, and there was a guarded look in her eye as
she read the description. “She sounds... high strung.”
“I talked to the owner,” Dad said. “He said she was too much horse on the ranch, but he’s sure
she’ll be perfect in the arena. Very fast. You want a barrel horse, right?”
“Evan said that?” Jess asked.
“No, it wasn’t Evan, but I didn’t catch his name. Too many Walker brothers to keep straight, and
they look so much alike.”
“Did he have blue eyes or brown?” I asked, trying not to sound like I knew too much about how to
tell the Walker brothers apart. Luke and Marshall could have been twins, unless you noticed their eye
colors and personalities.
“Aw heck, honey, I don’t look at a man’s eyes,” my dad laughed. “I think maybe it was that one you
went to prom with.”
I groaned. “Marshall.”
He shrugged. “I’m not sure. So, what do you think of her?”
“I’d be careful,” Jess warned. “Look how Evan describes their other horses, then look at what he
says about her. He’s not hiding anything. She’s going to be a handful.”
I nodded politely. Jess really had no idea what kind of horse I used to handle, and I didn’t feel like
she quite understood what I was looking for. Dad did, bless him, but neither one of them had an
inkling of the real reason why I wouldn’t let myself bid on the striking mare with the Longhorn W
brand on her left hip. I’d just have to keep looking for something like her.
Sixteen minutes later, that chestnut goddess walked into the sale ring, and my jaw hit the floor so
hard I’m still counting my teeth. She was exquisite. Perfection. Coiled grace with a big block engine,
and anyone with eyes could see what a marvel she was.
I couldn’t help myself. My bidder’s card was the first one in the air, and the last one to go down. I
was now a horse owner.
Chapter 4
I took the truck and horse trailer home early that day because Evan went and bought a used tractor
from the sale. He said it was a backup for us, but maybe Cody and Morgan could “use it.” I
wasn’t dumb. It was Evan’s idea of a housewarming gift, and a pretty good one at that. They’d need
something out there to get their new place built.
By the time I got back with the flatbed to drive the tractor home, the last horses were being loaded
up and the sale grounds were nearly empty. I found Evan closing out with the auction secretary, so I
drove the tractor up on the flatbed, strapped it down, and waited for him. He gave me a funny look
when he walked up, but didn’t say anything.
“Did we get good prices on the horses?” I asked.
“Better than I expected.”
“Guess that pays off the tractor you just bought.”
Evan just shrugged. “The sorrel gelding went to Joe Morrison. Sounds like he’s planning to give
you a run for your money at the next roping.”
“He can try. That sorrel will get him close, but my Bay Rum is still faster.”
I wasn’t a pro at my event like Cody, but I did like to try my hand at the local jackpots, just for fun.
I usually held my own. We all did, actually. I was a calf roper. Luke used to ride bulls, but he got hurt
in high school and had to switch to team roping, with Dusty as his heeler. They were hard to beat.
And back in the day, Evan used to be the best steer wrestler around. He’d given that up a few years
ago, but the rest of us were still at it.
“Did you stick around long enough to see the mare sell?” Evan asked.
“Nope. I was out in the truck having a cup of coffee.” I didn’t tell him why I’d been outside.
Evan grunted as I passed him back the keys to his truck. Then he got in, set his hat on the console,
and started the ignition.
“Well?” I asked him after a minute.
“Well, what?”
“You sounded like you had a story. How much did the mare go for?”
“It’s not how much, it’s who.” He adjusted his visor to cut the late afternoon sun, put on his blinker,
and pulled out on the highway.
“Are you going to leave me hanging like that? Who bought that crazy brute?”
“She’s not crazy,” Evan retorted, a little defensively.
“She’s not sane.”
“I’d have never put my guys on her if she was looney. She’s just not a ranch horse. Plenty of horses
aren’t. Doesn’t mean she won’t be good somewhere else.”
“I hope you at least sold her to someone who knows what they’re doing.”
“It’s an auction. I have no control over it once the reserve price is met.”
“Alright, fine,” I huffed. “Who bought her?”
He squinted into the sun, tweaked his visor again, and worked his mouth before answering. “Kelli
Mason.”
I hit the roof. “What? ”
“You know, Morgan’s friend. The one who owns the coffee shop.”
“I know very well who you mean. What in blazes is she doing buying a horse, especially that one?”
“I guess she wants to barrel race her.”
“No. No way. You’ve got to go buy the horse back.”
“I don’t ‘got’ to do nothing. She bought the horse fair and square.”
“She doesn’t know what she’s doing! She’ll get hurt on that lunatic. What kind of idiot would even
help her haul that horse home? Where is she keeping it? If you tell me it’s Cody and Morgan’s place,
I’ll go bust his jaw.”
“She’s boarding with that blonde from the auto body shop. The mechanic.”
“Jess?”
“That’s the one.”
“I didn’t think Jess was stupid,” I grumbled.
Evan shot me a look. “Stay out of it, brother. It’s their business.”
Their business, my rear end. It wasn’t Jess’s problem if Kelli got dumped and hurt. Evan sure
wouldn’t care, either.
Okay, neither of those were true. Jess would be crushed and Evan would feel bad, but neither of
them was going to put a stop to it before it happened. So, it was up to me to keep Kelli from killing
herself. Because like it or not, if something happened to her, I’d never be right again.
“How much did she pay?” I asked, trying to keep my voice casual. It didn’t work.
“Twelve.”
I coughed. “Twelve thousand for that nutty mare?”
“Like I said, higher than I expected. If I’d known she was looking for a horse, I’d have sold her a
good one for less than half that, but it went to a bidding war. She beat out old Bud Wilkins, and he
wanted that mare bad. The geldings went even higher.”
I shouldn’t have been surprised, I guess. Well-trained, healthy young ranch horses were always in
high demand in just about every market, but to think that Kelli had spent that much of her hard-earned
money on a basket case… I thought I was going to be sick right there in the truck.
Why would she do it? Why now? And why wouldn’t she have waited for Morgan and Cody to help
her find a horse if she wanted one so badly? Jess was too milk-toast to tell anyone they were being
stupid, but Morgan could have sassed Kelli down off her high horse if she’d been there. Morgan
would have made her see reason, and Cody would have picked out a nice, easy horse for her.
Maybe that was why Kelli hadn’t waited for them. There’d never been anything easy about her.
I remembered the horse Kelli had in high school. He was a knot-head if I ever saw one, but Kelli
had handled him alright. There was that one time she had raced the barrels at the county rodeo, with
the horse only about half broke. He bucked till she lost her hat, then went wide on the first turn, but
that didn’t slow Kelli down. She put her quirt in her teeth, black hair streaming out behind her, and
rode like the very devil to clinch second place.
Truth be told, that was the first time I remember ever seeing her, and it left an impression. I started
watching for her that summer, and she and that half-witted horse just kept getting better and better. She
even won a small-time rodeo queen title on him, and he still threw a buck about every other time she
rode him.
Yeah, I admit it. I watched her for months, just a teensy bit in awe of this little stick of dynamite.
She was hard not to stare at, but staring was all I did for a long time. Almost a year went by before I
ever talked to her, and that was to ask her out to my senior prom. Which turned out to be a disaster.
And now I had another Kelli-Mason-caused crisis on my hands. Somehow or another, I’d have to
put a stop to this awful idea she’d gotten into her head. And the best way I knew was to scrape up my
personal money and go try to fix this before it got out of hand.
“W ell, here’s your new home! What do you think of it? Oh, I know the stall’s probably not
what you’re used to, but I’m going to help Jess make some improvements to the barn for now
until I can buy us a place of our own. How about some nice hay?”
I couldn’t stop chattering. My feet were practically floating, and my stomach was a riot of
butterflies. I owned a horse! Me! The girl who always had to watch from the stands when everyone
else rode. I finally had my own horse again, and I kept wondering when I was going to wake up and
realize it was all a dream.
My horse hadn’t come with a name that I ever heard, which was even better. I wanted to pick a
name for her myself. A name that matched everything I hoped and dreamed for our new partnership.
She would be my rock, like Biz was for Morgan, and my refuge after a long day. She would be where
I found happiness, even when I felt lonely. She would be my Joy.
She didn’t seem to care what I called her, as long as I fed her. That was okay; she had probably had
a long day. Jess had helped me set the stall up for her. Then she’d gone to do some other work. It was
just Joy and me, and I had nothing else on my agenda for the evening. I poured out her oats and got a
brush.
She pinned her ears at me.
I jumped back, startled. That was probably the wrong thing to do, though. Morgan had trained me to
help with her therapy clients, so I knew a thing or two about equine body language and establishing a
respect. I frowned and moved close to Joy again.
She lifted her head and shook it at me, her nostrils curled, but I didn’t back away. After a few
seconds, she gave an indignant sneeze and kept eating while I brushed her.
I guessed we’d have to get to know each other. Somehow, I had this fantasy in my head that she’d
love me right away. All the horses at White Pines were friendly as big puppy dogs, and most other
people I knew talked about the funny things their horses did for attention. My old horse enjoyed being
petted and groomed, so I was sure Joy would come around. She just needed to settle in.
I brushed down her satiny sides, being careful around her ticklish flanks. She completely ignored
me. I thought about brushing out her long tail, but I didn’t have any detangler and I wouldn’t want to
rip out any of that beautiful hair. Besides, she kept swishing it like she was annoyed at something.
Maybe it was best to let her rest. I grabbed my new grooming bucket and started to back out of the
stall, staring at her all the while like a twenty-year-old guy who has to gloat over a new car every
time he gets out of it. I couldn’t get enough of looking at her. I was so focused on her I almost jumped
out of my skin when I heard a voice behind me.
“Kelli, what are you doing?”
A thrill shot down my spine, like it always did when I heard Marshall Walker’s voice, and I froze
before I could think. Then I squared my shoulders and turned around.
“Well, hello there, cowboy.”
He shook his head and locked those soft brown eyes on me. Uh-oh. If I stayed looking too long at
his eyes, I was a goner. He was the only Walker with brown eyes, and I knew precisely how gentle
they could be when he looked at a girl just so.
But that was a long time ago. Now, he was frowning and his expression looked almost as dark as
Evan’s on a bad day. “Don’t give me that ‘Hello, cowboy’ line. What are you up to?”
“Moi?” I tapped my hand on my chest. “I’m a big girl who has worked hard, and I mean to have a
little fun in life. You should try it.”
“Not with this horse, you’re not. What were you thinking, buying her? Don’t you know I’d have set
you up with something that will be exactly what you want? All you had to do was ask.”
“Like I’d come crawling to you!”
He stuck his hands in his pockets. “I don’t see why you wouldn’t. Maybe we don’t always get
along, but—”
“But maybe I don’t want to owe you anything, Marshall Walker. Ever think of that? Maybe I want to
do this on my own, just because I can, and not depend on Daddy to do it for me like some people.”
Oops. I’d done it again—pushed a button I shouldn’t have pushed. Marshall’s eyes glittered with
fury.
“Why do you always have to throw that up at me? I can’t help where I was born any more than you
can, but I’ll tell you something you sure as heck don’t seem to know. The ranch is more work than
you’ve ever done in your life. Before sunup, and long after a man’s ready to drop in the harness, it’s
back-busting work, all for a few pennies on the pound and the hope of hanging on to do it all over for
another year. But you wouldn’t understand praying for rain to soften rock-hard soil, and then praying
for it to stop so you can harvest hay to feed your animals, because you don’t have a clue, do you?”
I stalked up to him and I wanted so badly to slap him across the cheek, just like I’d done the night
he took me to prom. But if it was a mistake then, when I was sixteen, it was a bigger mistake at
twenty-six. I coiled my fingers into a fist and shook it at his face.
“Why do you act like I’m so ignorant? I bust my bootie, too, and it’s not like you’re the only
rancher I've ever talked to. And just so you know, I don’t need your help now, so you can just go on
back home and stack all your money, or do whatever it is you Walkers do after cleaning house at the
auction.”
He shifted his jaw, a whole tide of angry words dammed up behind his teeth. “Sometime,” he
hissed, “maybe I’ll show you our books. Maybe you’ll see just how slim our margin really is. And
maybe you’ll appreciate it when I say we’re not all in it for profit. We take responsibility, too.”
I crossed my arms and tilted an eyebrow at him. “Oh?”
He fished a wad of cash out of his back pocket. “I’ve come to buy the mare back. It’s two thousand
more than you gave for her.”
I blinked. “No.”
He pressed the money closer. “You don’t know what you’re saying. That mare put a man in the
hospital when she spooked at a grouse. She’s not what you want.”
I stiffened my spine. “What makes you think you know what I want?”
“Because, like it or not, I know you, Kelli. We’ve hated each other for almost ten years now,
haven’t we? A wise man studies his adversary.”
“Speak for yourself. I hardly think of you.”
His cheek flinched, and he swallowed. “Whatever, I don’t care. Just take the money and I’ll haul
her back. You can buy something really nice for what you paid for this nutcase.”
“Suppose I say no.”
“Then I’ll know you’re as crazy as the horse is. Come on, you’d be an idiot to turn me down.”
I studied my nails and kept my voice even. “I’ve turned you down before, and never been sorry.”
“I’m serious, Kelli! This horse is dangerous. She’s not for you.”
“That’s apparently not what you told my dad when he asked you about her. You said she’d make a
perfect arena horse.”
His brown eyes narrowed to slits. “That was your dad? Then he’s an idiot, too, because I never
said that. I said she might do better in a controlled environment. The word ‘perfect’ and this horse
don’t belong in the same sentence, unless you’re following it with ‘knucklehead.’”
I just shook my head and smiled. “Give up, cowboy. I’m keeping the horse, and I’m going to make
you eat your words.”
His chin pressed tighter, like he always used to do when he was fixing to tie down a wild steer at
the rodeos. Not that I’d noticed or anything. I just imagined that’s what he’d look like. He stuffed his
money back into his pocket.
“Fine. Have it your way, but don’t say I didn’t warn you. The offer still stands until you break your
neck.” He pivoted on the heel of his boot and marched out of the barn. A few seconds later, I heard
his truck start and peal out of the driveway like he was trying to lay down some rubber.
Well, he could be like that if he wanted to. I had more to look forward to in life than Marshall
Walker’s company. I turned around and went back to pet and love on my new horse.
She ignored me and kept eating.
Chapter 5
M arried life looked good on Cody.
The thought escaped before I could catch it and examine it. What was it exactly that made
him appear so easy, so assured? I could tell he was happy, but it seemed like more than that. It looked
like he had grown somehow taller in two weeks. That, and he and Morgan could share a look and
seem to have a full conversation that I couldn’t hear.
“Stay for dinner, Marshall?” Morgan asked. “You’re more than welcome, but I have to warn you,
I’m not much of a cook.”
“Cody’s still alive. I’m not afraid to take my chances, but I can’t stay long enough for that.”
They glanced at each other, and I didn’t miss the subtle lift of Cody’s eyebrow. I got the feeling that
I’d been the subject of some conversations between them, and I wasn’t sure if I liked that.
“Well, what’s up?” he asked. “Wanna sit down?”
I followed him to the couch and looked back at Morgan, trying to decide if I needed to talk to Cody
alone. Nah, she could handle it. I’d probably need her help eventually, anyway. “It’s like this. Evan
and I took some horses to the sale yesterday.”
Cody nodded and passed me a soda, then cracked one open for himself. “The geldings, yeah. Did
they sell well?”
“Yes, but that’s not the problem. We also sold the mare that spooked last week and dumped
Brandon.”
“I heard about that. Broke his arm, didn’t he? Morgan, we should stop over and take them some
dinner.” Cody looked over his shoulder at his wife with a teasing grin.
“I’m safer with flowers,” she shot back. “He doesn’t want to go back to the hospital.”
Cody chuckled, then sobered when he looked back at me. “Sorry,” he said, and cleared his throat.
“You were saying? Evan sold the mare? Which one?”
“You’d probably remember her. I did when I saw her, because she was a handful when you started
her. The big flaxy chestnut, four white socks and a blaze. Marked about as nice as you can imagine, so
she’d stand out if you remember her at all.”
“Oh, yeah,” he said, rubbing an old scar on his chin. “She’s the reason I started wearing a helmet
on the two-year-olds. Did she sell?”
“You’ll never guess who bought her.”
“Bud Wilkins. He’s looking for big broodmares to cross on his new roping stud.”
I shook my head and glanced over at Morgan. If Kelli had said anything to her, I would have
expected her to show it. But she looked just as clueless as Cody.
“Kelli bought her.”
Cody squinted, then looked at his wife. Morgan was in the middle of moving her laptop off the
dining room table, and she froze, returning Cody’s questioning look. “You don’t think…”
She was nodding numbly. “I do.”
Cody’s eyebrows jumped, and he shook his head. “That was fast.”
I spread my hands out. “Hello? What was fast? Will one of you tell me what you’re talking about?”
“Nothing,” Cody said. “Well, congratulations to Kelli. She must be thrilled.”
I stared, gap-mouthed, back and forth between them. “Did you miss the part about which horse she
bought? She’s going to get hurt on her! I tried to warn her, but she won’t listen to me. You guys have
got to talk some sense into her.”
“Kelli’s not clueless,” Morgan reminded me. “She can handle a horse with some steam.”
“It’s not that. The problem is that this horse can be unpredictable, and she’s not even nice about it.
She’ll be minding her own business one minute and bolting for the horizon the next, and heaven help
the poor devil that gets in her way. She’s the kind of horse who would come back to step in the
middle of you after she dumped you off, just because she could.”
Cody was laughing. “I don’t think she’s quite that bad, is she? Besides, I agree with Morgan. Kelli
knows what she’s doing. If she gets in a pickle, it’s not like she’s alone. Where is she keeping the
horse?”
“Jess’s house!” I exploded. “Just because she had an empty stall in her barn. Of all the people I
thought would know better—”
Cody got to his feet and clapped a hand on my shoulder. “Hey, speaking of barns, come check out
what I built for Morgan.”
I stopped, incredulous. Why weren’t they taking this seriously? Cody was already halfway to the
door, and I hadn’t started following him yet. Morgan spared me a smile that looked a lot like pity, then
turned around and started shuffling some papers from the therapy program.
What else could I do? I walked… okay, I stomped out to the porch, thinking Cody would be waiting
for me out there. It’s every guy’s Plan A, when a conversation could upset his woman. Move it
outside.
But Cody was headed for the White Pines barn, his hands stuffed in his pockets and little puffs of
steam rising from him whistling. I jogged to catch up.
“Check it out,” Cody said, rolling the door open just as I caught him. “We have a hoist for the
disabled riders now. I got it all set up this morning.”
I paused my tirade long enough to admire his handiwork. He’d mounted the new hoist on the
ramped platform that Dusty and I had helped build, but last I saw it, the railing wasn’t up. Now it was
all done and even stained with a light cedar finish. It looked terrific, but I saw it for what it was—a
distraction.
“Look, Cody, I know you don’t want to meddle with Morgan’s friends, but like it or not—”
Cody sighed. “Like it or not, Kelli’s an adult.”
“I’m rather well aware of that.”
“Then treat her like one. Give her the benefit of the doubt. Maybe stop acting like you know best,
and maybe, just maybe, stop acting so skittish about her.”
I narrowed my eyes. “Oh, I see where this is going. You and Morgan are still planning to fix me up,
aren’t you? Well, I’m not the fixing kind. I just don’t want one of your wife’s friends to get hurt on a
horse our ranch sold. It’s called covering your butt.”
“Is that what you’re covering?”
I stuck my finger in his face. “For the last time, there is nothing between me and Kelli Mason. All
I’m trying to do is the right thing. Why won’t you help me? Go talk to her! She’ll listen to you. Tell her
there are hundreds of better horses out there for her. Haven’t you told enough other owners not to risk
their safety on a horse they can’t handle?”
He hooked his thumbs in his belt loops and shook his head. “Yeah. I’ve said that, and I’ll say it as
often as I need to. But I don’t think this is about that particular horse or Kelli’s abilities. Is it?”
“Oh, screw it. You can imagine all you want, but I’m not wrong to raise the red flag. Call me if she
ends up in the hospital.” I spun around and stalked back to my truck.
I leaned out the window to pass out the tray of hot coffee cups. “Have a nice day, Wyatt! Tell the
guys at the office your drinks are always on the house.” The local sheriff smiled and waved back
as he pulled away.
Right about then, I saw Morgan’s old truck pull in at the end. She was alone, which was unusual
these days. Maybe Cody was back to his routine at the ranch, meaning he’d probably already gotten
an earful from Marshall Walker.
When she pulled up to the window, Morgan's face confirmed it. She couldn’t hide anything, even
when she tried. “Good morning!” she beamed, all sunshine and rainbows, but there was a line around
her eyes that wasn’t typically there.
“Well, hello, pretty lady,” I oozed, leaning out the window and grinning at my friend. I hadn’t seen
her since she’d been back. “You look all tanned.”
“Yeah, it turns out that you can still catch some sunshine in Vegas this time of year. Who knew?”
“Don’t spread that around. Folks will leave town like rats off a ship, and I won’t have any
business. What’ll you have? I’m fresh out of pumpkin syrup.”
“How about a dirty chai?”
“Comin’ up.” I moved back to my espresso machine and started pressing the coffee grounds. “Did
you guys have a good time?” I called over the noise.
“Amazing.”
I brought the cup to the window a minute later. “Did you gamble? Take in any shows? Walk the
strip?”
Her eyes fogged, and a faint smile played on her lips. “Vegas has those things? I had no idea. We
just stayed in the room.”
“Now you’re just trying to make me jealous. It won’t work.”
She chuckled and passed me her card, her new ring catching the sun as she did so. No matter how
many times I’d tried to tell her that coffee was always free for her, she almost never let me give it to
her. It was her way of showing respect, I guess, but I still felt weird charging her. I took the card,
though, and ran it.
“I heard you had a busy weekend yourself.”
I let a vague smile tug at my mouth. “Where’d you hear that?”
“Rumors.”
“You should know better than to listen to rumors.” I ripped the receipt out and handed it to her with
her card. “That’ll get you in trouble.”
Morgan arched a brow and puckered her lips. “Right. Hey, I was going to go to Jess’s place tonight
to say hello. If you’re in the neighborhood, you should stop by. About five or so?”
“You think you’re sneaky, Morgan Haskins, but you’re about as subtle as a bull with a tuba. Still, I
can’t wait to show you my horse.”
She grinned. “I can’t wait to see her. Later!”
“H ave you ridden her yet?”
“Ah, well, I’ve only owned her for two days. Plus, I took my old saddle into the leather
shop, and he can’t really fix it, so…”
“You need a saddle? I have one you can borrow.” Morgan reached up to pet Joy’s neck. I could see
she was testing my horse, trying to find out how she reacted and what her attitude was like. Joy
wasn’t giving her much.
“No, I ordered a new one. It will be here next week. I had some money set aside, and I don’t see
any earthly reason why I can’t spend some of it.”
“You didn’t tell me the coffee shop was doing that well,” Morgan teased.
“I’ve been saving money since I was sixteen. I just never told anyone, and I never let myself dip
into it even to buy my house or start the Coffee Wagon.”
Morgan nodded. “I see. Well, we need to get you on this horse. I want to see her go. Do you think
Jess has a saddle here you can use?”
“If I know Jess, she just went to grab it.” I looked over my shoulder; sure enough, Jess was
dragging a saddle out of the tack room with a bridle slung over her shoulder.
“Well, let’s get to it!” Morgan said.
Joy was a perfect lady when I saddled her. She stood completely still like she didn’t care what I
was doing. That was a good sign. My old horse used to paw and try to bite me when I saddled him.
I was grateful for all the time I’d spent helping at White Pines, because I didn’t feel that out of
practice. I couldn’t help comparing Joy to the program horses. Except for Biz, Joy was far prettier
than anything Morgan had, and she wasn’t as much of a pest as he was. He was a clown, always in his
handlers’ way, trying to play or get attention. Joy just stood like a rock, only her ears twitching. I liked
that. She was all business.
Jess helped me find a longer cinch to fit Joy’s thick barrel, and Morgan checked the bridle. “She’s
not real sociable, is she?” Morgan observed.
“She’s not mean, though. You can’t say she’s bad.”
“No,” Morgan answered innocently. “She just doesn’t seem as friendly as I expected.”
Well, that much was true. A cynical person might claim that the horse didn’t care whether I was
even alive so long as I fed her and didn’t interrupt her dinner. But I hoped she was a bit like an
introvert—internalizing everything and not expressing much. I felt like there was more going on in her
head than she showed. Only time would tell.
In a few minutes, we had her all ready. I just had to get on. My blood was racing and my stomach
had cramped. Come on, cowgirl, I scolded myself. Don’t chicken out now.
Jess’s place had a pretty nice barn, but it wasn’t large. Like many of the older barns in the area, it
started life as a cowshed, but they’d added on and made improvements. It had worked fine for them
for years. But one thing was nagging at me—I’d just bought a horse I wanted to spend a lot of time
riding, and winter was settling over us. The only place I could ride at Jess’s house was the sixty-foot
round pen, and even though they’d put good sand down for footing, it was getting sloppy. This
morning, it had been covered with ice.
But it was good enough for a test ride. I’d have to figure out the rest as we went along. I led Joy out
to the round pen and checked the cinch. Jess locked the gate behind us, and I gave them both a thumbs
up.
“Aren’t you forgetting something?” Morgan asked. She passed a helmet out through the bars.
My stomach sank. I felt like such a noob wearing a helmet. “We never used to wear these,” I
complained as I took it.
“We used to be made of rubber, too. What, are you worried about your hair?”
I made a face at her, but I buckled the stupid thing on. “Wish me luck.”
I gathered my reins up and put my foot in the stirrup. Joy was tense now, every muscle at attention
and her nostrils quivering. She looked like a statue of George Washington’s horse, all taut and alert,
with her head high. She was looking around at everything outside like she hadn’t seen it before, even
though I’d turned her out here every day. I figured I’d better mount up before she shifted her weight
and stepped on me.
That was a mistake.
I was about halfway aboard when Joy shot sideways, snorting. There was only one place for my
rear end to land… right in the mud. I just sat there for a second, trying to take in what had happened,
as my horse started prancing circles around me and blowing loud snorts to the skies.
“Are you okay?” Jess called. Morgan was halfway through the fence already, but she stopped when
I looked up and waved her off.
“Fine.” I got up and checked myself out. I was covered in goo from the waist down. I wasn’t sure
how Joy had caught me by surprise or what had bothered her, but there was one thing I did know: if
you get dumped off, you get right back on. But I’d been dumped off my old horse a hundred times and
never felt as uncertain as I did now.
“Maybe you should do some groundwork first,” Morgan suggested.
I seized on that. Yes, that would be better! Besides, who rode a horse they didn’t know well?
“That’s not a bad idea,” I said nonchalantly. I looked back down at my pants. “I shouldn’t get in Jess’s
saddle looking like this, anyway.”
“Oh, don’t worry, it’s a work saddle,” Jess said. “It’s seen a lot of mud and rain.”
I would have shot Jess a dirty look for undoing my best excuse, but my eyes were following Joy,
galloping around the pen with the reins dangling between her legs. What was I supposed to do now?
This wasn’t going to be as easy as I’d hoped.
Chapter 6
A nother frosty morning.
I cranked up the heater in the pickup's cab, but it wouldn’t do much good when I had to keep
opening the door every fifty feet to get out and pitch hay to cows. About the time it finally got warm,
I’d be done with the morning feeding.
My breath fogged the window as I got in to drive back to the barn. My fingers were numb and stiff
even inside my gloves, and the frozen snow made a soft crunching sound under my truck tires. Six
inches of snow had finally come last night, then freezing rain had followed, creating a glassy sheet of
ice over everything.
People with cameras couldn’t get enough of this kind of weather. The trees and the fence lines all
looked glazed over for an early Christmas, and the world felt like one of those snow globes I had as a
kid. It was gorgeous, but it made life on the ranch miserable for three or four months out of the year.
Still, we hadn’t had nearly enough snow yet to put our minds at ease for next summer. If we didn’t
get that snowpack, water would be scarce come July. I squinted out at the mountains, trying to decide
if that heavy fog over them looked like it held another storm for tonight.
An old blue pickup rolled in behind mine, the headlights weak in the morning mist. That was Dusty,
just getting back from feeding the herd on the lower eighty.
He parked right behind me and got out, two dogs piling out behind him. The dogs always followed
Dusty when they weren’t working. I never had figured out why, but every dog we’d ever had was like
that. He bent over to give them each a quick pat, then pulled his bandanna a little higher over his
mouth.
“You’re getting back later than usual this morning,” I said.
I saw his shoulders shiver inside his coat. “Didn’t get up till five.”
“What’s the matter? Trouble sleeping?” I knew what that was like. I hadn’t slept proper in days.
“I guess.” Dusty rubbed his hands together and started for the haystack to fill the truck all over
again. “I’m late getting feed to the broodmares. Are you busy?”
“Always,” I grunted, but I went to help him. It was a lot better than rushing to my next job, which
was helping Cody pull all the colts in from the pasture for their morning feed before they got to work.
I was still steaming at him, but if he saw me acting like I felt—ready to take his head off—he’d
assume I had unconfessed feelings for Kelli or something ridiculous like that.
“So, what kept you up last night?” I asked.
Dusty threw a hay bale and shook his head. “Nothing. Too much TV, I guess.”
“You never watch TV. Last I heard, your TV doesn’t even work.” I hefted a bale to toss on top of
his.
“I have my phone. What’s your excuse?”
“My excuse?” We tugged a couple of bales together, and like always, gave each other the side-eye
to see who would throw theirs first and whose would go farthest. I usually won, because I was about
