two inches taller and twenty pounds heavier than Dusty. “What do you mean?”
He pulled off his gloves and stuffed them in his pocket. That was the last bale we needed for the
mares. “You were up, too. I heard you in the kitchen a few times.”
“That’s because that salad you made for dinner wouldn’t keep a gopher full for more than half an
hour. I was starved.”
He grinned. “At least the salad was edible. Someone needs to tell Luke the truth about his stew.”
I shuddered. “I’ll let you do it. You all set? I’d better get out to the colts.”
He nodded and started for his work truck, but then he stopped. “Hey, do you have plans on
Saturday?”
“What plans would I have?”
Dusty shrugged. “I don’t know. I’m just tired of not doing anything. Luke says he has a date, and
Evan never goes anywhere. Wanna go to town?”
“Sure. Been a while since someone else cooked my steak.” I hopped down from the haystack and
tossed a cheery wave at my little brother, but he was sitting in the cab and looking down at something.
Dusty always was one to have his head in the clouds. Probably daydreaming again. I started for the
pasture.
Cody met me at the gate with the last three colts already haltered and trailing behind him. He didn’t
say anything—just passed me a lead rope and walked off with the other two. Well, that was fine. I
wasn’t in much of a mood to talk to him, either.
Emily, the 4-H kid we’d hired to fill in for Brandon, was already at work brushing the first batch of
horses we’d brought in. They had a huge lean-to shelter out in the field, but most of them just stood
out in the weather unless it was really nasty. Their coats were thick and shaggy, and they were as
warm as little bears in all that hair. On days like this, we’d probably spend more time grooming them
than we could spend in the saddle, because the last thing we wanted was to get them sweaty before
turning them back out.
Most of these colts would be laid off soon for the rest of the winter, but there would be a few we’d
keep riding—three or four that were good enough to shoot for the futurities next year, and one or two
that were bad enough that we didn’t dare give them four months off. That thought turned my face sour
all over again, thinking of another horse that shouldn’t be trusted any farther than she could be thrown.
“Morgan went over to Jess’s yesterday,” I heard Cody say from behind me as I was cleaning the
grime off a filly’s legs.
My spine prickled, and I straightened. “So?”
I heard him take a deep breath, so I turned around. The fact that he hadn’t immediately assured me
that everything was fine, and that I was overreacting didn’t bode well. “What?” I asked.
“Well, it sounds like it’s going to take some time.”
“Time for what, exactly?”
He frowned. “You know, I was thinking. Jess just has that little outdoor round pen for her to ride in.
What she really needs is to haul to an indoor arena somewhere. Someplace big enough for her to
actually get some work done, but somewhere close by. She could use our trailer if she wanted.”
My jaw tightened. I knew exactly what he was trying to get me to say, but I wouldn’t say it. “She
should try the fairgrounds.” I turned back to the filly I was grooming.
“Huh.” Cody walked a circle around the filly, stroking his jaw and kicking the dirt. “Yeah, that
would work. I’ll mention it to Morgan. Maybe she’ll pass it on. Wait a minute, aren’t they storing all
the stock panels in there right now while they fix up the old livestock building? I’m sure I heard
something about that.”
I put my brush down and walked over to grab a saddle pad.
“But you know,” Cody went on, ignoring the fact that I was ignoring him, “Bent Arrow Ranch has a
pretty nice setup, and they allow people to haul in for about ten bucks. They’re right down the road
from Jess’s place, too. Daniel would help her out if she needed training advice.”
I had the saddle in my hands, and I stopped to glare at him. “You wouldn’t send her to Daniel
Benton’s place. The dude’s a dirty old man!”
“He’s a good hand, though. Besides, she just needs a dry place to ride. Like Morgan said, Kelli can
handle things herself. She won’t be asking for Daniel’s help. Unless…”
“Unless what?”
“Oh, it’s nothing. I’m sure she’ll do fine with that horse. I’ll ask Morgan to talk to her about it.” He
patted the filly I was saddling and walked away.
I really wanted to knock his block off. Did he think he was being funny? This was no laughing
matter! And that cute little reverse psychology attempt was so pathetic. I should have told him where
to go stick it.
Cody was right about one thing, though. Kelli was too stubborn to take no for an answer, so if she
wouldn’t sell the horse and was determined to ride her, she’d be better off doing it in safe
surroundings. Hopefully, with competent advice near at hand.
But it would be a hot day in those frozen mountains before Kelli voluntarily came within a mile of
Walker Ranch.
T oday was the day. I was going to do it! As soon as I got home from running errands for the
Coffee Wagon, I was going to pay my girls, update our website, make an order for more cups
and…
No. Those things could wait till dark.
Okay. I was going to run home and change my clothes, and I was going to go ride my horse.
I just wished my hands would stop shaking.
Morgan had suggested a great idea to me, and I wished I’d thought of it before. There was a ranch
down the road that had a pretty nice indoor arena, and since the owner wasn’t in the horse training
business anymore, he let other people come ride in it. Morgan had even dropped off her truck and
trailer for me to use. Now, I could go to some place big and open and out of the rain and snow where
I could just get to know Joy.
Oh, and it wasn’t Walker Ranch, which was its biggest advantage.
I hurried home as soon as I could, and in a squirrel’s heartbeat, I was down at Jess’s house, loading
Joy in the horse trailer. I hadn’t driven a trailer in years, but I didn’t have far to go, and it turned out it
was a little like riding a bike. Easy enough, so long as I didn’t have to back the thing up.
No one seemed to be around when I pulled up at Bent Arrow Ranch. I couldn’t decide if that was
good or bad. Good, maybe because I’d have the arena to myself. Bad because… well, I guess it
wasn’t impossible that I could end up on the ground again. I could have asked Jess to come with me
after work, but I hadn’t wanted to wait for her.
Besides, she wasn’t too keen on my horse. Joy wasn’t Jess’s type at all. Morgan’s either. Maybe it
wasn’t such a bad idea to ride with someone who was unbiased, if there was anyone around.
I paid my haul-in fee at the money box, then saddled up and led Joy into the arena. Dead quiet. I
walked her in a little circle, checked the cinch one more time, and took a breath. Here goes nothing.
Joy stood like a stone, staring at the open door at the other end of the building. My heart was
hammering loud enough that she had to have heard it. She didn’t fidget; she didn’t try to walk away.
That was good, but she remained frozen in place even after I was settled. It took a couple of tries
before she would walk off when I asked.
After that, though, things went pretty well. I’d taken dozens of lessons from a great trainer when I
was younger, and I remembered enough to turn her in lots of circles, trotting her a long time and
asking her to be soft to the reins before I trusted her enough to let her lope. Then I gave her the signal.
I didn’t think she was really moving that fast, but my stomach was instantly in my throat. Her
strides were enormous, so long that I almost felt like she was closing in on the corners of the big
arena before she’d even straightened out from the last turn. What a racer she would make! No gentle
little puffs of dust was she kicking up, but scoops of sand, big handfuls flying behind her with every
step. How did she have so much more power than other horses? It was exhilarating!
But two laps around the arena were about as much as my nerves could handle. I was grateful now
that I was alone, because I could take things at my own pace without feeling like I had to impress
someone. I pulled back on the reins, but she didn’t answer.
My heart kicked into panic gear. “Easy, Joy,” I soothed, but I was so nervous I probably yelled it. I
pulled back again, tugging over and over. Her neck bowed, her chin almost to her chest, and she kept
going. Maybe a little slower than before, but she sure wasn’t stopping.
What was it I used to do on my old horse? Turn for control? That was laughable! If I did manage to
turn her at this speed, I felt like I’d pull her over on top of me. I probably should have tried it, but I
just didn’t trust it to work. I kept pulling back, trying to remind myself that we were in a fenced arena.
There was nowhere for her to go. Except faster.
Just when the tears were streaming down my face, she did something that almost dumped me. She
was surging out of another corner when one of her ears cocked to the side and she snorted, then
dodged hard to the left. If I hadn’t been holding on to the saddle horn in terror, I’d have been on the
ground… which didn’t sound so bad right about now.
But I managed to get her slowed down a bit. I finally dragged her head around in a circle and got
her to break stride. In a few seconds, the locomotive puffed to a standstill, and I was a quaking,
rubber-limbed rag-doll flopping on top of her. Yep, that was enough riding for me for the day. I
slithered down to sweet earth, almost tempted to kiss the arena sand when I touched it.
That was when I heard a low whistle and slow clapping coming from the arena's edge.
I peered over the saddle… well, I would have, if I’d been tall enough. I had to duck under Joy’s
neck to see who was standing there. He was a grizzled-looking cowboy, like most of the guys around
here past forty-five. They either wore out with hard work, or like in this guy’s case, ossified
themselves with hard living. I assumed that was Daniel Benton.
“That’s quite a horse,” he called.
I grinned shakily. “Thanks.”
“Seen her brand. Did you get her from the Walkers?”
I nodded and forced my breathing to calm a little and gathered up my reins to lead Joy out. “From
the auction last weekend.”
He whistled again, and his eyes roved approvingly over my horse. At least, I think he was looking
at my horse. I walked closer and stopped at the gate. Benton grinned, then turned and spit out a stream
of tobacco. “Whatcha gonna do with her? Breakaway roping?”
I almost laughed. A roping horse had to stop. “Uhm, barrels. Eventually.”
He nodded and worked the chaw around in his cheek. “Ought to be a good one. You got a trainer?”
I shook my head. “I’m just getting started.”
Benton’s eyes were wandering again, and this time, I could tell for sure… It wasn’t just my horse
he was admiring. His gaze took in Joy’s broad chest, then swept to mine. His mouth quirked. “I’ve
done my fair share of trainin’.”
What on God’s green earth did he mean by ‘trainin’?’ I got the feeling he wasn’t talking about
horses anymore. I backed away. “Uh, thanks, but I have a friend who…” Inspired, I faked a look at my
watch. “… is waiting for me. Like right now. In fact, I’m late. I gotta go!”
And that was my first and last ride at Bent Arrow Ranch.
Chapter 7
“W here to?” I asked Dusty as we got in the truck. “We could get real steaks at Beaufort’s, or
kick back with karaoke and peanuts on the floor down at the tavern.”
Dusty considered for a minute. I expected him to pick Beaufort’s because he wasn’t the karaoke
type. He’d fade into the background and I wouldn’t see him all night. But he surprised me.
“Let’s just do the tavern. I like their nachos.”
“Where was Luke going tonight for his date?”
Dusty was gazing out the window in that distant way of his. “He didn’t say. Probably someplace
nice.”
“Why do you say that? Luke’s not one to go fancy.”
“Maybe his date is.”
“Then what’s she doing going out with Luke?” I laughed.
Dusty just looked out the window.
Saturday night at the local tavern was always busy. They were really more of a brew pub, with an
open kitchen, some big communal tables, a stage and a little dance floor. You’d see a lot of families
here earlier in the evening because they were a relaxed joint with good food, and the prices weren’t
bad, either. Folks who were serious about their beer went across the street to the bar. The tavern, on
the other hand, was the established hang-out for people tired of their own cooking and their own
company.
We pulled in and my pulse started pounding. Apparently, Dusty and I weren’t the only ones who
were itching to see faces other than our own. That was Kelli’s car parked right in front.
I’d been wondering if she’d be out tonight. Sometimes she came just to get out of the house. She
could usually be found singing karaoke and laughing and having a good time with anyone who was up
for it, but she didn’t have a reputation for anything else. And she always went home alone.
I guess I didn’t know that. I mean, it wasn’t like I watched her. Much. But guys talk, and I’d have
heard if they talked about her.
My knuckles were still clamped on the wheel as I parked. I couldn’t do this tonight. Kelli and I
were like Mentos and diet soda—always good for a show, but too messy to mix in public. I almost
suggested to Dusty that we try Beaufort’s after all, but he was already unbuckling his seat belt and
grabbing his hat. The tavern it was, and I’d have to just sit at a table off in a corner.
Except that when we walked in, all the tables at the edges of the room were taken. I hung back,
hoping to slink in unobserved. That was, until my dipstick of a brother saw the woman I was trying to
avoid and waved her over to an open table with us.
“Hey, Kelli!” Dusty called cheerfully. “Are you here with someone? No? You’re welcome to join
us.”
Kelli was still wearing her coat and carrying her purse, just coming out from the restrooms. She
smiled at Dusty, but then her dark eyes shifted to me. Did she know how those deep brown eyes and
thick black lashes could twist my gut when she looked my way? I stiffened.
Her smile faded. “Thanks, but I’ll have to take a rain check, Dusty. I’m kind of tired tonight. I’ll
probably leave early.”
“Oh. Okay. Offer’s open if you change your mind.”
“Thanks.” She walked off, and something about the way my little brother watched her go slammed
me hard in the chest. Did he…? No! Impossible.
But when he turned back around and picked up his menu, he gave me a sharp look. Oh, he’d better
not be eyeing Kelli! I’d put the hammer down on that one. “What’s your problem?” I demanded.
Dusty’s jaw clenched, but he shook his head and kept silent.
I held up a finger to the waitress, asking for a drink, then stared at my brother. “You have something
to say? Out with it.”
He drew in a long breath, staring at the menu, then finally pushed it away. “I don’t get why you’re
always so rude to her.”
“Rude? You think I’m the rude one?”
“Yeah, I do. You’re the reason she wouldn’t stay.”
“I’m aware of that.”
“It should say something to you that she high tails it as soon as she sees your face. She’s really nice,
but all you do is glare. You hardly even talk to her.”
I snorted. “If you want to know why, ask her.”
“I’m asking you. It’s not like you guys don’t know each other. She’s only ever been friendly, but you
act like she’s some bitter ex or something.”
I crossed my arms. “Or something.”
Dusty narrowed his eyes. “I don’t remember you two ever dating. Did I miss something?”
“High school prom. My senior year, her sophomore. A night that will live in infamy.”
He leaned forward. “That’s it? One date?”
“Sometimes that’s all it takes to make a lifelong enemy.”
“Oh, that’s ridiculous. Did she light your truck on fire or something?”
“That would have been an improvement on the evening.” I sat back in my chair and cocked an
eyebrow at the table where Kelli was seated by herself. She was probably too far away to hear me
over everyone's voices, but I was pretty sure she was catching me in her peripheral vision, the way
she was oh-so-casually sweeping the room now and then.
“Okay, spill it,” Dusty said. “You can’t try to tell me she’s some awful person and not say why.”
“I don’t see how it’s your business.”
Dusty leaned over the table. “If you don’t say anything, I’ll make you sing karaoke.”
Leaping whistle pigs, Dusty was starting to sound a lot like Luke, and that wasn’t a compliment. I
curled my lip.
“What did she do to you?” Dusty urged.
The waitress dropped my drink right then, and I chugged half of it down in one swallow. “She
made me like her.”
“And?”
I bobbed my head, swirling my glass. “And… then she split my lip.”
His expression clouded. “I don’t follow you.”
I finished my drink and held up my hand for another one. “That’s okay, neither do I.”
Dusty sat back, his head shaking. “I really don’t see how any of that makes any sen…” He broke
off, and when I looked up at him, his face had closed down and he was staring at his menu like he
didn’t want to be seen.
I turned around to see what he’d just spotted, but it was only Luke and his date walking in. I had no
idea he’d asked Jess out. She was way out of his league; not that Luke had even a drop of humility to
know that. Why in the world had she said yes?
“I thought you said Luke was going someplace fancy.”
Dusty shrugged. “Guess not. You know, I had something I was going to ask Kelli. You don’t mind if
I…?” He chucked his thumb over his shoulder toward the woman who was doing such an excellent
job of ignoring me.
What was I supposed to say, no? I made a face, and he scooted off. When next I looked up, my little
brother was making nice with the raven-haired vixen across the room. And then he pulled out a chair
to join her.
Well, whatever. I could eat alone. I called for another drink.
I guess going out was a mistake.
Mom had invited me for dinner, but I hadn’t been equal to answering all my dad’s questions
about how things were going with my horse. Morgan was still a honeymooner, so I didn’t want to ask
her out for a girls’ night, and Jess said she had a date. So, my choices had been to eat leftovers at
home, or take my chances on going out. I should have stayed home.
The last time I’d ended up at the tavern at the same time as Marshall Walker, he’d ignored me most
of the night, hanging on the fringes when I talked to Dusty or Luke, and pretending I wasn’t there.
Finally, just before I gave up and left, he’d sidled his way to me with all the grace of a pregnant cow
and blurted out that he was worried about Cody. That was back when we’d found a little common
ground, helping Cody and Morgan survive their first growing pains. It had felt good to be on the same
team, for once.
Now, there was no reason at all for him to do anything but ignore me. I sighed and ordered my
dinner, trying not to let everyone in town see me break over his rejection. It wasn’t only my pride that
was suffering, either.
I just didn’t know what was wrong with me. I’d had a thing for Marshall Walker since grade
school, if I was honest. He was two years older than I was, and one day at recess, he’d stopped some
older boys who were picking on me because I… ah… “developed” a bit early. He’d even spent the
rest of recess cheering me up with corny jokes and funny stories about his older brothers. My nine-
year-old heart swore allegiance to Marshall Walker that afternoon, and it’s never really been the same
since.
The next day, I’d looked for him, but as far as I could tell, he didn’t even remember his gallant
deed of the day before. He was busy with his friends; all older than I was, and all fellow rancher’s
kids. And that was when I’d decided that I wanted to be one of them.
Yes, I wanted a horse like they had. What girl doesn’t? I wished I had a silent, faithful friend like
that, and I envied the things the ranch kids got to do that the rest of us didn’t. But it wasn’t just the fun
they had at the rodeos. It was the whole way of life, the camaraderie they shared, the hard-won
confidence they exuded, and the understanding of their place in the world.
That was something I didn’t have, being the daughter of a regular white-collar dad and an
immigrant mom. Of course, my family was amazing, but we stuck out like a sore thumb in this town. I
didn’t have the huge extended family at home, the generations’ worth of traditions, or the summers
filled with herding cattle and breaking colts like so many of them did. I hadn’t learned how to drive a
tractor when I was seven or help birth a calf in the mornings before going to school. It sounded like a
tremendous amount of work, but when I watched Marshall Walker, I could see the joy he found in it
all, and I wanted a slice of that for myself. So, I’d started begging my dad for a horse.
Well, that hadn’t worked out quite like I’d thought it would.
And now, it seemed like history was repeating itself. My troubles with Joy were starting to really
eat at my confidence. I’d ridden her in Jess’s round pen that afternoon, and while she didn’t precisely
run away or buck me off, I hadn’t been able to get her to do much that I asked. She was too nice of a
horse for me to blame our problems on her, so it must have been me. I was still just Kelli, the wanna-
be, playing at something I could never really become, and Marshall Walker didn’t want any part of it.
I’d just finish my dinner quickly and go home early tonight. Save face and scoot away.
That was when Dusty came over and asked to join me. I looked up, confused, but he was smiling
and polite and I couldn’t say no. Maybe he’d come to apologize on behalf of his dolt of a brother. Or
maybe he was just a nice guy who saw a friend sitting alone and decided to do something about it.
Why couldn’t I have fallen for Dusty? Fate is cruel.
He pulled up a chair, his back facing Marshall and the rest of the room, and started making small
talk. How was the Coffee Wagon doing, had I been out to see Morgan and Cody’s new place lately,
was my brother coming home for Thanksgiving, that sort of thing. I could have kissed him for being so
sweet and not mentioning the horse he had to know I’d bought. He didn’t even point out the fact that
Marshall was still sitting across the room watching us.
And I do mean watching. He wasn’t even trying to hide it.
I don’t think he blinked until Austen Conrad happened to walk by. I’d never talked to Austen, but
everyone in town knew who he was by now. Rumor had it that he’d gotten rich in Silicon Valley and
then cashed out to come here, for some unearthly reason. The Walkers probably knew him, since he’d
started snapping up all the ranch land and stocker cows he could get his hands on. People said he was
a nice guy, too; friendly and unpretentious, and he appeared to prove it by stopping to talk to
Marshall.
Dusty glanced over his shoulder when he saw me looking that way. He seemed to see Austen, but
then he kept looking around for a few seconds. The tavern was crowded; so many faces you couldn’t
pick them all apart. I wasn’t sure, but I thought I got a glimpse of Jess’s long blonde hair at another
table, but then Marshall Walker stood up and my whole world shifted. Oh, sweet Moses, he was
walking toward us!
But he wasn’t coming for me.
“Hey, Dusty,” he said, jerking his head toward Conrad, “Austen’s looking for a bookkeeper. Know
anyone?”
Dusty’s forehead pinched. “Not off-hand. I’ve been doing all our books for a few years, so we
haven’t hired anyone in a long time. Kelli, do you have any ideas?”
I opened my mouth to say that no; I didn’t, but Marshall cut me off. The jerk.
“Never mind. Hey, who was it that had that purebred Angus bull for sale? Austen needs a new bull,
too.”
Dusty swiveled in his seat. “I could name half a dozen guys with a nice bull for sale. What are your
herd stats?”
Austen smiled. “You sound like just the guy I need to talk to. Can I buy you a drink?”
Dusty looked back at me. “I guess excuse me, Kelli. Thanks for letting me join you.”
“Sure thing.” I watched him go, expecting all the guys would walk off together. But one of them
didn’t.
Marshall hung back, his eyes following his brother, but his body was already straying toward the
chair Dusty had just vacated. He wouldn’t…
He swung back around and gestured to the seat. “Do you mind?”
Chapter 8
I didn’t think I’d ever get Dusty away from her. Austen’s arrival was the perfect opportunity to
distract my brother, and he’d swallowed the bait. Smooth. Kelli probably wouldn’t even know
how hard I’d been conniving to make that happen.
I sat down, put my hat on the chair beside me, and smiled, sort of. Maybe it was more of a thin
greeting than a genuine smile because my stomach was twisted like snarled tumbleweed. She always
did that to me.
Kelli wasn’t smiling back. My face fell to its usual shape, and I folded my hands on the table. “Did
you order something?”
She gave a single nod. “A taco salad. You?”
I lifted a shoulder. “The usual.” When she didn’t look like she understood, I clarified, “Steak.”
“I should have guessed. Is that all you ever eat?”
“I ate chicken once.”
She shook her head, rolling her eyes. “Better watch out, or the waitress is going to give your dinner
to Austen over there.”
I looked back at the table I’d just left. Dusty was energetically sketching out something on a napkin,
and Austen was leaning over it, nodding and pointing to Dusty’s figures. “I think she can tell us
apart.”
“Shouldn’t be too hard. He smiles.”
“I smile.”
“No, you don’t. Here, proof.” She held up her phone and swiped to the camera, turning it to face
me so I could see a picture of my glowering mug.
“Very funny. Only an idiot smiles all the time.”
She put her phone away. “Ah, that’s right. You said I’m the idiot.”
“No, I—”
“No, no, that’s what you said. You said I was an idiot if I didn’t take your money, wasn’t that it?”
“That’s not how I meant it.”
She crossed her arms. “What did you mean, then? Because it sure sounded to me like you said—”
“Fair enough. Okay, maybe I said that, but I just meant you’d be a fool to turn down the
opportunity.”
“So, I’m a fool and an idiot. Got it.”
I groaned. “Why do you always have to twist what I say? Can’t we just talk like normal people?”
Kelli tilted her head. “That depends, cowboy. Are you going to grow some manners?”
“What kind of manners are you looking for? The kind that try to warn someone who’s doing their
darndest to get themselves hurt? Or did you just want me to say nice things I don’t mean?”
“Is there an option C? The kind where you say what you mean in a nice way and then leave people
to make their own decisions?”
I drummed my fingers on the table, staring down at those soulful, dark eyes that snapped with fire.
Heaven have mercy, but if she softened just a little… I’d take her out to my truck, and we’d have the
kind of talk that messed up her lipstick and ruined my shirt collar.
“How about we start over?”
Those perfectly curved eyebrows lifted, and she shrugged. “Fine. What do you want to talk about?
The weather? Politics? Food?”
I started to answer, but the waitress came up behind me with both plates. I hadn’t decided how long
I would sit there, but that sealed it. I’d stay until we were done eating, and then I’d take my hat and
go.
She dug into her taco salad, and I carved off a bit of my steak. There had to be something we could
talk about without fighting… something, something…
I swallowed my first bite, and inspiration struck. “The Coffee Wagon seems to be doing well for
you.”
She gazed up at me through those thick lashes, and I was glad I hadn’t taken another bite yet. I’d
choke on it.
“Pretty well. I’m planning to hire two more people, so I don’t have to cover so many shifts.”
“That must be satisfying.”
Her lips tightened as she chewed, and she nodded. Good, this was going well. She hadn’t even
cussed at me yet.
“So, what will you do with the extra time?” I asked.
She swallowed. “I thought I’d become a coffee tycoon and buy up every coffee shop this side of the
Divide.”
That was ambitious, even if it was an obvious exaggeration. How serious was she? “You’re
planning to expand?”
“Nah, just kidding,” she said, waving her hand. “I’m keeping it simple. Sell some coffee and donuts
in the morning, and then spend the afternoons riding my horse.” She slipped her fork in her mouth and
gave me a look that was somewhere between “Bite me,” and “Come over here.”
I gulped, and my knife shook. “Uh… yeah… about the horse…”
Manners, she said. Whatever that was supposed to mean. Maybe I’d play her game for a bit. After
all, I’d tried everything else with her over the years and always gotten shot down. “So… how’s it
going? Do you like her?”
“She’s an angel doll. A very fast angel.” Her expression clouded, but then she gave me that
dangerous smile again. “I just adore her.”
“Well, that’s… that’s good.” I sliced off a bit of my steak and chewed it carefully. “Where do you
ride her, if you don’t mind me asking?”
“Oh, around. Lots of places,” she said, but she was looking off to the corner of the room when she
said it.
“Liar. You don’t have anywhere to go.”
Her eyes snapped back to me. “You know, not everyone has the luxury of all these facilities in their
backyard. Some of us have to drive all over the country looking for something dry and available
and… and hopefully, not creepy.”
I didn’t miss the shudder she gave, and right then, I could have wrung Cody’s neck. If Daniel
Benton had said something slimy to her, so help me, I’d snap him like a twig. There was only one
option at this point, and she wasn’t going to like it.
“You don’t have to drive all over the country.” I looked down, concentrating on my steak.
She didn’t answer right away. When I risked looking back up, her eyes were black, glittering slits,
and she was puckering her lips. “Don’t you do it, cowboy.”
“Do what?” I asked innocently.
She pointed at my chest. “Don’t you dare me. You know it’s not fair.”
“Dare you to what? All I said was that you don’t have to go around hunting for arenas to ride in.
You could just wait for spring.”
“That’s not what you meant, Marshall Walker. You’re trying to manipulate me into asking to come
to your place, but I’d rather get frostbite than ride with you.”
I took a deliberate bite of my steak. “Ouch,” I said, with my mouth full.
“And I’ll tell you another thing. I’m not looking for help from over-bearing cowboys, so you can
just take all your know-it-all and stuff it somewhere useful, like the fertilizer wagon.”
I was trying so hard not to crack a smile, but darn it, she was adorable when she was telling me off.
“You’re making an awful lot of assumptions.”
“I have an awful lot of history to work from.”
I washed down the last of my steak with my drink, then set the glass on the table and met her eyes
dead level. I don’t think I smirked, but I might have. “And what does this history tell you I’m up to?”
Her chin dimpled like it always did when she was trying to bite her tongue. It never worked,
because she always sassed me afterward, but the fact that she kept trying made me smile.
“You’re trying to rub my nose in it, that’s what. You’re just waiting to say ‘I told you so,’ and I have
news for you. I’m not interested in this little ego game anymore.”
“Good.” I leaned back and rested my arm over the chair beside me. “Neither am I.”
She blinked and looked at me sideways. “I’m… I’m not sure what to say to that.”
“Say you’ll come over tomorrow.”
“Not unless you tell me why.”
I shrugged. “Okay, sure. Maybe I don’t want you hauling off to some crap arena with bad footing
and laming your horse. Folks’ll start talking like it’s the horse’s fault, poor breeding or whatever, and
that’s bad for business.”
She shook her head and snorted, then sipped some of her water. “You’re a lousy liar.”
“Never trust a good one.”
“Alright, fine.” She set her glass down. “One time. I still have the trailer, so I can be there at two.
Will that work?”
“I don’t know. Were you expecting me to show up, or did you plan to just ride alone?”
“Doesn’t Cody use the arena all day?”
“Not on a Sunday in the winter, sweetheart.”
Her teeth flashed savagely, and she poked her finger at me again. “I’ve told you before, don’t call
me that!”
I held my hands up in surrender, chuckling a little. “Fine, whatever. How about this? I don’t call
you ‘sweetheart,’ and you don’t call me ‘cowboy.’ Maybe we won’t come out all covered in claw
marks after spending an hour together.”
“So that means I’ll have to put up with you when I come?”
I grinned. Might as well issue that dare she said would be so unfair. I needed all the help I could
get. “Unless you’re not up for that.”
“Oh, no.” She laced her fingers and set her chin on them, giving me a sweet-as-honey smile. “How
am I going to make you eat your words if you aren’t there to watch?”
I winked—that always annoyed her—and collected my hat. “Till tomorrow… darlin’.”
She threw her napkin at me.
Best date ever.
I t had been eons since I’d set foot inside the massive iron gates guarding Walker Ranch. I
ducked my head under the windshield to look up at the heavy pine wood archway with the huge
laser-cut Longhorn W brand hanging high above. Stretched out in front of me was a wide-open yard
for parking trucks and trailers, and two main barns painted red and white.
The ranch house was set back from everything, up a long driveway bordered by horse pastures and
nestled against a green hillside. Many of the buildings had been standing for thirty years or longer, but
everything gleamed with fresh paint. All the fences were solid and tight, and not a grass blade was
out of place. It was the kind of place that made me go back home and notice all the weeds and
cracking paint at my own house.
I parked the truck and got out. I didn’t see anyone in the driveway, but I could hear voices of men
working in the barn. Was Marshall in there watching me, even now? The idea shot a nervous tingle
through my fingers. Why couldn’t I get my head straight about him? One minute, he was being horrible
to me; condescending, disdainful, and just plain unsociable. But the next minute, he’d smile at me just
so, or do something outrageously nice for someone, and I’d go weak in the knees.
Maybe I’d spent too many years idolizing him as the perfect man—the perfect eyes, the perfect
height, voice, hair… everything. I didn’t even know what other guys could look like. Maybe I should
start paying attention to something besides Marshall Walker. Yeah, that was what I needed to do. Just
not look at those brown eyes of his, not let the sound of his voice send a tickle down the back of my
neck and turn my brain to goo. I went to the back of the trailer to open the door, and that was when I
knew it was hopeless.
“Hello, Kelli.”
My heart stopped. Bless my boots. There was Marshall, standing by the far corner of the trailer and
looking good enough to wrap for Christmas. He had a dark felt hat on that made his eyes look
mysterious, and a thick coat and western scarf that did nothing to hide his broad shoulders. I
swallowed and pasted on a light-hearted smile. “Hello, Marshall.”
His teeth flashed in a brief grin. “Hey, listen to that. We both sound almost civil today.”
“Give it time,” I teased as I swung the trailer door open. “One of us will start swearing at the other
within half an hour. Shall we place our bets now?”
“Does swearing under my breath count?”
“If it does, I probably already lost.” I gave him a saucy look, then went inside to unload Joy. She
stepped out beside me like a lady, and then I wasn’t sure what to do next. Marshall was still standing
there with his hands in his coat pockets, looking me up and down like he was searching for something.
Or like he was hungry and hoping I had something to eat.
“What?”
He shook his head and held up his hands. “Sorry, jeez. Are you good for now? Maybe I’ll go
saddle a horse and join you.”
Join me? Like, in a friendly way? He wasn’t just going to lean on the rail and shout directions at
me like a know-it-all? I didn’t think my vanity could handle that. Riding with Marshall, though… that
almost sounded like one of my teen fantasies come to life. I nodded and hurried to tie Joy up and start
saddling her before I could say something stupid.
I got into the arena first. After my last terrifying episode, I’d decided to lunge Joy before I rode her.
She knew this game, and as soon as I clipped the long line to her halter and clicked to her, she
stretched out her beautiful legs and started circling around me in a dignified trot. She didn’t seem like
she was plotting mayhem and insurrection, but I knew her better than that by now. She was always just
a hair unpredictable.
Marshall walked in with his horse a few minutes later, so I called Joy back and put her bridle on
while he tightened his cinch. Once we were both in the saddle, he rode over to me. He’d added a pair
of worn chaps to his outfit, and the black iron of his spurs jingled faintly under the leather fringe as
his horse walked.
Some girls go wild for football stars. Some like a guy in uniform, and others love the look of a suit.
Me? I can’t get enough of leather, cowboy hats, and a five-o’clock shadow on a good-looking man.
My pulse started drumming again. I wasn’t sure which one of them had me more on edge—the horse
or the cowboy.
“So?” he asked as he stopped his horse.
I just nodded, my lips tight. “So.”
“Ah…” He cleared his throat. “What did you want to do? Anything in particular?”
“I’d like to ride, and I’d like to live to tell the tale.”
Marshall chuckled, reminding me all over again why I’d fallen for his smile so many years ago. “I
think we can figure something out. Let’s get these horses warmed up.”
He started trotting his horse around, not really watching what I was doing. I supposed I should try
to do the same thing… assuming Joy wouldn’t take off. I put away my long line and climbed on. So
far, so good. I squeezed my legs and closed my eyes.
She lurched forward, but she wasn’t running away. Then again, we were just trotting. We lapped
Marshall’s horse at least twice, though. He just watched us go by, and occasionally it felt like that
stoic look on his face had softened into a smile. After we’d trotted both directions for a little while,
he stopped. I pulled up beside him.
“Ready for more?”
I nodded breathlessly.
Marshall looked like he was going to say something, then visibly stopped himself. He didn’t move
off, though. He tilted his head and met my eyes, then looked away.
“What is it?”
“Well,” he began, looking anywhere but at me, “I don’t know how much you want advice, and how
much I should just shut up and let you work it out.”
I thought about that. “I think my ego can handle a little advice.”
The side of his mouth tugged upward. “Would it be different if I were Cody?”
“Absolutely. I’d pay money for Cody to help me. I’m just putting up with you for your arena.”
“I thought so,” he said ruefully, but he was still smiling. Had I actually managed to tease him
without either of us getting offended? That was worth writing down! He was being really sweet for a
change. Maybe I could sweeten up a little myself.
“Really,” I said, “I guess I do need some help. It’s… it’s been a long time, and she’s more horse
than I’ve ever tried to manage.”
“No, she’s not. I remember what you used to ride.” He was looking down at his gloves now, so I
couldn’t be sure, but he looked a little bashful when he said that.
“You still remember my old horse?” I’d never have guessed.
“You were quite the spectacle. Look, just relax. You’re rigid as a board, and any horse would get
tense and nervous. This one, though, she’ll get righteously offended.”
I swallowed and forced my fingers to relax their grip, my thighs to ease off her sides. “Like that?”
Marshall grabbed my reins and tugged them through my hands until they draped under her neck.
“There.”
“There?” I squeaked. “I’ll never get her stopped!”
“Have you tried saying ‘whoa’?”
“How am I supposed to just say a little word when she’s trying to break Secretariat’s record? And
you want my reins dragging down by her knees?”
“Well, you need to pick up sometimes, but get used to not hanging onto her mouth. That’s…” He
stopped himself. Then he moved his horse around so he could look me in the eye, and he waited until
I was looking back. “I know you’ll think I sound arrogant when I say this. Promise you won’t slap
me?”
“I haven’t slapped you in ten years.”
He smiled. A real smile. Maybe even a flirty smile. “That was a punch, but let’s not break the
streak. I was going to say that we train all our horses to work on a loose rein. Easier on the horses,
easier on the cowboy. We work hard enough as it is, and we don’t want to be rough on them on top of
asking for so much.”
I nodded. “Makes sense, but it will take some real getting used to.” If I didn’t die in the process.
“Well, get used to it, because there’s more. This horse is hot, and she has a temper, and she’ll fight
you if you get in her face more than necessary. You pull, she’ll run harder. I’m guessing you’ve found
that out already.”
“Maybe,” I drawled with a guilty smile. “I’ll never tell.”
“Now you’re keeping secrets from me?” He was grinning now, those brown eyes twinkling under
his hat. “What if you don’t tell me something important? That’s how we both get in trouble, you
know.”
My palms were sweating inside my gloves, and my stomach and my heart had switched places. He
was leaning forward, almost close enough to put his hand on my saddle horn. Like I used to imagine a
cowboy might do, right before he kissed me.
I gave him a roguish smile, tipped a little forward myself, and whispered, “Oh, I’m no stranger to
trouble.”
Chapter 9
S he was every bit the firecracker I remembered from high school, and then some. I was in big
trouble.
The thing was, Kelli Mason had always terrified me. It was more than just watching with my teeth
clenched as she streaked around the arena with her hair on fire. And it was more than wondering
when—not if—that crazy horse of hers would do something totally unpredictable. I couldn’t bear to
think of her getting hurt or scared on that horse, but that wasn’t what had me sweating bullets on a
thirty-degree day.
It was the feeling of utter panic in my heart when she smiled at me.
We were nothing alike. We couldn’t be in the same room for more than five minutes without sniping
at each other. So why were my insides turning to butter whenever I got close to her? I was having
flashbacks to prom night, and that didn’t help one bit.
I’d picked her up in my first truck, all dressed up in a suit for maybe the second time in my life. Her
dad had been friendly and easy-going. He had hair back then, and he seemed to know everything about
my dad. We got along fine for five minutes, but then her mom walked in.
She was a talkative spitfire who had the whole evening planned out for me before I’d even met her.
Where we’d go for dinner, how I’d park my truck under the light by the school so we couldn’t hide in
the back lot unobserved, how I should offer Kelli the corsage I’d brought, you name it. She probably
had our honeymoon scheduled and our kids named.
Right about the time I was tugging at my tie and plotting my escape, Kelli had come out of her
bedroom in her prom dress… and then, she was all that mattered. To this day, whenever I see a
woman wearing a form-fitting wine-colored dress, I remember Kelli Mason at sixteen, with her thick
hair curled and soft around her shoulders, and those eyes… I have to push my hand on my chest to
keep my heart inside when I think of it.
The evening had started so well. I took her out for a nice dinner, a double date with Cody and his
ex, Courtney. Kelli was funny and sweet, and she had me wrapped around her finger by the time our
sodas arrived. Cody’s date was the most popular girl in our class, but Kelli made Courtney look like
a stale muffin. You just had to stare at her, and I was the one who got to have her on my arm.
The dance itself was even better. I’d never really danced before, but with Kelli, things just clicked,
and it didn’t matter if I didn’t remember the steps. I didn’t know her very well, but by the time we left
the dance, I’d been ready to ask for more than just one date with her. Maybe a lot more.
The ride home was when it all turned south.
I probably started it. I’d always known that I couldn’t get serious about any girl who couldn’t do
the life I wanted, and Kelli hadn’t grown up the way I had. It only made sense in my mind to start
asking questions and laying out plans.
Maybe I could have waited till a second date for that. Or maybe I could have avoided calling her
dad a city boy with a plush desk job. How was I to know he’d just been laid off?
But the real kicker came when I said something about her horse being a bronc. For the record, he
was, but she didn’t tell me she’d just found out she’d have to sell him. Not that it mattered anyway,
because I said it all wrong.
I’d meant to say that I’d make sure any girl I was with would have her pick of the best horses we
had, and she wouldn’t have to just make do with whatever reject brute she could afford. Today, I
cringe at how conceited that sounds in my head—I was a hormone-crazed eighteen-year-old, after all
—but I think it sounded even worse in real life.
Maybe it was my timing. I don’t know, is it a good idea to have your arm around a girl, gazing at
the stars and talking about the crazy ideas stirring in your soul, letting your heart ramble ahead of your
brain? It’s probably not a good plan to whisper something about how you could give her so much that
she didn’t already have, then lean in for a kiss. I meant it in the best way possible, but my brain and
my mouth weren’t working right at the moment. In hindsight, I should have just told her she was
beautiful.
She let me kiss her, though. For about a second. Then I was seeing stars.
And it was happening all over again. She had me reeled in, thinking maybe we could make a go of
it together. Maybe we could finally get past my stubborn arrogance and her wounded pride, and just
learn to assume the best about each other. We’d tried that a few months back, when Cody and Morgan
were first getting together, but we fizzled like baking soda and vinegar. What made me think now
could be any different?
I guess because I wanted it to be different. Because of all the girls I’d ever dated—and there hadn’t
been that many, because I was picky—Kelli Mason was the only one who ever got to my heart. And
she’d stayed there for ten years, despite all my efforts at kicking her out.
I knew one thing, though. All those years, I’d lived in fear of hearing about her settling down with
someone else. If Kelli Mason ever claimed a man for her own, I wanted to be the one she picked.
Maybe we were on the right track, because we didn’t fight once when she came and rode with me.
We joked about it, but it didn’t happen. And I think I saw something that day that I’d missed for ten
years; something that might have made everything different.
I saw her dream. And I saw that I could help her build it, or I could tear it down all over again.
We didn’t dance off into the sunset that afternoon, but we did practice running loops around a
barrel together. We made quite a sight, with me on a futurity-bound cutting horse that was almost
shorter than the barrel, and Kelli on a former ranch horse that had never seen a barrel in her life. But
we had fun, and at the end of the day, I asked her to come back and do it again sometime.
She said yes.
“Y ou’re going back tomorrow?” Morgan asked over the phone.
I bit my lip. “Is that a problem? I know you didn’t really mean for me to keep your trailer
indefinitely.”
“No, no, that’s fine. I don’t need it anytime soon.” There was a pause on the line, and then—“So,
how’s it going?”
“You mean with the horse or with the guy?”
She giggled. “Either or both.”
I sighed and looked up at the ceiling as I thought about how to answer. “Let’s just say… I haven’t
been injured yet.”
“Kind of a low bar, isn’t it?”
“I’ll take what I can get. So, uhm…” I fingered the hem of my sweatshirt, trying to decide if I could
ever look Morgan in the eye again if I asked what I really wanted to ask. Heck with it. “Is, uh, Cody
going to be over there riding colts tomorrow?”
“Not if I tell him we need to work on something around here.”
“You’re a saint, Morgan,” I swore. “A blessed saint.”
She laughed on the other end. “Have a good time.”
“Oh, I intend to.” I hung up and went to sleep with visions of cowboys dancing in my head.
The next afternoon, Marshall was waiting for me with a big grin on his face. The snow was just
starting up again, and I’d honestly thought about not driving out on roads that could turn slick later.
The weather man said the worst of it would hold off till nightfall, but you never knew. When I saw
Marshall watching for me, all six feet two inches of cowboy scrumptiousness, I was glad I’d risked
it.
I hopped out of the truck and he walked over, his thumb hooked casually in his jeans pocket and his
hat tipped to the side. If he’d stood in the mirror and practiced that look, he couldn’t have nailed it
any better. He leaned against the truck and raised his eyebrows at me, turning the corny smolder up to
the max. “Howdy.”
“That’s it?” I asked, laughing. “Howdy?”
“Well, I’d have said, ‘Howdy, sweetheart,’ but I didn’t want you to get back in the truck and leave.”
“Ah. Be a gentleman, then.” I handed him the keys to the trailer tack room. “How about you haul my
stuff inside so I don’t have to saddle up out here in the snow?”
He took the keys and tipped his hat. “Anything for you… sweetheart.” I swatted at his chest, but
honestly, I could almost get used to it when he said it like that.
I unloaded Joy and brought her inside. Instead of helping me put my saddle on, Marshall met me
with a long rope and a stick with a piece of plastic tied at the end. “What’s that for?”
He held up the stick. “It’s a training flag. We do this with all the colts when we start them. We use it
for lots of things, but one is to teach the colts how to respond when something frightens them. I thought
maybe it would help your horse with her skittishness.”
“Didn’t she do that when she was young?”
“Yes, but maybe we could give her a refresher. Evan said that she bolted and ran hard when that
grouse popped up, so if we can remind her to stop and look when she’s afraid, maybe you’ll have an
easier time with her.”
I shrugged. “I’m willing to give it a shot. How do we start?”
His teeth showed again. “I was hoping you’d ask. Come over here.”
I didn’t expect that this exercise Marshall suggested would involve so much… togetherness. If it
was any other guy, I’d wonder if he planned it that way. I was almost wrapped in his arms as we
worked with Joy.
He was holding a long rope attached to her halter and schooling her on where to move her feet, and
my job was to trigger her by waving the flag around different parts of her body. When she got tense,
Marshall gave her a command to stand still instead of bolting. When she responded by waiting and
looking, I rewarded her by taking the flag away and we praised her.
As we moved around Joy, I was close enough to Marshall to smell his after-shave. All I had to do
was turn my head to the side, and I was bumping into his shoulder, and quite a few times, his chest
brushed my back. I’d be lying if I said it wasn’t intoxicating. Who knew that a bit of ground training
with a horse could be such a, uh, stimulating experience?
We worked with her for about half an hour, and the whole time, I was swirling in a hormone-
induced psychosis. Marshall Walker on a bad day had always been my biggest weakness, but turn up
the niceness and let him help me with something that was so important to me? Check, please.
I needed to say something to kick my brain back into gear, or I’d trip over his spurs and drag us
both down to… actually, that didn’t sound like such a bad idea. I coughed and took a deep breath
before I could get too carried away. “So, last I knew, the world isn’t full of giant plastic bags waiting
to jump out and scare a horse. What do we do when she sees a grouse again?”
“Doesn’t matter. You just keep doing things like this with her. Teach her to respond to your voice,
and she’ll remember it when it counts. See, she’s getting it now.”
He was right. Joy was still cocking an ear and lifting her head whenever I flapped the plastic near
her, but she wasn’t jumping out of her skin anymore. In fact, she looked pretty relaxed compared to
when we’d started. And when I said “Whoa,” she froze like a statue.
“Try it alone,” Marshall encouraged me, and he stepped back.
Doing it on my own wasn’t nearly as much fun as almost tangling boots with him, but it was still
pretty satisfying. Joy was responding to my voice no matter what was happening with the plastic, and
when I praised and petted her, she actually reached out with her nose like she wanted more affection.
That was a first! Maybe she was warming up to me. Maybe we were even on our way to becoming a
team.
“That’s excellent. I’d call that a good lesson for her,” Marshall said after a few more minutes.
I was rubbing her forehead, and I turned to grin at him in triumph. “This was amazing! Shall we
ride now?”
“I’m game, but have you looked outside?”
I turned around and saw the driveway where I’d parked Morgan’s truck. At least two inches of
snow covered the hood, and more was falling. “It wasn’t supposed to snow that hard yet! We’d
probably better call it a day. I didn’t realize it was coming down that fast.” Like I could notice
anything but the handsome cowboy beside me.
He looked as disappointed as I felt. “Yeah. You don’t want to drive when it gets really slick later.
Are you sure it won’t be too slick to drive now?”
I was coiling up the rope on Joy’s halter and zipping my coat. “Should be fine while it’s fresh.
Later, when it freezes will be a different matter.”
“Aren’t you nervous, though?”
I stiffened suspiciously. “I’m not nervous about driving on snowy roads.”
“Are you sure? Driving a truck and trailer is a lot different from your car.”
The back of my neck prickled in defiance. “What, don’t you think I can handle it? I grew up driving
around here too, you know.”
A glint of something appeared in his eye. “I think you probably haven’t had much practice hauling a
horse in a snowstorm,” he said, with an edge to his voice that wasn’t there earlier.
There it was again. That old condescension, acting like he was better than me. I’d been a fool to
think it wouldn’t come back. I narrowed my eyes and stuck out my chin. “Maybe not, but I’ve got
snow tires and four-wheel-drive. Unless it’s sheet ice, I’ll be fine.”
“But maybe it’s not a good idea,” he repeated between clenched teeth. “Why do you always have to
argue with everything I say?”
“And maybe I can take care of myself. Why do you always have to doubt me?”
He leaned in, his face lowered till it was level with mine, and the steam of his breath warmed my
cheeks. “It’s not about doubting you, it’s about looking out for you, and I wish you’d let me for a
change.”
I blinked and my heart about leaped out of my throat. Was that true? Because if it was, that put a
whole different spin on things.
“You… you’re trying to l-look out f-for m-me?” I pulled my shoulders back and tried to quit
shivering. Or maybe the cold wasn’t why I couldn’t stop stammering. “Wh-what do you m-mean by
that?”
“This.” His gloves hit the dirt, and an instant later, warm hands cupped my cheeks and then… oh,
Marshall didn’t kiss timidly. He took over, teasing my mouth open and flooding me with warmth
and feeling. His arms slid down around me and his hat covered my face as he nibbled my lower lip
and his tongue danced with mine.
I wasn’t exactly being passive. At the first touch of his lips, an electric shock set my nerves afire,
and I pushed up against his chest with all the impatience of ten years’ worth of frustrated feelings.
When he tugged me closer, I boosted myself up by his shoulders, and he even let me step up on his
boots so I could reach him better. I was kissing Marshall Walker with everything I had, and neither of
us seemed to want to be the first one to back down.
Finally, he settled it by kissing his way up to my ear and shuddering a ragged breath. “I’ve been
waiting a long time to do that, sweetheart.”
I pushed his hat off and held his face between my hands. “Me too, cowboy.”
Chapter 10
K elli stayed the night with me.
Well, not like that. It took us a while to come up with a plan because we kept disagreeing on
the details, but after ten years of being best enemies, it seemed we had finally figured out a way to
settle our differences. And suddenly, I wasn’t in a hurry to win any arguments.
“How about this,” she proposed after one breathless negotiation session, “We put Joy in a stall
here until tomorrow or whenever the roads clear, and then you drive me home in your big truck.”
“How will I get home? Those roads aren’t getting any better, the longer we ‘talk’ about it. Unless
you want to let me crash on your couch.”
“Oh, that’s a terrible idea,” she scoffed between kisses as she wrapped her arms around my
shoulders. “What if we get snowed in? Then I’m stuck with you.”
I started nibbling her neck, my hands up inside the back of her coat. “You’ll hardly know I’m
there,” I murmured.
“Do you snore?”
“I don’t know. Wanna find out?”
She laughed. “No.”
“Come on, it’s for science.” I slipped my hands down her waist and cupped her curvy butt.
She jumped. “Whoa! Slow down there, Butch. You’ll make me break my vow.”
I moved my hands somewhere safer. “What vow is that?”
She caught her fingers behind my ear and tugged me down for another kiss. “Not to slap you again.”
“That was a punch, but I can do without another one. Okay, no funny business. Really. How about
we do this? Put the horse in a stall, and you stay in the bunkhouse.”
“Cody’s old place?”
“Yeah, why not? You’d have everything you need, including a lock on your door, and instead of
going out on the roads, I’ll cook you dinner here.”
She tickled the back of my neck, sending electricity to every nerve of my body. “Let me guess.
Steak?”
“I also know how to make a mean hot cocoa.” I followed this with a little more of my new favorite
form of argument. The kind that messed up her hair and left my top shirt buttons hanging open.
“Okay, you win,” she finally agreed. “But I’m kicking you out, eventually.”
“A lot can happen before ‘eventually’.”
“Y ou didn’t tell me your hot cocoa had whole milk, real whipped cream, and Peppermint
Schnapps.” Kelli snuggled into the crook of my arm on the couch, her hair spilling over my
shoulder, and she sipped some more of her drink. “That’s not fair, you know.”
I buried my face in her hair. I couldn’t help it—I’d wanted to do that for so many years. “You know
how the saying goes. It’s all fair in love and war.”
She tipped her mug to her mouth and quirked her brows at me as she licked the chocolatey foam
from her rosy lips. “So, which one are we doing today, love or war?”
“Do we have to pick?” I leaned down to kiss her sweet, warm mouth. It started softly, but I was
learning that it’s serious business when Kelli kisses. We both had to set our mugs aside, and soon she
had her arms wrapped around me, her fingers snaking through my hair, and her knee hooked around
mine as we toppled over together.
My whole body was almost in pain. I wanted her so much, but my heart was too happy to protest
when she finally wriggled out from under me. I sat up, pulling her beside me, and we went back to
snuggling, drinking our now-cold cocoa, and swapping smoldering looks. The woman had me
hobbled and hog-tied.
“So, about that question,” she ventured a bit later as she stroked her foot up the side of my calf.
My eyes were following her foot, and my brain wasn’t far behind, but when she left her sentence
dangling, I met her gaze and smiled like a lush. “What about it?”
“What are we doing?”
I took a deep breath to shake the haze from my brain. This sounded like a conversation for clear
thinking. “Do we have to know now?”
She lifted one shoulder. “I’d like to.”
“Okay.” I took another swallow of my cold chocolate and put it down. “Well, I’d like to see you a
lot. Kiss you whenever we feel like it. Maybe take you out every weekend. And I guess we see where
it goes from there, but I’m not interested in just fooling around. I think I’m pretty serious about this.
What were you thinking?”
She hid a shy smile behind her mug. “Kind of that.”
I had my arm behind her, and I traced my fingers around the edge of her ear. She was so warm and
so… so real. All those years when I couldn’t get her out of my head, I’d wondered if she would feel
like this, and now I wasn’t sure what to do with the power of the reality of her. It rocked my insides
and made me wonder if I was even sane enough to recognize what was really happening. “Does that
scare you?” I asked softly.
Kelli caught her lower lip between her teeth. “Yeah. I’m worried we’ll screw it up. We’ve never
played the game by the rules.”
“There are no rules, you know. Only what we make ourselves.”
She finished her mug and set it on the table, then curled her knees under herself and leaned
forward. “And how good have we been at that?”
“Not very. But I’m willing to learn. I mean, hey, if a bonehead like Cody can figure out how to
make a relationship work, maybe there’s hope for me.”
Kelli giggled. “Except Morgan’s a lot nicer than I am. I’m way too mouthy and I don’t know when
to lay off.”
I wrapped my arm around her again and pulled her against my shoulder. “I like mouthy Kelli. I like
how you stand up for yourself and won’t let go of something important to you.”
She twisted a little in my arms to put her elbow on the back of the couch and smile up at me.
“That’s not always true because I’ve made you pretty angry in the past.”
“And I haven’t provoked you? Come on, we both have some habits to change. I figure as long as
we make up afterward…”
She tilted her head and rolled her eyes up to the ceiling. “Explain to me how this ‘making up’
happens.”
“Oh, sweetheart, now you’re speaking my language. Come here.”
I learned a lot of things that evening. First, I learned Kelli Mason is like Christmas candy—sweetly
spicy, colorful, and impossible to put down. Second, I discovered that she’d been aching for me all
those years as sharply as I’d been pining for her. What a waste! But that was all over now, and the
reason was because of the most important thing I learned.
When I stopped worrying so much about what I wanted and what I thought should be done, and
tried to please her instead, a whole world opened up before me.
From now on, things were going to be different. Life was going to be brighter and my days were
going to have a bigger purpose than just routine because I had found what I really wanted. And when I
kissed her goodnight, holding her close under the porch light of the old bunkhouse, my neck tingling
from the frost and a faded moon over the distant mountains, I think I discovered I was what she really
wanted, too.
“Y ou’re kidding. I don’t believe it!” Jess gasped on the phone. “I thought you and Marshall
weren’t even on speaking terms, and now you’re crashing at the bunkhouse and snogging with
him over hot cocoa?”
“Scout’s honor. Wait, what does ‘snogging’ mean?”
Jess laughed. “Never mind. I read too many British romance novels. So… how is it?”
I kicked back on the couch, my stockinged feet covered in a patchwork quilt made by Marshall’s
mother. “The bunkhouse? Oh, it’s okay once you get the wood stove to vent properly so that it will
heat the place. Not my idea of a vacation home, but—”
“Marshall must be pretty forgettable if you’re talking about the bunkhouse instead.”
“Ooh, that was a good burn! I never knew you had it in you, Jess.”
“I’m not as sweet as I look!”
“Yeah, I guess not. And for the record, Marshall’s anything but forgettable.”
She sighed on the other end, and I could almost imagine her lily-white hands fluttering at her heart
as she smiled sentimentally up at whatever saints in heaven presided over romance. “I knew he was a
softie deep down.”
“Softie? No way, he’s… well, never mind. So, anyway, I’d have called you sooner, but I was sort
of distracted. Obviously, I won’t be bringing Joy back to the barn tonight. I’ll head home with her in
the morning when they get the roads plowed.”
“Oh, haven’t you heard? They’re expecting freezing rain about midnight, and then more snow at
about two that will last most of tomorrow.”
“I hadn’t heard that. Is that the updated weather report, or is that Jess the Cupid talking?”
“Definitely not Cupid. You may be stuck there until tomorrow afternoon at the earliest. Maybe the
next day, if the roads get bad enough.”
“Hmm. I guess we’ll have to see, but I was supposed to bring more supplies in to the Coffee Wagon
tomorrow. They’ll run out of cups by eight.”
“Not if everyone else has to stay home, too. It’s not just the outskirts of town that are expected to be
bad.”
“Good point. I’ll get in touch with Missy. She’s supposed to be on shift tomorrow.”
“Okay. Stay safe and be sure to make the most of your time there.”
“Wow.” I laughed. “Now I know you’re not as sweet as you look. Catch you tomorrow or
sometime, Jess.”
“Good night!”
I hung up and stretched out on the couch. I fired off a quick text to Missy, telling her to open an hour
later tomorrow, and to not go in at all if the roads were dangerous. Then I leaned back, twirling a
hank of hair around my finger, and daydreamed about all that had happened in the last few hours. Was
it even real?
But it must be, because I was staying in the bunkhouse and Marshall’s cocoa mug was still on the
coffee table beside mine. I threw the quilt over my head and squealed. I was dating Marshall Walker!
And we were actually getting along! I should probably head off to bed, but the couch was comfy.
Besides, it still smelled like Marshall. I drank in a contented sigh, tucked the quilt under my chin, and
sank into blissful dreams.
T he next morning, I awoke at six to a text from Marshall. Breakfast is cooking. Let me know
Already? I straightened and pushed the hair out of my face to send back a reply. Give me ten
Ten minutes later, I heard a utility vehicle chug up to the door. Marshall had the side-by-side, and
he greeted me with a smile as glorious as the sunrise. “Mornin’ sugar.”
I zipped my coat and stepped out. “Okay, ‘sweetheart’ is officially off the banned list, but I draw
the line at ‘sugar’.”
“You’ll change your mind after you’ve had a ranch breakfast,” he said as I got in. “Ready?”
“Should I fasten my seat belt?” I asked, half-jokingly. I was shocked that the UTV still had the seat
belt installed, and it hadn’t been cut out by impatient ranch hands.
Marshall leaned over to kiss me. “I’ll take it slow,” he promised in a sultry voice. “I’m in no hurry
to share you with my brothers.”
“Except it’s still snowing, and I’m cold,” I whispered back.
“Alright, then. Hold on.” He stepped on the gas, and I did, in fact, have to hold on. How had I lived
twenty-six years without riding in one of these things? Marshall swerved into a snowdrift, laughing
like a kid, and when I squealed in delighted alarm, he reached out to grab me. I twisted my arm
around his and locked hands with him. “Don’t slow down for me!” I yelled over the engine. He
floored it, grinning the whole time.
A gust of snow blew into the house behind us, and I felt suddenly bashful. I’d never been in the
ranch house, and it looked exactly like I’d thought it would—a bachelor’s haven, with furniture
arranged for practicality rather than eye appeal. There were still touches everywhere of Marshall’s
mom—pictures all over the walls, a hand-hooked throw rug by the door, and just about every nook
and cranny housed one of her quilts. I looked around, trying to hide my awe. So, this was where
Marshall had grown up.
The air held the homey aromas of a wood stove and fresh bacon. Dusty was flipping pancakes,
Luke was fighting with the coffee machine, and Evan was sitting at the table reading yesterday’s
newspaper. No one was talking, unless you counted the garbled swearing coming from Luke about his
coffee. Everyone looked up at me.
I cringed and waved shyly. “Uh, hi, guys.”
Evan lifted his chin and almost smiled, in what probably amounted to a friendly greeting on his
part. Dusty waved his pancake flipper. “Hi, Kelli! Sleep well?”
Luke had finally gotten his coffee cup half full, and he came over with a flirtatious smirk. “Of
course, she did. We Walker brothers know how to make a woman comfortable.”
I blushed hotly, and Marshall shoved his older brother in the shoulder. “Shut up, Luke, and give
Kelli that chair.”
Luke laughed and moved aside, turning a chair around for me to sit in. Marshall grabbed the one
beside me, growling as he sat. “Don’t mind Luke. He’s not housebroken.”
“I’m cranky is what I am,” Luke piped up. “That fancy coffee pod machine is a piece of junk.
Why’d we have to throw away the old drip pot?”
I was peeking over my shoulder at him. “Do you have any coffee that’s not in a pod?”
Dusty pulled open the kitchen cabinet and grabbed a bag. “Whole beans and a grinder, but Luke’s
right. We got rid of the old pot.”
Marshall leaned close to my ear. “Because Dusty had the idea that if we didn’t brew a huge pot
each morning, Dad wouldn’t drink six cups every day.”
“That must be why he’s become a regular at the Coffee Wagon every day at about eleven.”
Luke roared with laughter, and the pancake flipper clattered on the counter. “I knew it!” Dusty said.
“The old sneak!”
“Speaking of which, where is he?” Marshall asked. “When I left a few minutes ago, he was getting
ready to go outside.”
Evan finally spoke up. “Upstairs. He’ll be down by the time we’re ready to eat.”
“Yeah, and there’s nothing to wash my bacon down with,” Luke whined. He drained the last of his
cup and made a petulant frown. “That’s it. I’m ordering a new coffee machine.”
I twisted around to face him. “I can make a killer brew with just a saucepan. Want me to whip some
up?”
Luke’s face dropped in astonishment, and he blinked, open-mouthed. “You can do that?”
“Sure, it’s easy. Dusty, do you have a pot?”
I went into the kitchen and started rummaging around. Dusty helped me find what I needed, and I
got to work. It probably took longer than necessary because Marshall followed me and insisted on
“helping.” Which meant he took every opportunity to sneak a kiss over my shoulder or wrap an arm
around me. But by the time we all heard the stairs squeaking with Blake’s descent, Dusty was loading
up the table with eggs, bacon, and pancakes, and I was carefully ladling the coffee into mugs for
Marshall to carry to the dining room.
“Well, good morning, Kelli!” Blake’s voice boomed behind me.
I smiled and handed Blake a full coffee cup with a wink. “Good morning, Mr. Walker.”
“Bless you,” he said, raising the cup in a grateful salute. “And lay off that Mr. Walker business.
You’re not a kid anymore.”
“Old habits die hard… sir. Thanks for letting me use your bunkhouse last night.”
“I had nothing to do with it, but it’s a good thing you didn’t try to drive home. I was just listening to
the scanner. Wrecks all over the place, ever since yesterday afternoon. They’re having a devil of a
time getting a snow plow out to the less-traveled county roads.”
I took the last cup for myself and followed Blake back to the table. Marshall held my chair for me.
“Is it bad still?”
“Hard to say when they’ll get everything cleared. Depends on the weather. But we’re pretty well
set up here to hunker down, so let’s say Grace and dig in. Evan?”
I bowed my head with the others as Evan blessed our breakfast. Such a simple thing—why hadn’t
that been a routine in my family? At my house, breakfast was a hurried affair, usually a toaster tart as
we were dashing out the door. But if I knew the Walker family, they’d already been working for an
hour or two before they finally sat down, and they had an honest appetite worth praying over. And
they’d be off again in just a few minutes, because every single one of them ate like their food was
going to disappear. It was utterly foreign to me, and even though I tried to do justice to Dusty’s
cooking, there was no way I could keep up with their appetites. Or their schedule.
Luke and Dusty tied, mopping up the last of their syrup and swilling the dregs of their coffee almost
as one man. “Well, I’ll say one thing,” Luke said as he got up from the table. “I hope we have snow
all day and half of the night.”
“So do I,” Marshall echoed, his hand trailing up my thigh under the table. I nudged him away,
blushing, and he shot me a playful grin.
“Why’s that, Luke?” Evan asked mildly as he, too, carried his plate toward the kitchen.
“Because I’m hoping Kelli will stick around to make me another decent cup of coffee tomorrow.”
“Hear, hear!” Blake grunted, banging his cup on the table as he left. “You’re welcome around here
anytime, young lady.”
Marshall was the last of his family still at the table, and he leaned over to put his arm around me.
“Hear that? You’re a pretty good fit in this family, sugar.”
Chapter 11
M uch to my dismay, the county got our road plowed by about two in the afternoon. There was
even a break in the weather, so if Kelli was going to get herself and her horse home, that was
the time. “You’re sure you don’t want me to drive with you?” I asked again as she got in the truck.
She turned around and looped her arms over my shoulders, pushing my hat back with her nose.
“Then what, cowboy? We get snowed in at my place next?”
“That sounds promising.”
She shook her head, grinning. “Hold your ponies, sir. We’ve only been on speaking terms for
twenty-four hours.”
“Yeah, but we have ten years to make up for. At least give me a kiss to survive on till I see you
again. It might be days before I can get another one.”
She leaned out a little farther, letting me almost catch her, and kissed me until I was so dizzy, I
wasn’t sure I could hold us both. “I’ll call you when I get home,” she promised. And then she kissed
me again. About ten minutes later, I finally closed the door of the pickup and waved as she pulled out.
There was no shortage of work to catch up on. Most of the world slows down on a snow day, but
not the ranch. And I’d been slacking on my chores, trying to make sure Kelli didn’t feel abandoned.
She’d ridden with me to drop hay to cows and de-ice the water troughs, but I couldn’t expect her to
slog through the snowy mud with me on the more demanding jobs. By the time I got to the haystack to
load bales for the horses, Dusty had already beaten me to it.
And he didn’t seem very talkative. “What’s your problem?” I finally asked him.
He walked past me carrying a bale, with two dogs following right at his heels. “I don’t have a
problem.”
“It’s all over your face. Look, I’m sorry I didn’t get over here sooner. I was kinda… busy.”
He shook his head, and his jaw locked. “It’s nothing like that. I got this.”
I watched him for another minute—the tension in his shoulders, the dejected way he was walking
and how he wouldn’t risk looking me in the eye. He wasn’t even baby-talking the dogs like he usually
did.
“Seriously,” I said, “I didn’t think you’d get that upset. Thanks for covering for me, brother, but I’ll
take over now.” I reached for the pitchfork he was using to clean up the loose hay, but he didn’t give it
to me.
“It’s not about work, Marshall!” he snapped. “It’s… just forget it.”
I blocked his path out of the hay shed and stood there with my arms crossed. “Is it Kelli? You’re
upset that she and I are… well, I’m not sure what we are, but we’re something.”
Dusty stabbed the pitchfork into a hay bale. “I don’t care what you do,” he said tiredly. “At least
you’re finally being nice to her. Just let me get back to work, okay?”
“Okay, sorry,” I said, holding up my hands. Dusty walked off to help Emily clean stalls, slamming
the barn door in the dogs’ faces.
Just what had crawled up inside him and died? It wasn’t like him at all. But wondering about my
brother’s sour mood didn’t occupy my thoughts for long. I had much better things to think about.
Kelli called me about the time I headed into the house to get supper ready. It was my turn tonight,
and I thought I’d go out on a limb and try something totally different: roast beef. It’s different—you
cook it in the oven instead of on the grill. I answered the phone and bragged to Kelli about what I was
doing.
“Really? That sounds delicious. Roast beef dinner was never a thing in my family. Did you put it in
a pot with the potatoes and onions, or did you cook it alone?”
“Well, I haven’t started it yet,” I confessed. “Seems like my mom used to put the potatoes with it,
but I thought I’d try something simple.”
Kelli was quiet. Actually, it sounded like the phone was muffled. After a few seconds, I found out
why—she was covering the mouthpiece and laughing. “Sorry,” she said when she came back. “I hate
to break it to you, but I think it’s going to take more than half an hour to cook your roast.”
“You think? It’s just got to stop mooing before we eat it, right?”
“You’re thinking of Prime Rib, another delicacy I know almost nothing about, except that it also
takes hours to get right.”
“Huh. I know fifteen ways to make steak and I’m pretty good with brisket, but I always go to
Beaufort’s for that kind of thing. How about it?”
“How about what?”
“Beaufort’s. You. Me. In public, holding hands.”
“Well, I don’t know,” she purred, and I got my first taste of full body shivers from a phone
conversation. If she could sound that seductive when she wasn’t even there in person… “Seems to me
folks might think one of us is under duress and call the cops to defuse the situation.”
I laughed. “Okay, then, dinner at your place, as soon as the roads get better.”
“Funny you should say that. You ranchers may not have this on your calendars, but Thanksgiving is
in two days.”
I was bending over the chest freezer, pulling out something for dinner, since the roast wasn’t going
to work out. “You don’t say. I guess we’ll do our cookout like usual.”
She gasped in horror. “You guys don’t do turkey dinner with all the trimmings?”
“We used to, before Mom passed. Nobody else knows how to make it. I guess maybe Cody might
get turkey this year, but we’ll just do our normal thing.”
“That’s too bad. So…” She hesitated. “I talked to my parents just a few minutes ago.”
I walked outside to fire up the grill. “Yeah? Did you tell them? About us, I mean?”
“I sort of had to. Dad was all worried about me driving in the snow and he actually came to my
house looking for me. He could have just picked up the phone. Anyway, my mom wants to know if
you’d like to join us for Thanksgiving.”
I almost dropped the phone down into the grill. “Join you? All of you? Like, for a family dinner?”
Kelli sighed. “It’s too soon, I know, but I promised Ma I’d ask. I’ll tell her it’s not going to w—”
“Hold on there. I didn’t say I wouldn’t come.”
“My brother’s coming. And his kids.”
“I like kids just fine.”
“Not these kids.”
“Well, sweetheart, you gonna keep me away from them forever? Your mom asks, I’ll come.”
I heard a sharp intake of breath. “Are you sure?”
“Sure, I’m sure. Just as long as I can hold your hand under the table like I did this morning.”
“That wasn’t my hand, cowboy.”
“Felt good to me.”
She chuckled, then sighed again. “Well, thank you, I think. And thanks for saying you’ll come. That
will mean a lot to my mom, really.”
“Anytime. I guess I’d better go for now, though. These steaks aren’t going to cook themselves.”
“M a, Daddy, do you remember Marshall Walker?” My stomach was twisting as I stood stiffly
in my parent’s living room and made the introductions. Or re-introductions.
Marshall took off his hat and bowed slightly to my mother. “Ma’am.”
“Ooh, so polite! You don’t do that here. I’m not royalty.” Then my tiny mother reached up and
embraced him, dangling from his neck like he was her son or something. And I died just a little.
Marshall’s eyes widened like a deer in headlights, but he smiled and shook his head slightly, then
