put his hand out to my father. “Mr. Mason, sir. Thank you for having me.”
Thank Heaven Dad can greet my boyfriend like a normal person. He shook Marshall’s hand and
made me proud of how friendly yet dignified he was. “We’re glad you could come, Marshall. It’s
been a long time.”
“Too long,” Marshall agreed, with a meaningful smile at me.
“You two go sit down,” Mom insisted, shooing Marshall and my dad toward the couch. “Kelli is
helping me.”
I was? Sure, I hadn’t meant to leave her to do all the work, but I also wasn’t happy with
abandoning my brand-new boyfriend in the living room with my dad. What in the world were they
supposed to talk about? But then Marshall gave me a confident smile and said “Yes, ma’am,” to my
mother, and that was that.
I didn’t have long to suffer in awkward sympathy for the stilted conversation in the living room.
Not five minutes after I tied on my apron, my brother Kevin and his family arrived. And all hell broke
loose.
Here’s the thing. My mom loves me, I know she does, but in our family, I was always Daddy’s pet,
and my brother was my mom’s hero. The sun rose and set on him, and he could do no wrong. So,
guess who got in trouble when anything happened? Spoiler: not Kevin. That lavish favor had passed
on to Kevin’s hellion children, Shepard and Brady, both of whom would probably be on wanted
posters someday.
Okay, they were only two and four, so it wasn’t like anyone could expect perfect behavior. But
maybe it would be awesome if Shepard, the oldest, didn’t make it a tradition to pull his pants down
and water the potted plants. In the living room.
I got along okay with Jenny, Kevin’s wife, but we’d never gotten to know each other well. She had
met my brother in college, and now they lived about four hours away, so our exposure had been
limited to family holidays and so on. She was always a little breathless and harried, and I didn’t
wonder why, but it didn’t make it easy to sit down and just talk to her.
My parents’ little house practically exploded with people and chatter, family coming in and moving
around, and the constant battle of trying to keep Shepard from pulling boiling saucepans off the stove.
Meanwhile, Brady found my mom’s candy bowl, and he was systematically demolishing it and
spitting the half-chewed pieces on the carpet. I’m telling you…
But Marshall practically rose to deity status in my eyes. He struck up a conversation with Kevin
about my brother’s one and only great love—football—and kept things open and easy. And when
Shepard dashed by hot on the heels of my parents’ cat, Marshall never broke eye contact with my
brother. He just snatched the kid out of the air, play-wrestled with him for a few seconds, and turned
him loose after the cat had made it to safety. I don’t think Kevin even noticed what was going on, but I
did. The man was a walking miracle.
I was watching all this from the dining room, and it was probably a good thing no one was looking
my way, because I was rapidly turning to mush. Could that cowboy get any yummier? Oh, yes, yes, he
could, because right about then, he glanced around and gave me a reassuring wink.
My mom and I had almost everything to the table before Jenny joined us, but she came in to “help”
anyway. Truth be told, she slowed us down because she had a toddler determinedly clinging to her
legs, and more than once I was carrying a hot serving dish and had to dodge a careening four-year-
old. But eventually, we were ready to eat.
Finally, we could sit down and breathe. I’d sneak my hand under the table to my cowboy, and we’d
have a nice, pleasant Thanksgiving dinner with my family. It was all going to go beautifully.
Oh, how wrong I was.
“S ay something.”
Marshall’s cheek flinched, and he spared me a quick glance, then turned his attention back to
the road. “What do I need to say? We survived.”
I buried my face in my hands. “I’m sorry. I had no idea my mom was going to do that.”
“Why not? What mother doesn’t have big dreams for her daughter?”
I groaned at the sarcasm in his voice. “Look, I’m sorry she started acting like we were planning a
wedding. I don’t know where she got that idea, or the notion that we had picked a Caribbean cruise
for a honeymoon. She just got… excited.”
“Oh, it wasn’t that part that spooked the bejeezus out of me. It was when she started talking about
where our kids would go to college that got me a little worried.”
“I told her we weren’t anywhere close to being… well, you know. It’s just…” I broke off with a
sigh. “She’s like that because when she was growing up, she didn’t have the luxury of long courtships,
and she doesn’t seem to understand why we aren’t ready to rush to the altar.”
Marshall gave me a thin smile. “I get it, but boy, howdy. She had me sweating bullets for a while.”
“Are you sure it was because of her? I thought it was when Shepard dumped the hot gravy boat in
your lap.”
He grunted and glanced down at himself. There were still crusty brown smears all over the front of
his jeans, despite his best efforts at cleaning up. “I thought more than my dignity was in jeopardy there
for a bit, if you know what I mean.”
I laughed brokenly, then stroked his shoulder in sympathy. “I’m so sorry it was such a disaster! I’m
glad you’re okay.”
“Who said I’m okay? I probably have second-degree burns.”
My eyes bugged out in alarm. “What? You didn’t say you were hurt! Do you need to go to the
hospital?”
He chuckled and cracked a grin. “Naw, I’ll live. Sorry to say it, but I was actually grateful for the
excuse not to stay for the football game after dinner.”
I winced. “My brother is intense.”
“Kevin? He’s fine. It’s just there’s only so much I can say about football, then it would be nice to
talk about something else. Like, maybe parenting classes.”
“Oh, gosh…” I sank my forehead into my palms again. “Jenny’s into permissive parenting.”
“I hope she never gets a dog,” Marshall snorted. “But hey, it was fun. I’m glad I came.”
“Are you? You didn’t seem to eat much.”
“Sure, I did. I ate turkey and potatoes and pie. Not sure what was in that green bowl.”
“That was pancit. It’s just a noodle stir-fry.”
His brows crumpled together. “Oh. What were those taquito-looking things?”
“Lumpia. You’d probably like those if you gave them a chance. I noticed you didn’t try the Kare-
Kare, either.”
He shuddered, and looked faintly green. “So, what, was I supposed to sample everything?”
“That’s usually considered polite, but if you don’t want it, you can just pass instead of pretending
you have to go to the bathroom.” I gave him a hard look.
“I was trying not to gag, sweetheart.”
Something lurched inside me. That was the first time he’d called me that with his old tone of voice,
and I didn’t like it one bit. “You didn’t have to be rude,” I said, crossing my arms. “My mom worked
really hard trying to make a nice meal. Family dinners are important to her, and we don’t have just
steak and potatoes all the time.”
He raised a brow and looked suspiciously at me. “Gee, sorry. I didn’t mean to get you all riled up.”
I sighed and looked out the passenger window. “Just forget it.”
We were both quiet for a few minutes. “Look,” Marshall finally said, “what’s the big deal? So,
Thanksgiving wasn’t perfect. It wasn’t totally ruined, was it?”
I didn’t look at him, but I smiled a little. “Not entirely. There was still turkey.”
“Darn straight. And pie, don’t forget the pie. Your mom makes a good pie.”
“And the house didn’t burn down,” I suggested. “That’s always a plus.”
“See? Not the worst day by any means. How about we cap it off by dropping in on Cody and
Morgan before I take you home? Cody said they’d be around if we had time.”
I kneaded my forehead. Hanging out with Morgan and Cody sounded terrific. But I’d been on the
brink of a nervous breakdown all afternoon, and now my head was splitting. Besides, going to the
home of a couple of newlyweds like a couple ourselves meant… well, I didn’t know what it meant.
Probably less than bringing Marshall to Thanksgiving with my whole family, but I just wasn’t sure if I
was up for more of the same. I liked it when I had him to myself. I wasn’t sure about sharing him in
public yet.
“I’m kind of tired,” I said at last.
Marshall’s fingers flexed on the wheel, and he glanced uncertainly at me. “Oh.”
“Can we do that on the weekend, maybe?”
I saw his chest rise and fall, and he seemed to relax a little. “Yeah. Sure.”
We pulled into my driveway a few minutes later, and I instantly felt a little peace claim me. My
own space, and my own quiet. That was how I wanted to wrap up the day with Marshall. Just the two
of us, sitting on the couch like we had at the bunkhouse. I’d make us some frilly drinks, and we’d take
turns kissing each other and laughing about the stupid family things that somehow turn into such a big
deal.
Except when I asked if he wanted to come in, he just looked down and fumbled with his keys. “Not
now.”
“Oh.” My heart crashed. I couldn’t believe he would turn down a little “snogging,” to use Jess’s
word. “Okay, whatever.”
He shrugged and gestured to his jeans. “I kind of need to go home and clean up. Plus, chores to do.
The usual.”
“Right.” I fought down a tremor of disappointment. I’d really wanted to just cuddle with my
cowboy. My consolation after an afternoon of anxiety. “Well, I guess this is good night, then.”
He smiled and brushed my cheek, then pulled me close in the kind of kiss that could melt the snow
off the mountains. I wouldn’t have minded more of that, but he stepped back, tipped his hat to me, and
went home. I was alone, but at least I had one delicious memory after a day of stressful feasting.
Chapter 12
“H ow was dinner yesterday?” Cody tossed me a pair of fencing pliers and then pulled out a
clasp to wrap around the post. He hooked it in place and nodded for me to tighten it down
with the pliers.
“Dinner was fine,” I grunted as I twisted the clasp. “You?”
Cody grinned, like only a besotted newlywed could. “We didn’t do a huge deal. We got a small
turkey and tried deep frying it. It was good, but the company made it better.”
“You had people over?”
“No. Morgan’s mom showed up, but thankfully, she didn’t stay long. I was talking about after
dinner. Dessert.” He winked.
I rolled my eyes and tightened another fence clasp, then waited for him to pass the next one through
the fence to me. We were out at their new place, trying to take advantage of the clear weather today to
get a line of their perimeter fence up. We’d gotten most of the posts anchored, and now it was going
pretty fast.
“So, it went okay with Kelli’s family?” Cody asked.
“How’s it supposed to go? Pistols at ten paces?”
He laughed. “Thanksgiving with the in-laws? It can.”
“They’re not my in-laws.” I pinched another clasp in place, then looked up at him. “Do you
remember Kevin very well?”
“Not really. He went off to college right after school, didn’t he? How’s he doing?”
“Fine, but Kelli and I agreed that if he should ever ask us to babysit, the answer is no.”
Cody gave me a funny look.
“What?”
“Nothing.” He walked back to the side by side for another bag of fence clamps. “You guys got
serious even faster than Morgan and I did.”
“We’ve known each other a lot longer.”
“Yeah, and you always told me you guys had nothing in common.”
“Well,” I pushed up the brim of my hat and wiggled my eyebrows. “We found at least one thing we
have in common.”
Cody narrowed his eyes and pointed a gloved finger at me. “Don’t you hurt her, Marshall.”
“Why would you think I’d do that?”
“You wouldn’t do it on purpose, but I can see you charging ahead like a bull and not thinking about
the consequences.”
I reached through the fence and grabbed the entire bag of clamps out of his hand. I’d hook them on
for myself if he was going to talk to me like that. “Just so you know,” I grumbled, “I have thought
things through. With my brain.”
“And?”
I shrugged. “We’re going to see how things go.”
“How far?”
I stopped working. “What’s that supposed to mean? I’m not taking advantage of her if that’s what
you’re thinking.”
“No, but…”
“But what?” I asked testily.
He shook his head. “Look, nothing would make Morgan and me happier than to see you two happy
together, but just… take it easy. Kelli’s… she’s a lot of woman.”
I cocked an eye at him. “You saying she’s too much for me?”
“I’m saying she’s as stubborn as you are, and you’re both a little proud of it.”
I frowned. “Well, you got me there.”
“You know where I screwed up with Courtney?”
“Marrying her in the first place?”
“Besides the obvious.”
I shook my head. “You weren’t the one who screwed up.”
“I could have made things better. I thought it was supposed to be fifty-fifty. I’m finding out it’s a
hundred-hundred type of deal. There’s no room for pride.”
I crossed my arms over the field fence we’d just put up and stared over the mountains for a minute.
“It’s not like I haven’t thought about this. I guess to answer your question, I’d like to try to do life with
her. Maybe we don’t fit, but I don’t want to live the rest of my life wishing we’d given it a shot.”
“I guess that’s enough for now.” Cody fetched another bag of clamps. “Think we can get the rest of
this row done before it starts snowing again?”
I looked up at the sky. “If we stop talking about my love life.”
“Deal.”
“Y ou know what normal people do on Black Friday?”
Morgan’s head popped up over the back of the buckskin horse we were grooming. “Throw
elbows and max out their credit cards trying to buy useless crap that will end up being tossed out
before the next year?”
“After that.” I stroked the horse’s long forelock, and he nudged my hand for more petting when I
dropped it.
“I suppose they over-eat on Thanksgiving leftovers.” Morgan put her brush back in the bucket,
blowing out an exhausted sigh. “There. Biz is all clean after his roll in the mud. Just two horses to
go.”
“I mean, they decorate for Christmas, or they make hot cider and spend time with their families.”
Morgan unclipped Biz’s tie rope and started walking him to his stall. “That’s not true,” she called
over her shoulder. “For lots of people, it’s a workday, maybe the busiest of the year.”
“The Coffee Wagon was almost dead. Because in this town, everyone who didn’t stay home went
shopping in the big city.”
Morgan slid Biz’s stall shut, but not before he forced her to scratch under his chin one more time.
The big ham. “It’s just business as usual around here, except we have no clients today. Or
volunteers,” she added ruefully. “Thanks for helping me get everyone cleaned up.”
“I like helping out,” I said, but I was watching Morgan as she brought the next horse out. This was
Duke, who, like Biz, had made the best of his turnout time this morning by coating himself head to tail
in icy muck. But the mud wasn’t what I was noticing.
What caught my eye and made me stew in thought was that Duke seemed to really love Morgan. His
ears perked up when she was around and he watched her every movement like a dog worshiping its
master. Pretty much every horse here whinnied when she walked into the barn. Biz had a whole
performance he would do when she came in, but even the quieter ones adored her in their own way.
So why didn’t my horse seem to care if I was even alive? Was it me? It must be. What was I doing
wrong with her?
I started rinsing Duke’s legs off with a sponge and a warm bucket, and Morgan brushed down his
face and neck. “You haven’t said much about Thanksgiving dinner,” she said after a few minutes. “Did
it go well?”
“Sure.”
She ducked under Duke’s neck to look at me and arched a brow. “What does ‘sure’ mean?”
I shrugged. “It means yes, it was nice, but I guess maybe I was expecting more than nice. What
about you?”
“Oh… Cody and I have decided to try to make peace with my mom. She can’t come over whenever
she wants, but we offered to have her for Thanksgiving. She never said yes or no, but she showed up
just in time to eat. And left without helping us clean up, but I was ready for her to go by then anyway.”
“Did she try to make you and Cody fight again?”
“No, she’s decided that he’s her favorite person in the world, and she would love to set him up
with her connections in California for a big-time training operation. Or Texas. I can’t keep it straight
because the story kept changing.”
I rolled my eyes. “She sounds like a new drama every minute.”
Morgan nodded as she got out the sweat scraper to clean the excess water off Duke’s belly. “Pretty
much. It must be nice to have a normal family.”
From anyone else, a comment like that would make me feel guilty. But from Morgan, I knew it was
just honest praise. She loved my parents. “Yeah,” I agreed. “It is.”
“So? How did Marshall get along with your dad? You’ve told me nothing at all.”
“What am I supposed to tell you? They talked about sports and land prices and cattle, and then we
ate dinner and nobody said much of anything until my horrible nephew spilled the gravy all over
Marshall. Then he drove me home.”
Morgan was giving me a funny look, but she just shook her head and went back to grooming Duke.
“What, what’s that face?” I asked.
“Nothing. I just expected more drama. I mean, it is you and Marshall, after all. Where are the
fireworks?”
I grabbed a brush to start working on Duke’s mane. “Well, he wasn’t too crazy about my mom’s
cooking. Does that count as drama?”
“Did it bother you?”
I shrugged. I hated to admit it, but yes, it bothered me, because once again, it meant we came from
different worlds. Is there a more basic representation of your home life than your family’s customs
and the foods you grew up eating? But to Morgan, I just said, “No.”
“No problem, then,” she decided as she stepped back from the horse. “There, I think we got the
worst of it, Duke. Can you behave yourself now?”
He reached his nose out to her for a friendly nuzzle, and she patted his neck. “Two down, one to
go.”
I waited as Morgan swapped out a clean horse for a dirty one, and again, I couldn’t stop comparing
her easy relationship with her horses to my trouble with Joy. It would be one thing, I decided, if Joy
seemed to be afraid of me. But she wasn’t. She was just… indifferent. Like Marshall seemed to be,
all those years.
Well, I’d been wrong about him. Maybe I was wrong about Joy, too.
“You know what we should do?” Morgan said as she brought Badger to the tie rack.
“I’m dying to find out.”
“It’s supposed to warm up overnight and be nice tomorrow. We should load up a trailer and ride
out on the new property. You, me, and the guys. Doesn’t that sound like fun?”
A glowing tingle flooded my body. That sounded like the kind of fun I’d always longed to be a part
of. “Yes,” I replied emphatically. “I’m all over that. Do you think Marshall and Cody will be?”
Morgan grinned and pointed her brush toward the driveway, where the gate was swinging open for
Marshall’s truck. “Let’s ask them.”
“Are you crazy? Taking that horse on a trail ride? That’s a terrible idea.”
Kelli stared at me, and I could see her lower jaw fixing in that stubborn look of hers. “Why?”
“Do I even have to say it? You can’t take that mare out in the mountains. She scares me to death in
the arena. How do you not see that?”
Kelli crossed her arms and glared at my dash. I was driving her home after we’d spent most of the
day helping Cody and Morgan, and we’d had a great time together. Except for every time they started
talking about going riding tomorrow. I’d agreed because, hey, why not? An afternoon with my woman
and our best friends, who conveniently wanted to see us together? Except Kelli was determined to
ride her own horse, no matter how many times I told her that would be the height of rank stupidity.
“We’re just planning on walking up the trail,” Kelli huffed. “I’m not roping any calves or popping
in and out of the brush.”
“Yeah, but can you control everything? Come on, you were doing great with her the other day, but
there’s no need to push it. Morgan has horses, I have horses. We can find you something—”
“Gentle?” she interrupted, her voice dripping with sarcasm.
“Just what’s so wrong with gentle? Nobody with any sense wants a horse that isn’t, because like it
or not, sweetheart, they’re bigger and stronger than you are.”
Her teeth flashed, her shoulders drew up, and I knew I’d said too much. “Don’t call me
sweetheart.”
“What? Now this is personal?”
“It is when you belittle me. When you talk to me in that condescending tone, it’s personal.”
I rolled my eyes. In retrospect, that was a bad idea. Her face darkened, her eyes lit with fire, and
her lip curled dangerously. “You can’t even have a fair argument without mocking me.”
“Oh, come on. I wasn’t mocking you, and… oh, just forget it. You’re not listening to me, anyway.”
She wasn’t. Her arms were crossed, her chin high, and her eyes locked on the road. I risked a
couple of glances at her, but mostly, I just drove. It was safer than trying to talk.
We were almost to her driveway before I heard “the sigh.” Every guy knows what I mean. It’s
preceded by a trembling intake of breath, and then sharply released. Usually, when she’s looking
away. It means she’s too furious to talk and too proud to cry, so she’s just holding it together, trying
not to explode. I didn’t even think she realized what a brutal weapon that sigh was in the game of love
and war, but it was more than I could handle.
“All right,” I grumbled as we turned in her driveway. “Why are you so set on taking your own
horse?”
She didn’t look at me right away, but something in her face softened and she started blinking again.
“I don’t think someone like you could understand.”
“Someone like me?” I asked incredulously. “Just what the heck is that supposed to mean, that I’m
too ignorant?”
“No.” She finally turned to me, and for the first time, I saw her lip trembling. “Don’t turn it into an
insult, because it’s actually a compliment. This—all of this—has always been so easy for you. It’s
part of you.”
Her softened tone took the edge off my anger, and I could breathe a little. “What’s your point?”
“It’s just that…” She started gesturing with her hands, but then clasped them in her lap and started
worrying her lower lip and blinking faster. And that, right there—that broken look and the hesitation
showed me a side to Kelli Mason that I’d never known could exist. She looked… vulnerable.
“Hey.” I rested my hand on top of hers and waited for her to look me in the eye. Slowly, she did.
“Is it that? You feel like I think you can’t hack it?”
Her expression didn’t change much, but her shoulders crept upward in a shrug of acknowledgment.
“You do sort of act that way sometimes.”
“I act like a guy who finally got to hold and kiss the girl of his dreams and doesn’t want to see her
get hurt.”
The corners of her mouth twitched, but she didn’t quite let herself smile. “The girl of your dreams,
huh? Must have been some boring dreams you had.”
“Oh, Darlin’, you have no idea. Ever heard of a man having to be locked in his bedroom because
he’s sleep-dancing and trying to kiss his pillow?”
She snorted. “No.”
“Neither have I, because I just made it up. The sleep-dancing part. I plead the fifth to the pillow
kissing.”
She shook her head, but she was smiling at last, so I dragged one of her hands to my mouth and
pressed my luck. “I don’t mean to sound like I doubt your abilities. I’ve seen you stick to a march hare
like you had velcro on your butt. I know you can handle a horse. I just don’t know if you and your
horse are ready to go out together. And you don’t need to yet, either. There’s no schedule. Why not
give it a little more time in the arena? Get to know her better?”
Her gaze fell, and I could see the gears of her brain spinning and grinding. “It’s not that what you
say doesn’t make sense. But why are you so sure she’s not safe? Didn’t she spend a few years as a
ranch horse, in the fields and mountains every day?”
“Yes,” I admitted reluctantly. “But Evan sold her because the guys couldn’t count on her.”
“They were working. Probably distracted by the cows, driving through the brush, you name it. I’m
talking about walking her on a trail with other horses that are walking. No ropes, no cows, just us.”
I shook my head. “I don’t know. Why are you so determined to do this?”
For the first time in our whole conversation, her fingers came to life in my hand, and a glow lit her
face. “Because I’ve wanted to do this for so long. Not have someone do it for me, not ride someone
else’s pet horse. I need to know I can do this. Can you understand that?”
I sighed. Drummed my free hand on the steering wheel and stared at her house. She was crazy.
Gloriously, deliciously crazy, like Luke when he used to ride bulls, or my logger friends who tackled
widowmaker trees and laughed when they survived by mere inches.
Like her horse.
And the truth was, I wouldn’t trade Kelli’s kind of crazy for all the sweet, tame girls in the whole
state. Because deep down, I worshiped her—always had. She had a hunger and a drive I’d never
known, and it was intoxicating. If I quenched that, I’d crush everything I loved about her.
I ran my hand through my hair, sighed, and looked at her. “Fine. If it’s that important to you.
Promise me you won’t take risks?”
She tilted her chin and gave me an arch look, but she was smiling. “I don’t have to promise you
anything, cowboy.”
I grinned and flicked my eyes to the house. “Then I’ll work on you the old-fashioned way. You
gonna invite me in?”
Kelli leaned forward and kissed my nose. “Funny you should ask,” she whispered. “That was how
I was planning to break you down.”
We didn’t even make it out of my truck that evening. But we did prove that we can fight like cats
and dogs… and make up like a house on fire.
M y new saddle came early the following morning. I may or may not have squealed like a
little kid at Christmas and offered the UPS guy a handful of free coffee cards for making me
the first stop on his route that day. I didn’t even bother fetching a knife to open the box—I just ripped
it open in the living room and sighed at the glory of it.
It smelled amazing, and it looked even better. Oak-toned leather with a rough-out barrel racing seat
and some bling here and there to make it stand out. I’d even ordered an extra wide tree for my big,
gorgeous mare, and a new cinch and bridle to go with it. I knew that Marshall’s tack was all
handmade by a master craftsman from Wyoming, but I didn’t need—or want to wait for—a custom
saddle. This one was me all over. I was really into this thing, hat over spurs, and I couldn’t wait to
take flight.
Marshall met me at Jess’s barn with the horse trailer, and he didn’t say a word when I brought Joy
out. He just kissed me, waved at Jess’s dad, who was on the tractor, and opened the trailer door for
me. That was a relief. I didn’t want to fight when I was in such a good mood.
I led Joy into the trailer and tied her beside Marshall’s horse, Bay Rum. She made him look…
well, like a scruffy ranch horse. I had braided her mane and spent maybe an hour grooming and
pampering her before he got there, and she smelled like flowers and coconut oil. Even with a full
winter coat, she was dazzling, and I couldn’t have been prouder.
We got to Morgan and Cody’s property first, so rather than getting out right away, Marshall kept the
engine running and gave me “the look.” There was no way I could resist that kind of invitation, not
when he was wearing his favorite flannel and my favorite denim fleece coat. I had to bury my face in
his shirt collar and see if he smelled as good as he looked.
He did.
“Hey,” he said after a few minutes. “I wanted to apologize for yesterday.”
I rested my cheek on his shoulder and let him drop his arm down behind me. “Me too.”
He kissed my hair. Did he know the full-body shivers I got when he did that? “Let’s just have fun
today, alright?”
“Safely, yes.”
He drew back to smile at me a little. “Sweetheart, if there’s one thing I’ve learned, being around
you is never ‘safe.’”
I laughed and punched him playfully in the chest. “I’m just trying to keep up with you.”
“Oh, now you’re in for it,” he growled—the best kind of growl. He pushed me over on the seat,
and I secretly thanked whoever it was who had decided that some trucks needed bench seats instead
of center consoles. I had my arms around him and he couldn’t have sat back up if he’d wanted to.
Kissing Marshall is unlike being with any other guy. Okay, it’s not like I have many samples to
compare him to. Three, actually, and none of them made me go weak in the knees. But with Marshall,
it’s way deeper than just a kiss. He tugs at something far down in my soul and makes me hot and cold
all over. It’s almost like a battle of wills, where we both just have to keep pushing, taking, and
begging for more. Except in this kind of battle, there are no losers.
It was sweet oblivion, holding him close in that warm pickup. It was how our first kiss should
have gone—just us, close and complete, with no ego or misunderstanding between us. I twisted my
fingers through his hair and giggled when he left off kissing my mouth and started down my throat.
And then I jumped so hard I rammed my nose into his forehead when someone knocked on the
window.
“Ow…” I was holding my nose, hoping it wouldn’t start bleeding.
“Are you okay?” Marshall was tipping my head back, apparently looking for the same thing. “What
happened?”
“That’s what happened,” I said, pointing to the driver’s side window. Cody was looking in and
waving at us, grinning like a moron. “Didn’t you hear him?”
“Nope. I was listening to something way better.” Marshall leaned close to my ear and whispered,
“You make interesting sounds when I kiss your neck.”
“Are you two done yet?” Cody called from outside. “Or should we just saddle up without you?”
“Come on, cowboy,” I laughed. “Let’s ride.”
Chapter 13
“L ooking like progress,” I said to Cody as our four horses started walking up the hill together. We
hadn’t been on this part of the property yesterday, and now I could see the new strip of gravel
that was starting up from the main road. It stretched maybe seven or eight hundred yards, but it had
over a mile to go to reach the house's building site. “You almost have a driveway.”
Cody chuckled and shook his head ruefully. “Yeah, and we ‘almost’ have electricity, too. Maybe in
about ten years we’ll get the house’s foundation poured. I swear, I don’t see how we’ll ever get it all
done.”
“It’ll be worth it, hon,” Morgan said. “Every time I feel overwhelmed, I remember the view from
up there, and I get all excited again.”
“Must be quite the view,” Kelli agreed.
I didn’t miss the wicked looks Cody and Morgan exchanged. “Oh, it is,” Cody grinned, winking at
his wife. “Especially that one flat rock, right, baby?”
“Okay, stop.” Kelli waved. “I don’t need any more visuals. Is that the house site up there?”
Morgan pointed. “Yes, that clearing on the shorter slope. Actually, I was hoping to explore a little,
if it’s not too icy up there. There’s a stream I want to check out to see how much of it freezes in the
winter. So, the fields to the south there will be for cattle,” she said, gesturing, “and to the west of the
new barn we plan to put in some divided paddocks for rescue horses.”
“I like it,” Kelli said. “The barn’s looking good. That’s a perfect spot for it.”
“Thanks. Easy access from the road, and nice and open for parking and all the surrounding corrals.
We’ll have the program running out of here long before we get the house built. What do you think?”
Kelli nodded wordlessly, and I took a second to watch her. She was positively starry-eyed, just
gazing around at the fields and hills. She wasn’t a tall woman—in fact, she was pretty tiny, but she
looked right at home on top of that big mare of hers, out here in the middle of a cowboy’s paradise.
Apart from certain memorable encounters, all quite recent, I don’t think I remember seeing her more
relaxed and happy.
She happened to turn slightly, and our eyes met. “It’s perfect,” she sighed. “It’s all perfect.”
I couldn’t argue. Even her horse, the thing I worried about the most today, seemed to be on her best
behavior. I don’t know why I was surprised, because Kelli was right. Evan’s guys had probably
ridden her all over the back country, doctoring and moving the herds, so a quiet little walk like this
was a piece of cake. But to me, it was just the proof I needed that things really could work out okay—
with the horse, and with us.
“Oh, you’ve got to be kidding me.” Cody stopped his horse and stood up in the stirrups, shading his
eyes with his hand. Then he swore and pointed to the northern border of the property. “The deer have
already ripped down the fence we put up yesterday!”
“What?” I walked my horse up beside him. “The whole length?”
“I can’t tell from here. Blast it! We’re going to have to put a seven-foot fence in over there,
Morgan.”
I heard her groan. “Is the fencing at least salvageable for somewhere else? We already spent a lot
on it.”
“Probably, if we pull it back down before the deer finish demolishing it.” He glanced over at me.
“Marshall, do you mind a quick detour? I need to go check out the damage, but I think Morgan would
like to show Kelli the house site.”
I didn’t like that plan at all. If I was nearby, I could at least be sure that Kelli was safe. Maybe I
couldn’t stop my worst fears from happening, but I felt like I could do something.
But if I kept treating her like she needed me to protect her, she’d eventually pitch me to the curb.
Besides, Morgan was a pretty good hand, and they were just walking up to the building site. It would
be better for Kelli to go with her than to follow me down with Cody, where there were probably still
deer ready to pop out of the trees and spook her horse.
As if I needed any more assurance, Kelli spoke up. “Go on with him. We’ll meet you at the top.”
I eyed Kelli carefully. If anything happened to her… if that horse so much as stepped on a twig
wrong and messed up her hair… “You’re fine?” I asked, as if there was any hope of her saying
otherwise.
“Yep. See you in a little bit!” Those thick dark lashes winked and she blew me a kiss with the kind
of smile that warmed my frozen toes.
“Well… Okay, then.” I nudged my horse and followed Cody, but I was watching her over my
shoulder until she was out of sight.
“S o, this is it, huh?” I drank in a deep breath and closed my eyes. “It’s so peaceful up here.”
“Yeah,” Morgan agreed. “I still can’t believe it’s ours. And I can’t believe Blake just gave
this to us.”
“It’s pretty incredible. The view alone is to die for.”
Joy stomped a foot, probably impatient to be doing something rather than just standing there, but I
wasn’t ready to go anywhere yet. I stroked her neck and kept drinking in everything I could see. The
trees were all fluffy with snow; the landscape muffled and still. It felt like a sacred mountain up there,
and I could have stayed forever. The whole idea of starting out fresh in a place like this, building your
dreams with the one you love… well, it made me think of Marshall. What was his plan for the future?
Surely, he wouldn’t live in the ranch house with his dad and brothers forever. I mean, you know, if
things happened.
As if she could hear my thoughts aloud, Morgan asked, “So, how did Marshall handle you riding
your own horse? He didn’t seem very happy about that idea yesterday.”
“Oh, you know Marshall,” I replied slowly, with a mischievous smile. “He’s just the typical
pompous, know-it-all, unfairly handsome, over-protective rancher. Who just happens to be sinfully
good at kissing.”
Morgan chuckled. “So that’s how you talked him into it.”
“Not really, but I guess it helps that I know his weaknesses. He promised not to complain about my
horse, and I didn’t really promise anything at all.”
“The truth comes out. Joy seems to be doing fine, though.”
“Of course, she is.” I glanced down at her. As always, my horse was alert and eager, with her ears
pricked forward and nostrils quivering with each breath. Morgan had ridden Biz—I guess he would
always be her favorite, just like Joy was the horse of my heart. He was resting a hind foot and his
head was hanging like he was asleep where he stood. Even his lower lip drooped and twitched like
he was dreaming. Joy, on the other hand, felt alive and giddy beneath me, like she was ready to sweep
down the mountain racing the eagles if I would only loosen the reins.
I was half inclined to let her.
“Hey, let’s go check out that stream,” Morgan said. “The guys might take the shortcut up the back
side of the slope anyway, so we could meet them.”
Joy and I followed Morgan down into the gorge and it looked like something out of a movie.
Majestic rocks crested the cliffs high above, and giant snowy boulders lined the crack in the world
that opened for the river. Biz broke the snowpack in front of us, taking each step carefully and so very
slowly. It was all I could do to keep Joy from charging over the top of him. I was gritting my teeth,
fighting her in deep snow over the icy ground, and I prayed we wouldn’t surge off the path and
stumble over some hidden hazard. I had to admit that sometimes a quiet, boring horse made sense.
We had gone about a hundred yards when Morgan stopped. The creek was just ahead of us, mostly
iced over on the edges, but still flowing fast in the middle. A few glazed chunks floated here and there
from where they’d broken off at the bank, but the creek was in no danger of freezing solid. “Well, that
answers that,” Morgan said, her breath a puff of fog. “If we lose power to the well in the winter,
we’ll probably still have water in a pinch.”
“Unless it gets a lot colder,” I agreed. “Can we cross it and meet the guys on the other side?”
Morgan looked up and down stream. “It’s pretty rocky here and I don’t know what’s under the ice.
There’s a deer trail along the back of the ridge, though, and it comes out at a wide crossing down
below. This way.”
She turned Biz carefully, giving him time to pick his way through the snow. I followed, trying to
keep Joy from bumping impatiently into Biz’s hind end as we turned. We eased our way around the
snow-covered rocks, through the trees, just letting the horses find their way. The only sounds I could
hear were their muffled footfalls and the squeaking of my new saddle. My toes were getting colder by
the minute, but somehow, it didn’t really matter. I was where I belonged, doing what I’d always
wanted to do.
Morgan’s horse was going slower and slower, though. “I don’t see the trail anymore,” Morgan
confessed after a few minutes.
“Can’t Biz find it?”
“Maybe. It’s not much of a trail, even when the ground is clear. We might need to go back up the
mountain. Wait… there it is.” I saw her hat tilt downward as she peered around her horse’s head. She
lifted her reins to guide him to a narrow path between the overhanging cliff and a drop-off to the
creek below. And then Biz’s front feet slid in the ice-covered mud.
“Whoa, whoa!” she called, as if the horse had any control of it. His hind feet were scrambling, and
his rear end was slipping sideways. Biz, blind on his right side, tried to turn left to recover his
footing, but only slid further downhill.
“Morgan, get off!” I looked from side to side, trying to figure out how I could help. There was
nowhere wide enough to turn around, and Joy fought me when I tried to back her up. She jerked her
head up and down, then tried pawing the air like she wanted to jump forward. I couldn’t have that, so
I turned her loose and, wonder of wonders, she stood still for a second.
Morgan had her hands full, trying to topple off on the uphill side of her horse while he was still
fumbling for what he could see on the downhill side. There wasn’t a darn thing I could do to help her
but sit still and watch. “Whoa, Biz!” she called again, and finally, he froze just long enough for her to
swing her leg over the saddle. But when she hit the snow, he lurched again and her boots went out
from under her. Still clinging to her reins, she rolled down the trail and splashed into the creek.
“Are you okay?” I cried, grabbing my horn to dismount. Where I was going to stand, I didn’t know.
I’d probably be in a worse predicament than she was in half a second.
“I’m fine,” she grumbled as she reached for a nearby rock to pull herself up. “Just… cold.” She got
up to her horse and looked down at herself. It was a good thing she was wearing the thick leather
chinks Cody had bought her in Fort Worth, because she was soaked in freezing muck from the waist
down. Biz had managed to get himself pointed back uphill, though, and she’d be able to lead him to a
safe spot to mount again.
That left me.
“Can she turn around?” Morgan asked through chattering teeth.
“Uh… I don’t know. Maybe I need to get off, too.” I looked around, but there wasn’t a square inch
of flat ground anywhere near us. I just knew that if I dismounted, with my short little legs, I’d wind up
underneath my horse and we’d still be stuck. Joy would have to flip around in mid-air, and somehow,
I’d have to stay with her. “Maybe if I…”
I caught my breath and cautiously flicked my hand to the right. And that was when my mare
performed a miracle. You can’t tell me it was anything else, because she saved my backside that day.
Probably literally. Her ear turned to me, and the next thing I knew, she lifted up on her hind feet,
twisted a perfectly controlled one-eighty with her front feet never touching the earth, and then calmly
walked off the way we had come.
My heart was still pounding fifteen minutes later when we crested the rise and saw the guys coming
up to look for us. But my horse and I had passed our first actual test, and I couldn’t wait to tell my
cowboy all about it.
“I love this horse!” Kelli crowed when I got close enough to hear her.
A lead weight shifted off my chest. She was still in the saddle and it didn’t look like she’d been
tossed in the snow, which was more than I could say for Morgan. “What happened?” Cody demanded,
trotting up to his wife. “Are you okay?”
“I’m fine,” Morgan assured him. “The trail got too icy and he couldn’t get turned around on the
slope, so I got off and slipped into the stream.”
“But Joy was a champ!” Kelli added. “Right, Morgan?”
“She took good care of you,” Morgan agreed. “As sure-footed as they come.”
I was cocking an eyebrow and still wanting to check Kelli for hidden bumps or bruises. “You’re
serious?”
“See, I told you he wouldn’t believe me, Morgan. Look, I don’t even have a drop of ice on my new
saddle. My toes are cold, though, and Morgan’s turning into a popsicle. What say we race back to the
trailer?”
I shook my head. “First rule of riding in the hills. Never run home, or you’ll make your horse barn
sour and they’ll want to run every time.”
She gave me the sweetest pout I’d ever longed to kiss, but her eyes glittered with mischief. “I’ll
have to find some other way to get warm, I suppose.”
“Now you’re talking, sweetheart.” I rode Bay Rum up beside her and let myself feast on the sight.
Her cheeks were bright under her hat, her lips full and pink from the cold, and she was wearing a
smile as wide and white as that snowy mountain. Happy, confident, and just a touch wild—that was
my woman. Maybe this kooky horse had been just the thing to unlock it all.
“I’m gonna over-rule you this time, Marshall,” Cody piped up as he stripped off his outer coat to
hand it to his wife. “Morgan’s freezing and it’ll take us forever to get back at a walk. We’re going to
trot back to the trailers.”
I glanced at Kelli, and she nodded. “Ready when you are, cowboy.”
“Okay.” A trot wasn’t so bad, and Cody was right. Morgan needed to get warm as soon as
possible. I eased Bay Rum into a nice, extended trot, and Kelli stayed right beside us. She shot me a
cocky grin, and her horse’s head edged in front of mine.
“So that’s how you want it, eh?” I leaned forward a bit and Bay Rum popped ahead of her horse.
“You’re not getting away that easy!” Kelli stood in her stirrups and pulled her hat down tighter.
“Come on, Joy, let’s show off those long legs of yours.”
I itched to really give her a race, but I didn’t speed up any more. I opened my mouth to say that I
didn’t want to get the horses too hot on the way home, but I never got the chance. Out of nowhere,
Joy’s head jerked to the right, and she jumped left. All I saw was the white of her crazy eye as she
slammed into my horse, then took off at a dead gallop.
Kelli had been half out of the saddle when it happened, standing up to race me, and I saw her
grasping for the saddle horn and the reins all at once. The horse gave another little hop to the side,
and my world came to a gut-wrenching stop as Kelli lost her balance and fell… with her foot still in
the stirrup.
“Kelli!” I was already going after that stupid horse with everything Bay Rum could give me, but
Cody was cussing and shouting my name. I barely heard him, but something checked me when I saw
Kelli’s boot pop off her foot after about ten feet. She fell clear, and I pulled up hard to keep from
trampling her as her nutty mare high-tailed it for parts unknown with the reins flapping behind her.
The horse could go break her fool neck for all I cared.
I jumped out of the saddle and had her pulled into my arms in an instant. “Kelli! Talk to me, baby.”
Her coat was ripped and her lip was bloody, and her hair was full of snow. Morgan and Cody were
both there a second later, both shouting her name. But Kelli’s eyes didn’t open.
L ight pierced my brain. I winced and groaned, but it wasn’t going away. Neither was
Marshall’s voice. “Kelli! Say something!”
I squinted and swatted his hand away. “I will if you stop trying to pry my eyes open. What’s with
all the yelling and the flashlight in my face?”
He sat back on his heels and pushed his hat back on his head. “I was trying to make sure you
weren’t dead. You were out cold.”
“No, I wasn’t. I could hear you the whole time.”
“Sure, you could. And that’s not a bump on your head. It’s a tiara. Are you okay?”
I scrunched up my face and focused on my body, taking a few deep breaths. “My ankle hurts.
Where’s my boot?”
“Over there, and you’re not going to be putting it back on anytime soon. Morgan’s calling 911
now.”
“911? I don’t need—” I sat up and immediately felt sick. “Oof.” I laid back down and realized that
I was lying on Marshall’s coat on cold, hard ground. He leaned over me again, his cowboy hat framed
by an iron sky and his face a tight mask of worry. I tried to smile at him, but then I remembered how
I’d gotten there. “Hey, where’s my horse?”
Marshall’s expression went dark. “Cody’s still chasing her, as far as I know. Crazy brute just took
off.”
“She’s not crazy. She saw a snake.”
Marshall rolled his eyes. “A snake? Come on, Kel, it’s December. Try again.”
“Well, it looked like a snake. I saw it, too. It was probably a branch, but we were trotting straight
for it and she saw it jump up. I think she almost stepped on it.”
Marshall’s teeth glinted against his tanned face, but he bit off whatever he was going to say and
shook his head. “We’re not doing this now. Let’s just get you to the hospital.”
“I’m not going to the hospital.” I pulled myself up on my elbow and saw Morgan, about ten feet
away and pacing while she talked on the phone. She was watching me like a hawk, and she raised her
eyebrows in question when I looked at her. I gave her a cheesy grin and a thumbs up, and she turned
around to finish her call.
“All right. Yes, she’s up and talking now. I don’t think you need to send the ambulance, we can
bring her in. Okay, we’ll do that. Thanks.” She hung up, then came and squatted beside me. She
brushed the hair off the side of my face and gave me a smile. “How’s your head?”
“Feels like someone put it in the microwave and pushed ‘popcorn.’ Help me up.”
Morgan glanced at Marshall. “If you think you can. Are you sure?”
“Sure, I’m sure. It’s just a goose egg.”
“And maybe a broken ankle,” Marshall growled. “You’re not walking anywhere. I’ll carry you to
the truck.”
“That’s a mile away. I’m fine,” I insisted, holding my hand out to Morgan instead of Marshall. She
looked skeptical but tugged me upright all the same. The world swam and tilted for a few seconds and
I had to close my eyes to make it all straighten out. My right ankle was screaming, and I was listing
sideways when Marshall caught me around the waist.
“You’re in no condition to walk, baby.”
“I’ll be all right. Just give me a second.” I blinked a few times and let go a shaky breath. I couldn’t
pass out now! I wobbled, and Marshall’s arm tightened around me. It was so nice, knowing he was
the kind of guy who would treat me like his princess…
But I wasn’t in the mood to be his princess. I wanted to be an equal, and right now, I looked pretty
darned stupid. Nobody else had lost control of their horse and gotten dumped like I had. And worse,
Morgan was looking colder by the minute. It was my fault she was still out in the weather. I sucked in
a gulp of air and forced weight onto my injured ankle.
Cold sweat broke out on my forehead almost immediately. It hurt like all get out and I just wanted
to cry like a baby and let Marshall carry me, but the only thing that hurt more than my ankle was my
pride. “There,” I quavered breathlessly. “All good. See? I can walk back.”
“Bull. Come here.” I wouldn’t say Marshall used physical force, but he didn’t give me a chance to
object. He scooped me up like a feather and slid my butt into Bay Rum’s saddle. “Can you swing your
leg over on your own?”
I tried not to make a face, but it really hurt, and my head was swimming. Still, I lifted my right leg
over the saddle horn and gingerly rested it along the horse’s side. “Okay. I’m fine.”
“Good. Lean forward a bit.” Marshall stepped in the stirrup and swung up behind me, then pulled
my shoulders back into his chest. I let my head rest back on him and we started back for the trailers. I
was glad he couldn’t see my face right then, because the whole time, I was fighting tears. And they
weren’t because of my ankle.
We were walking down the slope where Cody and Morgan’s driveway was going to be when Cody
came trotting up from the snowy meadow with my horse in tow. My heart beat a little slower when I
could see that she wasn’t hurt.
“Boy, howdy, this mare can run!” Cody whistled when he met up with us. “Wouldn’t let me catch
her until she was good and ready. You ok, Kelli?”
I nodded and hoped no one would notice the tremble in my voice. “I’ll be fine. Thanks for catching
her.”
Marshall didn’t say anything, but I heard a faint hiss of his breath and his arm tightened around me.
He wouldn’t even look at Joy—just turned his horse back toward the trailers. Ten minutes later,
Morgan and I were sitting in the cab of Marshall’s truck with the heater on, watching the guys load the
horses up for home. I winced and peeled down my sock to look at my ankle. It was already black and
blue.
What a rotten way to end such a promising day. Instead of enjoying a romantic dinner with my
cowboy, I’d be parked on a hospital gurney waiting for an X-ray and maybe even an MRI. And once
that was over, I got to look forward to a fight with my boyfriend because he would want me to get rid
of my horse.
Chapter 14
“I don’t need a nurse escort and I sure as heck don’t need a wheelchair. Really! It’s not broken, so
I’m fine.” Kelli shifted her weight and tried to hide the flash of pain that hit her eyes when she
bounced on the toes of her sore foot.
She’d already refused the crutches the hospital offered for support. Not that I minded lending her
my shoulder when she lost her balance, but she was mostly too proud to let me help. This was about
the tenth time in the last five minutes she’d used the word “fine.”
I crossed my arms. “You heard the nurse. Hospital policy. It’s a liability thing, especially after
injuring your ankle and getting thumped on the head. Cool your jets. They’ll be right back with your
ride.”
“I’m walking. See, it’s better already. I just need to move it, so it doesn’t stiffen up.”
“Kelli…”
“Just as far as the bathroom,” she insisted. “The nurse will be another fifteen minutes if I know
anything about how hospitals work. Look, it’s right across the hall. I don’t need help. Unless you think
you need to accompany me.” She arched her brow and gave me a challenging look.
I shook my head in surrender, and she limped off. The woman was bound and determined to injure
herself because she was just too darned stubborn to let anyone help her. Her independence, I adored. I
wasn’t so sure I could handle her bullheadedness.
A few minutes later, the door across the hall cracked open and Kelli emerged, leaning just a bit on
the handle as she tried to walk out. Her eyes met mine, and for a second, she wasn’t hiding behind a
false front. Her lips were pale and there was a crease around her brown eyes that told how much she
hurt.
That was all I needed to rush forward.
She stiffened, looked like she was going to tell me to back off, then switched her attention to
someone coming down the hall. It was a brunette in her mid-twenties wearing a classy blue pants suit
with her hair pulled up in a sleek bun. “Audrey Livingstone?”
The woman stopped and blinked as if she’d just noticed us. “Kelli Mason? What are you doing in
the ER?”
“Oh, just a twisted ankle. No big deal. Hey, do you know Marshall Walker? Marshall, this is
Audrey, one of the therapy center’s best sponsors.”
Audrey turned to me politely. “I don’t think I’ve had the pleasure, Mr. Walker. I’m just a guest in
town—Katherine Tracy is my sister.”
I offered my hand, and she hesitated before she took it, just barely touching my fingers. “Nice to
meet you,” I said, “but I’m afraid I don’t know your sister either. Any relation to James Tracy?”
Audrey’s upper lip twitched. “Unfortunately.”
Kelli cleared her throat. “Yeah. So, I hesitate to ask, but what are you doing in the ER?”
“Katherine was having chest pains again. It’s the potassium. I think they’re going to have to admit
her overnight. I was just walking Lizzy out to her father, since he finally showed up to take her home.”
Audrey’s eyes narrowed in contempt, but only for a second, then her face smoothed once more.
“Oh, Audrey, I’m sorry. I thought I heard Kat was getting better.”
Audrey’s expression was unreadable. “One can only hope. I’m sorry, Kelli, but I have to get back.
Take care of yourself, please. I’ve had enough of this place and I’m a little weary of seeing my friends
and family here.”
“I will,” Kelli promised. Her eyes followed Audrey as she left, then her shoulders sagged and she
stared at the floor. When she looked up again, her forehead was wrinkled and she was worrying her
pretty plump lip with her teeth. “I… uh… I think I’ve been a bit of a pain, huh?”
I put my arm around her shoulders and was relieved when she actually let herself lean on me.
“Naw… well, maybe a little. I like you when you’re being stubborn.”
She gave me a playful nudge with her elbow but wouldn’t meet my eyes. “You know what I mean.”
“Fine. You’re a cussed mule who keeps pullin’ up the picket line and causing me all kinds of
headaches, but you’ve got the prettiest little—”
“I think you can stop there, cowboy,” she laughed. “Look, I know I’ve been pushing hard. I’ve
wanted this for so long, and when things don’t work the way I want them to, I just…” She frowned
and her eyebrows pinched together in thought. “I guess seeing Audrey reminded me that… that we
can’t always control everything.”
I tightened my arm around her. “How do you mean?”
“I mean, she’s not just a guest in town, like she says. If she had her way, she’d be back East making
high six figures as a hot-shot oral surgeon in Manhattan. Instead, she’s stuck here, filling kids’ cavities
by day, then running Kat’s dialysis and checking Lizzy’s homework by night. I guess sometimes reality
isn’t what we want it to be.” She sighed heavily and frowned at the floor, but she let her head rest
against my shoulder.
“Hey, baby, it’s been a long day and a crazy week. Let’s just get you home. Cody and Morgan got
the horses all back where they belong, and Luke is waiting for us out front in his truck. I was hoping
you wouldn’t object to crashing at the bunkhouse again.”
She tipped her chin up to give me a suspicious look. “Why? Trying to keep an eye on me?”
I gave her a slow grin. “Maybe I just like having you close. And we all like your coffee better than
Dusty’s.”
Kelli snickered, then sighed. “Fine, on one condition.”
“I have a sneaking suspicion that I already know what it is.”
“No ‘I-told-you-so’s. I can’t do that right now. I just… not now.”
I rubbed my eyes. Sooner or later, we would have to have a serious talk about that horse of hers.
I’d have to spell out again why this was all a terrible idea, and she’d… I sighed. She’d ignore me and
do as she pleased. Probably get hurt again, and the next time I might not be around to make sure she
was okay.
At least if I didn’t pick a fight about it tonight, maybe I could get her to listen to me tomorrow. Or
the next day. “Whatever. Let’s just get you home and put your foot up on ice. You’re going to stay off it
like the doctor said, right?”
One of her eyebrows curved upward, and she brewed up a wolfish smile to match it. “Maybe. Do
you like romance movies?”
“No.”
She lifted her shoulder. “Too bad. The only time I sit still for over ten minutes is when I’m
watching romance movies, but I get bored trying to watch them alone. Shame I won’t have any
company in that drafty old bunkhouse.”
I shook my head and leaned down to kiss her cheek. “You are the most exasperating woman I’ve
ever met. Deal, but I get veto rights on whatever you pick.”
“Three out of five. Otherwise, we’ll be fighting about what to watch all night.”
“That’s ‘debating,’ sweetheart. ‘Fighting’ is what we used to do.”
“Better word. Oh, look, my ride’s here.” Her face fell into a scowl as the nurse pulled up with a
wheelchair. I took the handles as she plopped into it with less grace than she’d ever admit. Her ankle
must have been a lot sorer than she wanted me to think. But she gave a confident thumbs up to the
nurse and me, then laughed, “Giddy Up!”
M arshall didn’t give me the six-a.m. wake-up call the next morning. I assumed he wanted to
let me sleep in, but I’d owned a coffee shop too long for that. I rolled over at five and stared
at my phone for a while until I realized he wouldn’t drag me up to the ranch house for an early
breakfast unless I texted him that I was already awake.
It was a couple of minutes before the message showed that he’d read it. Then I got an instant reply.

p.emphasis: Didn’t think you’d be up yet. Sleep ok?
Fine. I could get used to how quiet it is here compared to living in town.
You won’t think it’s quiet in about five minutes.

section.p: I squinted and stared at my phone, waiting for him to explain, but he didn’t have to. I’d heard the
cows in the nearest pen carrying on for about ten minutes—I guessed they were impatient for
breakfast—but something must have riled them up. Suddenly, they started bawling like they were
being roped and branded. Maybe they were.
I rolled out of bed, pulled some clothes on, and then went to the window to see what the ruckus
was all about. It was still dark, but the light from my window cast a dim glow over the corrals.
Marshall and Evan trotted by on their horses, slapping their ropes on their thighs and calling out to the
herd. I grabbed an extra coat I'd found hanging in the bunkhouse and limped out to the porch to watch.
The cold slammed into my lungs like a physical force. I ducked my chin deeper into my coat and
wished I knew what had happened to the heavy, waterproof one I’d worn riding the day before. I
hadn’t been on the porch a full minute, but my toes were already freezing, and my ears were so cold
they felt hot. Yet there were four cowboys saddled up before dawn, pushing about twenty new cows
into the pen with the others.
The creatures were black against the fresh snow, and they surged restlessly around the corral,
bumping into the fences and each other and searching for their place in the group. Luke and Marshall
were quietly pushing them around as a group, trying to get them to settle together. Dusty was the first
out of the gate and rode over to talk to me.
“What happened?” I asked him.
“I’m surprised you didn’t hear it crash. We lost a tree on the back forty last night, about three
o’clock or so. Happens every winter. The freeze and thaw over and over again weaken the roots, then
the weight of the ice and snow gets to be too much. It took out a line of fencing when it fell. Took us
about an hour to get all the cows rounded back up, but they’ll be safe here until we get the fence
fixed.”
My jaw dropped. “How long have you been out here?”
He shrugged. “Soon as we heard it. Reckon we all came out about the same time and started
saddling up. Man, Cody sure picked a convenient time to move out!”
“What about your ranch hands? Isn’t it their job to come help you guys?”
Dusty narrowed his eyes and got a faraway look, staring at the herd in the dusky corral. “They just
work here. At the end of the day, their life is their own. For us, this is our life. You have to love it, or
you have to get out.”
I crossed my arms and tried to stop shivering. “And do you love it?”
“Right now, what I love is the thought of hot grits and strong coffee,” Marshall said as he trotted
up. “Dusty, I’ll put your horse up if you want to wrangle some breakfast for us all.”
The youngest Walker brother still hadn’t answered my question, but from the look on his face, I
didn’t think he’d ever meant to. He tipped his hat to me and stepped off his horse, handing the reins to
Marshall. “Be ready in fifteen minutes,” he called as he walked into the darkness.
Marshall watched him go, then gave a low whistle and shook his head, muttering something.
“What’s that?”
“Hmm? Oh, just Dusty. He’s been lollygagging about here all month. Can’t figure why. Last year, he
was making some noise about wanting to moonlight at the fancy college in town, but I thought Luke put
him off of it.”
“What for?”
Marshall shrugged. “What does anyone go to college for? Blasted waste. If I’m going to park my
rear at a desk, someone better darn well be paying me to be there, not the other way around.”
“I meant, why would Luke talk him out of it? If Dusty wants to take classes, why shouldn’t he?”
“Luke probably didn’t want to lose his heeler. Dusty’s a good loop. But hey, how ‘bout warming
me up for a second before I feed horses?” He swung down out of the saddle and stepped up on the
porch to pull me into his arms. He kissed me softly, his jaw cold and scruffy as it scratched my cheek.
I’m not sure why my heart started beating faster at that sensation, but it did. “How’s your ankle?” he
murmured against my neck.
“Ready to sprint about as far as the coffee pot.”
He grinned. “I’ll race you. Be right back, baby.”
K elli wouldn’t stay off her foot. I couldn’t say I was surprised because I wouldn’t have,
either. Ranch life doesn’t stop for a twisted ankle, and Kelli, for better or worse, was as
hardworking a girl as any born and raised ranch wife I’d ever known.
She’d returned to her own place after one night in the bunkhouse and went back to taking daily
shifts in the Coffee Wagon. Several hours a day, on her feet. I wished she’d take it easy long enough to
heal up, but I was secretly sort of happy to see that nothing seemed to slow her down.
She wouldn’t talk about that horse, though. Every time I tried to bring it up, she found some way to
change the subject—usually something that involved me getting lipstick all over my neck. And I knew
she was limping out to Jess’s barn every afternoon to work with that crazy mare because Dusty had
happened to talk to Jess at the feed store about how Kelli was loping in the round pen every day that
it wasn’t frozen. But she wouldn’t tell me what she was doing, and I wasn’t sure I wanted to know,
anyway. It would just make us fight again.
Things at the ranch were pretty quiet for a couple of weeks. We turned out all the young horses at
Cody’s place for the rest of the winter. We didn’t lose any more fences, and all the cows looked fat
and content. It was just the daily slog of hauling hay, keeping water tanks defrosted, and fixing things
around the ranch that had been ignored in the busier months.
Cody spent most of his time working on their new place, and Dad seemed to be gone most days, but
the rest of us were mainly in a routine. I saw Kelli every day, even if it was just a quick run to the
Coffee Wagon when I knew she was there. But on Friday, a week before Christmas, I took her out to
Beaufort’s.
She met me at the door looking fresh and gorgeous, but I glimpsed her cowboy boots on the porch,
half covered in paddock mud, and I raised an eyebrow at her. She just smiled sweetly and caught me
by the chin for a kiss. “Missed you too, cowboy. Let’s go.”
“Is your ankle feeling better, or are you just too stubborn to limp on it?”
She hooked her arm through mine and gave her right foot a vigorous stomp. “Good as new. Doc
says they won’t even need to amputate.”
I opened the truck door for her. “Then, sweetheart, how about going dancing later? Maybe redeem
the last time we went dancing.”
“You mean Cody and Morgan’s wedding when you ignored me?”
I pulled her close and lifted her curvy hips to set her in my truck. I liked how she let me raise her
up, and I liked it even better when she looped her arms around my shoulders, pushed my hat back, and
gazed deep into my eyes, barely out of kissing range.
“For the record, I didn’t ignore you. I never took my eyes off of you.”
“But you wouldn’t ask me to dance.”
“Well, I’m asking now. Let me show you off and make every guy in town jealous.”
She cocked her head and pursed her lips. “And after?”
I slid her hand down my shoulders and kissed the back of her knuckles. “We’ll see when we get
there, but I bet we can improve on the last time I drove you home from a dance.”
“Worth finding out.”
S weet Moses, but that cowboy can dance. Usually, Luke is the Walker brother you see twirling
girls around, and I’d learned that Dusty can two-step pretty well when he has to. But Marshall
must have been storing it up all these years, because I hadn’t had that much fun on the dance floor
since… well, since that ill-fated prom. Who knew my handsome rancher could swing and dip and
sugar push as well as he could ride? I’d never been led by a partner who actually made me a better
dancer, and I started blushing like a schoolgirl when everyone else stepped back to clap for us. When
Marshall said he wanted to show me off in town, I guess he meant it.
“I need a break!” I gasped as the fourth song ended. “Buy a girl a drink?”
“Thought you’d never ask.” Marshall slid his hand down to the small of my back and guided me
toward a table. He held up two fingers to the waitress, and she plopped our sodas down a minute
later.
I lifted mine. “To us.”
Marshall’s grin was brighter than the neon lights on the dance floor. “Amen. Never thought I’d like
wintertime, but you’re giving me all kinds of reasons not to be itching for spring.”
I squinted and took a sip of my drink. “What’s wrong with spring?”
“Calving season.”
I arched a brow. “And?”
He swallowed and set his glass down. “Isn’t that enough? I’ll be out in the field for weeks. Darn
sure won’t have time to take my favorite woman out dancing.”
“Well, I suppose I’ll have to think of some other entertainment while you’re working.” I gave him a
mischievous wink.
“Uh… yeah… speaking of entertainment… We should probably talk about—”
I nudged his arm hard and pointed to someone just walking in the door. “Hey… sorry, didn’t mean
to interrupt. Isn’t that Kaycee Whitmore?”
Marshall swiveled around. “Sure is. Must’ve come home for Christmas. That was fast, wasn't NFR
just wrapping a couple of days ago?”
My heart did a flip, and crazy ideas were exploding in my head. Kaycee Whitmore was a multiple
world champion barrel racer, and yes, I'd just tuned in to catch her latest championship run in Vegas.
She was incredible to watch.
She lived in Oklahoma now, but her family still owned a farm down the road from Morgan’s old
place. In fact, she was the one who’d given me barrel racing lessons back when I was a teenager, and
I’d idolized her for most of my life. And here she was, back in town, right when Joy and I were
figuring each other out and I needed someone with Kaycee’s kind of expertise.
“Don’t even think it, Kel,” Marshall warned.
“Too late,” I whispered. Kaycee had spotted us and was walking to our table.
She pointed at me with a huge smile and started laughing like she was thrilled to see me. “Kelli
Mason? It is you! You haven’t changed one bit. Oh, give me a hug, none of that hand-shaking.”
“Kaycee! So good to see you again.” I hugged her back—she didn’t give me an option.
“So, how the heck are you? Still riding?”
I felt Marshall’s eyes on me, and my cheeks were hot as I answered, but I wasn’t about to let
Kaycee think I’d wasted what I’d learned from her. “As a matter of fact, I just got another horse again
after a really long time. She’s a good one, I think. Big and gorgeous and fast.”
“Awesome! I hope I have time to see her while I’m in town.” Kaycee’s eyes wandered to Marshall
with a question in them.
“Oh, excuse me. Kaycee, this is Marshall Walker.”
Her brows arched at Marshall’s name. “That’s right. Been a long time. How’s your dad?”
Marshall shook Kaycee’s hand. “Still kicking. Working too hard and drinking too much coffee.”
Kaycee laughed. “That sounds like Blake. Well, hey, Ben’s waiting for me, but let’s catch up. Let
me text you my number.” She pulled out her phone, and we exchanged info. She gave me one last hug.
“Don’t forget to call me, now. I want to see that new horse of yours before I leave. Better yet, bring
her over, and we’ll ride together one day.”
I had chills racing up and down my spine as Kaycee walked away to join her family. I couldn’t
believe she even remembered me, let alone wanted to make a special point of catching up. What
would she think of Joy? Only one way to find out.
“Kelli…”
I held up a finger. “Don’t you do it, cowboy. Don’t you ruin my fantasy right after taking me dancing
and all. Let me daydream for a few minutes.”
Marshall gave me a cynical look as he finished his drink. “Nope, I made that mistake once. Come
on, let’s get out of here. I have calves to feed.”
Chapter 15
“W here’s Dad? He’s disappeared again.”
Evan looked up from his paper and shrugged. “Maybe he’s got a girlfriend.”
I snorted. “Yeah, right. Well, wherever he is, Cody wanted to ask him about that nice filly he’s got
in training.”
Evan’s face had disappeared behind stock reports again, but I saw the top of his head bobbing.
“Maserati?”
“That’s the one. Said she was off during her work today and Doc Burns is coming out to check
her.”
“Hope it’s nothing serious.”
“Cody said it probably wasn’t. Just a tiny bit stiff, but he’s not taking any chances with her.”
Evan nodded and went back to reading. Maserati was the ranch’s top show horse now, and an
awful sweet little mare to boot. She probably just needed a few days of rest, but it wouldn’t be the
first time “a little off” had meant an early retirement from the show ring. No one wanted to see a nice
horse get hurt in training.
I’d be a liar if I said I hadn’t immediately thought of Kelli. If Maserati had to retire from showing
early, it didn’t necessarily mean she wouldn’t be sound for regular riding. Happened all the time, in
fact. She’d make a fantastic and trustworthy horse for Kelli.
But that was getting ahead of things. Maserati was going to be just fine after Doc took care of her.
Cody would follow her treatment to the letter and she’d be back under saddle soon. And anyway, I
didn’t have a prayer of getting Kelli onto another horse until she finally gave up on that looney one
she had.
“Did you get the lower eighty checked?” Evan asked from behind his paper.
“I thought Dusty was riding fence this afternoon. I was patching that leak in the barn roof.”
Evan lowered his paper. “I haven’t seen Dusty all day. Luke? Hey, Luke?”
Pots banging in the kitchen were the only answer he got. I groaned. That meant we were getting
Luke’s sausage and cabbage stew for dinner. Again. My stomach lurched. I’d better just swallow
some heartburn medicine now and get it over with.
It took a minute or two, but Luke finally stuck his head out. “Yeah?”
“Know where Dusty is? Marshall said he was supposed to ride fence.”
“He sent Mitch to do that. Dusty went up to Austen Conrad’s place today. Helping him plan his
herds out for next year.”
“Austen Conrad? I didn’t know they were that tight.”
Evan shrugged. “Dusty enjoys helping folks. Oh, speak of the devil.”
I looked up as the front door opened. Dusty pushed his way in and shut it immediately against the
winter wind. He hung his hat on the rack, draped his coat on the hook, and turned around.
“Am I late for supper?”
“Unfortunately not.” I pointed a thumb over my shoulder at Luke in the kitchen.
His face fell. “Again?”
“‘Fraid so. Where ya been?”
Dusty picked up the new copy of the Stockman’s magazine and started toward the den. “Out.”
I followed him. “You don’t say. What’s up with Austen Conrad?”
“He just wanted help with something. Why do you care?”
“I don’t, except he doesn’t need to hijack all your time. We need you here.”
“You need me, huh? I’m surprised you noticed I was gone.” Dusty dropped onto the old sofa and
started flipping through the magazine.
“Hard not to notice when you and Dad keep disappearing.”
Dusty shot me an annoyed look. “It just so happens that working with Austen is good business for
us. And anyway, what’s the problem? I made sure everything got done today.”
“All right, all right.” I stuffed my hands in my pockets and just watched him. Something was up,
and I had a feeling it had nothing to do with Austen Conrad’s stocker cows.
Dusty went back to his magazine. He scanned the contents page, his finger landing on something in
the index, then he turned the pages some more. Finally, he seemed to find what he was looking for,
then he stiffened and closed it again. “Why are you staring at me?”
“I’m not. You’re being touchy.”
Dusty slapped the magazine down on the side table. “I need to clean up before dinner.”
I put out my hand to stop him. “Hey.”
Dusty erupted. “Hey, what? Why are you always ragging on me, Marshall?”
“Whoa, what? I was just going to ask if you were okay.”
“Well, I’m fine! At least I would be, if you’d just leave me alone. Get out and see your girlfriend if
you need to jaw about something. I’m not in the mood.” He pushed past me and headed for his room
down the hall.
I was still standing there at the entrance to the den with my mouth hanging open in shock. When had
Dusty ever lost his temper? I could think of maybe a handful of times in his whole life. Once was
when we were little and I kicked his dog—I’d deserved the split lip he gave me. But other than that,
my little brother had always been practically unflappable. What had gotten under his skin?
He did have one good idea, though. I’d call Kelli.
I was just pulling my phone out of my pocket when it rang. I smiled when I saw her name and
answered with the flirty drawl I knew drove her wild. “Well, hello, sweetheart. Your ears must’ve
been burning.”
“Oh, really? Why is that?”
“Because I was just about to call you, too.”
“Mm, that sounds interesting. What’s up?”
“You called me first.”
“Ah, I see how it is,” she said with a laugh. “Okay, well, two things. You’re probably going to hate
them both.”
I dropped onto the sofa that Dusty had just left and kicked my feet over the side. “I’ll probably like
them just fine if you kiss me enough afterward.”
“Worth a shot. Okay, the easy one first. Mom wants me to invite you to Christmas Eve dinner. You
don’t have to say yes!”
“Well, I can’t very well say no. Especially not after how Thanksgiving went. They’ll think I hate
them.”
“Nooo, I don’t think so… Thanksgiving wasn’t that bad. Was it?”
“Is your brother bringing his kids again?”
“Will it change your answer?”
“No, but I need to know if I should wear my steel-toe boots and a hard hat.”
Kelli sighed. “You can just say no. I won’t be upset.”
That wasn’t true. She might think she’d be okay with it, but if she had to go to her mom’s Christmas
dinner alone, with her family asking where I was… well, I could just say goodbye to any Christmas
canoodling afterward. “Of course, I’ll come. What about gifts? Shouldn’t we, like, pick something out
for your parents?”
She gasped faintly. “Oh…”
“What?”
I heard a sniff. “That is the sweetest thing! I can’t believe you thought of it! Okay, so Mom is a
sucker for gardening stuff, so I bought hers when it all went on sale last fall. Dad… actually, I always
get him a new wallet, but maybe we should step it up this year, because, well. And Kevin…”
“Kelli? Hey! Let’s slow it down. How about we make it a date and go shopping later? What was
the other thing you wanted to ask me?”
“Um… Okay.” She went quiet for a few seconds, and I imagined her grimacing and biting her lip
like she always did when she was fixing to tell me something serious. “You see, it’s like this. Kaycee
Whitmore stopped by the Coffee Wagon today and invited me—again—to come ride her barrel
course. I really, really want to.”
“But?”
“But…” Another sigh. “I don’t have a way to get there. Morgan’s picking up some rescue horses
this week and getting them settled at the new place, and Jess’s trailer is getting new floorboards.
Um… you wouldn’t…?”
I scowled up at the ceiling and closed my eyes for a second. “Darlin’, I’d haul your horse to the
moon and back. You know that. I should’ve been your first call.”
“Except you don’t want me racing barrels on Joy.”
I spun around and put my feet on the floor so I could sit up. “I don’t want you getting hurt, and not
racing that horse seems like Step One on that plan.”
“She’s been doing great these last few weeks! I’ve been working on the things you and I did with
her, and Jess and Morgan gave me some pointers, and she’s actually getting way friendlier, too.
Really, you haven’t seen her, but she is.”
“Uh-huh.”
“We’ll be in a nice, safe space. No cows, no open range, no snakes—”
“That was a stick.”
“—Groomed arena. Totally private. And a top-notch professional to help me! Can you think of
anyone who knows her business better than Kaycee?”
“Yeah. Cody.”
“Come on, baby,” she crooned. I could imagine that playful pout, with her lower lip all plump and
her dark eyes looking so teasing and inviting. I was done for.
“If you’re going to talk to me like that…”
“I’ll do a lot more than talk,” she promised.
“Well…”
“Pick me up at three tomorrow?”
I sighed. “You got it, sweetheart.”
“S he’s sure a looker, Kel. Can she run?”
“She most certainly can. It’s stopping that seems to be the problem.”
Kaycee laughed as she ran an expert hand over Joy’s neck and withers. “Well, let’s see what she’s
got. I’ll go saddle Shooter and we’ll get warmed up.”
I caught Marshall’s eye over Joy’s back, and I’m sure I looked like a kid on her first trip to an
amusement park. I was getting to ride with Kaycee and Shooter? Pinch me! Shooter was her first
world champion, the horse that put her on the map ten years ago. She’d sent him home to the farm for
his retirement, but everyone who knew anything about the sport still revered Shooter’s name.
Marshall’s face was guarded, but he still smiled and clasped my hand. Bless his protective heart.
He was trying. And he hadn’t even muttered anything sour about my horse all day. “I’ll get your
saddle,” he said.
Joy was feeling more familiar to me each time I got on her. It was almost like learning a new
language—the cock of her ear, the tone and flex of her powerful muscles, the particular softness
through her whole body when I picked up the reins or touched her with my leg. I felt like she was
opening up to me the longer we were a team, and even Jess had agreed that Joy was acting more
relaxed and friendly. Yesterday, she’d actually whinnied after me when I left her stall! It was slow,
but we were getting somewhere.
