I hadn’t told Marshall this because I thought he would just say I was imagining it, but I was starting
to believe that Joy had never let herself trust anyone. Maybe being a remuda horse, ready for any of
the cowboys to ride to work, hadn’t been the right thing for her. She needed one person to put her
confidence in, someone who would take the time to know her and who would believe in her.
Someone like me. Of course, maybe I really was imagining it, but it seemed to make sense.
Kaycee took me back through some of the lessons she’d given me as a teenager. I was rusty. Holy
smokes, I was out of practice! But it wasn’t long before she had me remembering the steps toward
building a finished barrel horse. They call it “patterning,” where you might spend weeks or months
just walking or slow jogging the course. It’s about feel and body position, finding the right track for
the fastest pattern, and getting all the parts clicking smoothly together long before you ever turn on the
speed.
Joy was a champ. Kaycee taught me to find “buttons” I didn’t even know she had. A little shift of
my weight, a tweak of my hand or my foot, and Joy started to show me what she could do. I couldn’t
believe how subtle my cues to her got in such a short time, nor how quietly she was responding! Was
she really getting more confident and comfortable with me? It made me swell with pride to think of it.
After we had practiced the pattern about a thousand times at a walk and a trot, Kaycee called me
over. “You want to do a quick test before we call it a day?”
My heart started thumping, and I saw Marshall stiffen on his perch on the fence rail. “What kind of
test?”
“See if she’s got what it takes to be your barrel horse. She’s listening to you really well, and she’s
obviously had a lot of training because she’s very balanced and guides like a dream. I don’t think it
would confuse her at all to slow lope the pattern once. You could even let her loose after the last turn,
see what she offers you.”
I closed my mouth, because it was hanging open. “Uh, yeah. Sure, I can do that. Um… Just one
thing. She doesn’t really have a ‘slow lope’. How fast should I let her go?”
Kaycee looked thoughtful. “You said you had trouble stopping her when she gets going. You think
with the stuff we just worked on, you will have a better handle on her?”
“Definitely,” I lied.
“Well, let her go as fast as you’re comfortable with. Just don’t panic and try to fight her if she
wants to do more. Remember how I showed you to rate her down without getting on the muscle with
her?”
I nodded tightly. “Right. Work with her strides, turn for control. Check.”
“Okay! Let’s see what you’ve got.”
I sent a swift glance to Marshall. His eyes were narrowed, and his jaw was tight, but he was still
sitting on the fence instead of marching over to tell me what a lousy idea this was. He didn’t have to
say it—his whole expression screamed it. His gloved hands were no longer clasped lightly over his
thighs but gripping the rail now. I smiled, and he… well, that was probably supposed to be a smile
back. It looked like he was in pain.
But he couldn’t feel what I was feeling. The only mistakes Joy had made all afternoon were
because I’d cued her wrong. Kaycee thought Joy was ready, and she ought to know. I turned and made
for the top of the course, then softly asked Joy to lope off to the right. We did a couple of warm up
circles, finding her stride, then I sent her down the middle of the arena.
Her ears swept forward when she saw the barrel and I felt her body stretch out for it. Her hooves
pounded like thunder and she was getting faster with each step. No, no, it was too early to run like
this! I tried to check her down, but she was already reaching hard for the first turn. Her hind feet
grabbed the ground beneath us, and as she sat down to spin around the barrel, a light went off in my
brain.
Joy got it. She was smart and powerful and she already understood at least part of her job. And she
loved this as much as I did.
Kaycee told me later that I started laughing like a wild thing after that first turn and never stopped
until it was over. I’ll have to take her word for it. I was up over Joy’s withers, running free with her
and bleeding all my hope and confidence down through the reins. She hesitated just for an instant as
we closed in on the second barrel, but I gave her a little soft direction, and she shifted her balance for
the left turn.
“Sit! Look!” I heard Kaycee calling. “Let her go!”
Let her go? Kaycee thought I should turn Joy loose already? My horse’s strides were churning in
rhythm with my hammering pulse, and I felt like we could fly if we wanted to. For the first time, I
trusted her with everything I had. No more snatching the reins in fear that she’d bolt, no more cringing
when she made a big move. She was all heart, all try, and I’d been cheating her out of showing it to
me. I held the horn, anchored my weight in the saddle's pocket, and fastened my eyes on the last barrel
as Joy shot out of the turn.
Kaycee was shouting at me now, wanting me to turn on the speed, but I wasn’t paying much
attention to her. I was listening to Joy, and she was telling me everything I needed to know. I could
feel her thoughts, as plain as I could feel Marshall’s, in the soft touch of his hand and the gentle turn of
his smile. Joy’s ears pinwheeled once, asking me a question. She might not have been confident in the
pattern yet, but she was confident in me. I answered, and she throttled forward, setting her hind feet
once more to skid around the barrel like she’d been doing it for years. And then we were turning for
home.
She found a whole new gear as we hit the straight. Long legs stretched out, and she was snorting
with every step, glorying in the freedom of speed. We streaked between the cones that marked the end
of the pattern, but Joy didn’t slow down. With the end of the arena coming up fast, I had to make a
quick decision. I’d never get her stopped that soon. It wasn’t something we’d worked on, like Cody’s
reining horses, that could go from top speed to a sliding stop in a few feet. Joy would just fight to
keep running. So, I turned her.
“Go left!” I heard Kaycee shout.
Too late. I’d already committed to the right turn. Even as I did it, I knew what a dangerous position
I’d put us in. Joy had just turned left around the last two barrels and she was still surging forward on
the left lead, reaching farther each stride with her left feet. By turning her sharply to the right at this
kind of speed, I was throwing her off balance and setting her up to fall. But before I could even shrink
back in terror of my mistake, Joy’s hips lifted beneath me, her shoulders shifted…
And we were loping around the corner on the right lead. She was even slowing down, her nostrils
flared and her teeth showing as she champed the bit. I could breathe again.
“That horse just saved your bacon!” Kaycee laughed.
I nodded and gave a nervous chuckle as I got Joy stopped. “Yeah, she sure did.”
Kaycee was grinning and shaking her head as she stepped off of Shooter. “I’d say you’ve got
yourself a nice horse there, girl. Keep it up and maybe I’ll see you on the circuit with her.”
I clambered numbly to the ground and stroked Joy’s sweaty neck. “Hah, sure. That would be
something.”
“I’m actually serious. This mare just ran about an eighteen second pattern on her very first time
through above a trot and you never even asked her to try hard. She’s got speed and some big-time
athleticism. Not to mention, instead of falling down or running into the fence when you made a
mistake, she just did one of the prettiest lead changes I’ve ever seen and made it all look easy.”
“Uh, yeah. I can’t believe I made a rookie mistake like that. It could have cost us…”
“But it didn’t. Your horse has some real class, and you still ride like you’ve got super glue holding
you to the saddle. We just need to work on your timing and your confidence, get you some more
experience, and you guys will be unstoppable.”
I blinked and tried to think of words. Nothing came out. I was in such awe that my hero, the woman
I’d wanted to ride like since I was a kid, had such nice things to say about my horse and me. I
swallowed and managed, “Th-thanks.”
“Don’t mention it. Let’s get these horses cooled out, huh?”
My heart was soaring as I loosened Joy’s cinch, and we fell into step beside Kaycee and Shooter.
Could this be the best day of my life? Not quite, because getting snowed in with Marshall still took
the cake. But this was sure a close second. My eyes found him on the rail, and I beamed the biggest
smile that would fit on my face. He just nodded and held up a hand as he hopped off the fence and
went to warm up the truck.
Chapter 16
“H and me that impact.” Cody reached down from his perch on the ladder, and I passed the driver
to him.
“So, you don’t think it’s anything to worry about?” I asked.
Cody didn’t answer right away. He was focused on his task, driving home the screws for the
lighting box he was hanging. The therapy program’s future office in their new barn was all framed and
almost ready for insulation and sheeting. Both Cody and Morgan were spending every spare minute
working on it. Today, I’d come up to help him with the interior wiring, and we were just about done.
Cody finished setting the fixture with a grunt and lowered the impact driver to me as he climbed
down the ladder. “Remind me again what we’re supposed to be worried about. Kelli’s horse? Blake?
Or were we back to talking about Dusty?”
“Well, now that you mention it, I am a little baffled by Dusty. When’s the last time you saw him
blow his top?”
“When Luke insulted a girl he was sweet on. Lacy? Lucy? Something like that.”
“Laini, and Dusty wasn’t sweet on her any more than he is on Kelli or Morgan. He just wouldn’t
stand for Luke to call her horse-faced because she wouldn’t dance with him. Anyway, that’s not the
point. Something’s eating him.”
Cody emptied the screws out of his tool belt and folded up the ladder. “Maybe he’s sick of the
ranch. Wouldn’t be the first one to want out of the life.”
I didn’t like how my stomach twisted at that thought. Cody had only said aloud what I’d been
starting to fear, and the worst part was, he could be right. “But where would he go?” I immediately
protested. “What’s out there that he can’t have at home?”
“Most folks call it a ‘normal life.’ I hear it’s the new thing.” Cody set the ladder down in the hall
and surveyed the office with a satisfied grin. “Starting to take shape! Thanks for your help today.
Morgan’s handy with tools, but she won’t touch wiring. It would have been a month before I could’ve
done it all by myself.”
“Yeah.” I hooked my thumbs in my tool belt and stared at the open rafters. The room design was
simple and not very large, but it would be airy, light, and beautiful when they had it finished. Cody
had told me they planned the same sort of look for their house, and he’d showed me the blueprint. It
suited their style, and I thought it was about what I’d want to build for myself someday. If I had
someone to share it with. “So, back to the other thing I was asking you…”
Cody shook his head and grinned. “You know, about six months ago, you were the one teasing me
about fretting over a woman.”
“Yeah, well, just pretend you’re not enjoying this. I’m serious. What do I do? Is that horse ever
going to come around for her, or is it just a matter of time until she winds up in the hospital again?”
Cody shrugged and started coiling up extension cords. “You said she did fine yesterday at
Kaycee’s, right?”
“More than fine. That’s what scares me.”
He stopped and squinted at me. “You’re such a glass-is-half-empty type. Can’t you accept when
something good happens?”
I crossed my arms. “No, because as soon as you relax and think stuff is going well, that’s when
everything hits the fan.”
“Not always.”
“Usually.”
“Look,” he said with an exasperated chuckle, “you can’t stop every bad thing from happening. I
don’t even think it’s healthy to try.”
“What do you mean?”
“Remember when your dad let us drive the old truck up in the mountains for an overnight camping
trip when we were, like, twelve?”
“I’d just turned thirteen. But come on, we drove that thing in the fields every day to feed stock, and
we never even left the ranch. We knew those mountains like the backs of our hands. Not that big of a
deal.”
“Sure, it was. All kinds of things could have gone wrong, but your dad figured we were ready for
the challenge. Evan told me when we got back that your mom didn’t sleep the whole time we were
gone, but they still let us go. And you know what? We felt like men after that. We’d tested ourselves a
little and learned that we could handle things.”
“So, what are you saying? You think Kelli can handle that crazy horse?”
“First thing I’m saying is that you need to stop calling her crazy.”
I scowled. “She is, though.”
“She’s touchy. A little hot, and maybe a little funny about some things, but definitely not crazy.”
“She’s unpredictable.”
Cody shook his head. “I don’t think she is. I was watching her when she spooked in the hills a
while back, and Kelli’s right. She stepped on a stick under the snow and the end snapped up and
scared her. Maybe she overreacted, and maybe Kelli panicked, but—”
“See, that’s my point! Kelli hasn’t been back in the saddle long enough to handle surprises. You
don’t have time to think when stuff like that happens. Yesterday they ran this incredible barrel pattern,
but then at the end, Kelli turned the mare against her lead and almost pulled her over.”
Cody raised a brow. “Almost?”
I sighed and stuffed my hands in my pockets. “Okay, not almost. The horse changed leads and took
care of her, but she shouldn’t have had to. An experienced rider wouldn’t have made a mistake like
that. It would have been sheer instinct to know which direction to turn.”
“And you don’t gain that kind of instinct on push-button horses. Sorry, Marshall. I know you want
to protect her—heaven knows, I understand—but you have to let her do this or you’ll push her away.
It’s not about barrel racing to her, it’s much bigger. She’s got something to prove to herself, and if you
try to babysit her…”
I hung my head and scuffed my boot through a bit of sawdust on the plywood floor. “Yeah, I know. I
made that mistake with her once.”
He gave me a sympathetic smile and clapped me on the shoulder. “I’ve got bigger problems than
you. Morgan wants to have kids.”
“And you don’t?”
“Of course, I do. We settled that before we got married, but we thought we’d wait a while.” He
grimaced. “Guess she’s already tired of waiting.”
“That was fast. So, what are you going to do?”
He got a lopsided grin and shook his head. “Complain and kick the dirt for a few weeks, then give
in and do whatever my woman wants. That’s how it works, brother.”
“That doesn’t sound right. What about teamwork, what about communication, doing the smart thing,
holding to what you agreed?”
“It’s a relationship, not a business contract. Look, I don’t have all the answers. I had a terrible
track record from my first marriage and Morgan and I are just starting out. I know one thing, though.
We work best when we support each other’s dreams. No one wins if we shoot each other down.”
I pointed at him. “You’re not that subtle, you know.”
“Never claimed to be.”
“Well…” I went to the workbench and picked up my hat. “I guess maybe I let it ride for now. I still
think the horse is a time bomb, but I can’t really stop her without picking a fight.”
“A fight you won’t win.” Cody pulled on his coat and fished the truck keys out of his pocket.
“Come on. Morgan’s got clients till late tonight, and I’m hungry. I’ll treat you to a steak for helping me
today.”
“You don’t need to do that.”
Cody opened his truck door and got in. “Sure, I do. I hope to con you into a work party soon.
Morgan’s got a bunch of the White Pines clients all organized to help finish this barn off so we can
move in, and I could use someone who knows what they’re doing to help keep things going smoothly.
Bunch of kids with more excitement than focus, know what I mean?”
I grunted. “I can do that. What day?”
“New Year’s Eve, of all days. Supposed to be this big party. Wanna change your mind now?”
New Year’s? I’d been planning to spend a romantic day with Kelli. A snowy outing followed by
hot chocolate by the fireside, and a midnight kiss to start the next year off just right. But if Morgan
was having a work party for White Pines, Kelli would be sure to be there, so there wasn’t much help
for it. “I’ll come.”
“Well, thanks. We could sure use you. You coming over for Christmas Eve next week? We’re
having pizza and making pies.”
“No. Kelli’s folks want me to come over.”
He gave a low whistle as he started the truck. “Sounding serious. If it goes like Thanksgiving and
you need to let off some steam later… don’t come over.” He got a funny smile and winked. “I might
be busy letting my wife win an argument.”
I rolled my eyes. “Just drive.”
“S he’s sure changed in these last few weeks.”
I looked up from brushing Joy as Jess walked into the stables, swinging a halter over her
shoulder and looking stunning as usual. If it hadn’t been for the engine grease on her jeans and the mud
on her boots, I’d swear she’d just stepped out of a modeling session.
I scratched Joy’s ear, and she gently leaned into me to show how much she enjoyed it. That was a
new thing she’d started just this week. “She sure has. She whinnied when I pulled up to the barn again
today.”
“She only does that when she hears your car engine. No one else even gets a swish of her tail.”
Jess came to stroke my horse’s forelock, but Joy stiffened and pulled her head away. “Guess she only
likes you,” she said with a shrug.
“I don’t think that’s it. I think she just needs to warm up to someone before she lets them in. Trust is
a big deal with her.”
“I know a lot of people like that.” Jess slid the halter off her shoulder and held it out to me. “Merry
Christmas.”
My eyes dropped to the soft leather. It was a brand-new custom halter with a white cotton lead
rope and a shiny brass nameplate that read “Kelli & Joy.” “Aw, Jess. You shouldn’t have. I didn’t get
you anything nearly this nice.”
“Well, you deserve it. I don’t know anyone more dedicated to making their dreams come true.”
I hugged her. “Thank you, but I’m in good company. I sure appreciate all your help.”
“Of course! You know, I’ll admit it. I was worried about this horse at first.”
“Hah. You and everyone else.”
“Yeah, but you kept at it, and now look at you. You took the time to build a relationship with her,
and she’s flourishing. I think you guys are a great team.” She stuffed her hands in her pockets and
squeezed her shoulders like a little kid. “So? What about that other relationship?”
I grinned modestly. “It’s incredible. Marshall’s… he’s a great guy. I’ve had a thing for him since
we were kids, and being with him is even more amazing than I had imagined. Just about everything I
could ever want.”
Jess arched a brow. “‘Just about?’ What’s missing?”
“Oh, it’s nothing, really. Just…” I blew out a sigh and feathered the hair away from my face.
“Sometimes I feel like he’s more interested in keeping me safe than letting me be me. And there’s the
whole ranch life thing.”
“What’s that?”
I smoothed out a few stray hairs in Joy’s mane. “He’s as good as married to the ranch. I mean, I
knew they worked really hard, and I know how one bad season can completely wipe them out, so
they’re always fretting about rain and market prices and this, that and the other. But I guess I didn’t
fully understand it until I saw them all bail out of a nice warm house in the middle of a snowstorm,
three in the morning, to go save their cows.”
“Yeah. The ranch always comes first, doesn’t it?”
“I guess it’s how it has to be, but it takes some getting used to. I wonder if Marshall would expect
me to fix fence in the middle of the night, too. If, I mean, we got married or something.”
“Well, the ranch wives I know are pretty tough. Don’t think most of them stay in bed when their
husbands are out saving the herd.”
I frowned. Could I live like that? I wanted to say yes, unequivocally. I’d do whatever it took to be
the one by Marshall’s side, loving him and building a life together. But was I ready to marry the ranch
if I married the man? I cocked an eye at Jess. “Could you do it?”
She lifted a shoulder. “I don’t know. I never thought about it until recently.”
“And what changed?”
A guilty smile warmed Jess’s wide, rosy mouth. “Well, I didn’t want to say anything yet. It’s all
pretty new, but I’m also starting to see someone.”
“Jess Thompkins, you sly thing! It’s not Luke, is it?”
She sputtered. “No! Ugh. I had one date with him, and I’ll never have another.”
“That bad, huh?”
“Oh, you can’t even imagine. Dakota has better manners.”
“That’s pretty bad if he’s not even as well-behaved as your dog. Well, do I know this new guy?”
“I think so. Austen Conrad?”
“I’ve seen him around. Wait, didn’t you dance with him at Morgan and Cody’s wedding?”
Her expression clouded. “Oh, right. I forgot about that. I had a few guys ask me to dance that night,
so it was kind of a blur.”
“Good grief, it must have been. He has that big place up High Line Road, right?”
“Yes, he bought it about three months ago. It’s just him, no family or anything, so he’s relied a lot on
the neighbors to learn the ropes and find good help. I met him again when his tractor broke down a
couple of weeks ago, and he brought it in to be fixed.”
“I didn’t know you worked on tractors, too.”
“I don’t, but he didn’t know who else to ask. I told him to take it to Bobby Eckhart. “
“So, what, was it love at first sight? Come on, details!”
She giggled. “To be honest, no. I wouldn’t have even thought of him again, except the next day he
came back with flowers to thank me for helping. Since I didn’t do anything but give him Bobby’s
name, I thought it was kind of weird. You know, like he was trying too hard. Don’t you hate it when
guys do that?”
“Uh. Yeah. Actually, no. No guy ever ‘tried too hard’ to get my attention. Must be nice.”
“Not really. I kind of brushed it off and forgot about him, but two days later, I got this sweet note in
my box. He said he was sorry if he made me uncomfortable, but he was new in town and just really
wanted to get to know me. Long story short, we’ve been out a couple of times now.”
“Wow. I’ve heard he’s quite a catch. Good for you!”
Jess’s cheeks pinked. “Thanks. It’s nothing serious yet, but we’ll see, right?”
“Right. Hey, maybe we can go out sometime, all four of us. What do you think?”
“Maybe. So, what are your plans for Christmas?”
I gathered up all my grooming supplies and backed out of Joy’s stall, carefully hanging the new
halter on the door. “Well, we always have a big family dinner and open our gifts on Christmas Eve.
Not sure why we started that tradition, but by now it seems weird to wait till Christmas morning.
Marshall and I are going over there tomorrow, and then I’ll go up to the ranch on Christmas for their
cookout.”
“Wow,” she breathed. “Christmas dinner with your parents is a big deal. He’s like part of the
family already!”
I made a face. “Not exactly. My mom terrifies him and he thinks my brother’s kids are criminals in
the making.”
Jess laughed. “I haven’t met the kids, but what’s so terrifying about your mom? She’s a
sweetheart!”
“Mom’s an angel, but she’s… intense, I guess. You know how she is—wants to help everybody,
even if they don’t want help. Sometimes she comes off as being kind of nosy and bossy.”
“Well, how about your dad? I always figured any man I got serious about needed to get along with
my dad.”
“They’re fine, if my mom leaves them alone long enough. I haven’t taken Marshall over there
much.”
Jess slid the barn door shut as we walked out into the snowy yard. “Why ever not? I thought you
two were pretty serious.”
“We are, it’s just…” I fumbled in my coat pocket for my car keys while I tried to think how to say
it. “He and my family aren’t much alike. Mom still thinks Marshall has to milk his cows every
morning and Dad’s idea of dressing like a cowboy is pressed generic blue jeans and a crisp white
polo. They’re just from different worlds.”
“But you’re part of both of them.”
I pushed my warm fuzzy cap a little farther back on my head to look at her better. “What’s your
point?”
“Oh, I don’t know. It just seems like you’ll need to make some peace between where you came
from and where you want to be.”
I shook my head as I opened my car door. “When I figure out what that means, I’ll come up with
some way to argue with you about it. Oh! I almost forgot your present.” I reached over into the
passenger seat to grab a shiny red bag. “Here you go. A little thank you for everything you’ve done to
help me.”
“Aw.” She opened the bag and pulled out the tissue, then gasped. “Kelli! How did you know?” She
dropped the bag in the driveway and held up the antique collector’s copy of Jane Austen’s Emma I’d
found for her. “This is my favorite! I’ve already worn out three hardcovers.”
“I know. I saw what was left of your old one.”
“Oh!” She clasped it to her chest. “Have you read it?”
“Not my type, but I’m glad you enjoy it.”
“You’d love it if you tried it. It’s so funny in parts!”
“Yeah,” I said, shrugging. “I don’t get it.”
She sighed and stroked the cover. “Maybe you’d like the movie. The end, when they both realize
the one they’re meant to love has been in front of them all along—oh, I can’t get enough of it! Thank
you, Kelli. This was so thoughtful.”
I hugged her before I got into my car. “You’re welcome. Merry Christmas.”
“You, too. Tell Marshall I said hi. First Christmas as a couple! I hope it’s magical.”
Magical? I chuckled and waved as I left the driveway. Magical. Jess read too many romance
novels. Real love was… well, it was gritty. Sometimes prickly. It was curious and uncertain, fierce in
the middle and messy around the edges. Yet, I didn’t know how to breathe without it.
I frowned over the steering wheel. Did I truly love Marshall Walker? More than a crush, more than
a fantasy. Did I love him enough to change my whole world for him? I knew I didn’t want to ever be
without him again, but could I really spend my life with him?
I didn’t have an honest answer yet. So, I cranked up the Christmas carols on the radio and hummed
all the way home. I’d have plenty of time to think through my relationship with Marshall Walker.
Chapter 17
“M arshall, come on in. Great to see you again. Merry Christmas!”
I smiled and doffed my hat at the door to Kelli’s folks’ house. “Merry Christmas to you,
Mr. Mason. Thank you.”
I caught the door he held open so Kelli could pass through in front of me. Not only was it the polite
thing to do, but it gave me another chance to admire her. She was wearing a long, fitted red sweater
over black leggings and fuzzy, mid-calf boots. Her black hair fell to her waist in soft waves and a
pair of sparkly Christmas earrings dangled at the curve of her neck. There was the faintest shimmer to
her cheeks today, and I wasn’t sure what she’d done differently with her eye makeup, but it was
dazzling. She looked good enough to eat.
I’d picked her up earlier than necessary on purpose, because I’d wanted to spend a few minutes
alone with her. Best decision I’d made all week. We hadn’t had much time together lately that didn’t
somehow involve livestock or work, and I’d been missing just holding her. I could still smell her
perfume clinging to my shirt, and her hair was not quite as perfectly curled as it had been when she’d
first greeted me at her door. She’d given me a new hat for Christmas, but nice as the hat was, it was
nothing to the way she’d felt in my arms when I’d thanked her for it. This woman took my breath
away.
Did she look like the ranch girl I used to think I’d end up with? With that sassy outfit and her silky
hair all styled fancy, I had to admit to myself that no; she didn’t. Didn’t act much like one, either. She
was spicy-sweet in a way that was all hers, and she never failed to make me stop and stare because
she stood out from everything else. I could live with different, especially since it was so delicious.
We came into the living room, and I looked around for a place to hang my new hat. I’d forgotten
that they didn’t have a rack by the front door. Kelli saw me and held out her hand. “I’ll take it. They
always lay out guests’ coats and hats in the master bedroom.”
I gave her a funny look as I pulled my arms from my sleeves. Who took other people’s dirty coats
and hats and put them on their bed? But they could do whatever they wanted, so long as I didn’t have
to carry my hat around the house.
“Marshall! Good to see you again. Still dating my sister, huh?”
I gave a tight smile and put my hand out as her brother came over. “If I’m lucky. Good to see you,
Kevin. Where are the kids?” I glanced around apprehensively.
“The wife’s giving them a bath. Dad and I just built a snowman with them and she didn’t want them
catching cold. Can’t be too careful, you know. Thirsty?”
I glanced through the window to the backyard and I could just barely make out about a two-foot-tall
snowman. It must have taken all of five minutes to roll. There was a mountain of snow boots, hats,
coats, and long overalls melting by the sliding glass door. How cold could the little rascals have
gotten? Maybe a little chill and some exercise was just what the varmints needed. But what really got
to me was the way Kevin talked about his wife. I’d be hanged and probably skinned if I ever called
Kelli “the wife,” like she was “the truck” or “the dog.” I guess I was old-fashioned.
I shook my head and followed Kevin and Kelli’s dad to the kitchen island. “Yeah, sure. How was
your drive?”
Kelli’s dad smiled and poured me a tall glass of eggnog. He really was a sweet man, and I
probably should have been trying to get to know him a little better. I nodded my thanks as Kevin
leaned against the counter and put on a haggard face. “Awful. Gets worse every year.”
I sipped my egg nog. “Roads were bad?”
“Bad! Just wait till you have kids. The squabbling, the diaper changes, and the music! They only
listen to one song on repeat.” He groaned and rolled his eyes. “I’m going to get a movie player before
our next road trip and make them keep their headphones on. They’ll probably scream half the night
tonight, too, because they aren’t in their own beds. We’ll have to end up with them between us again,
and I hope Brady leaves his pull-up on this time.”
“Uh-huh.” I swirled my egg nog and wondered where Kelli was. Her dad was just listening with a
mild smile on his face, so he was no help. I couldn’t even think of what to say to Kevin, except that he
and I had very different philosophies on raising kids. It was probably best that I didn’t say that. After
all, I didn’t have any of my own, so what did I really know? Kelli and I hadn’t even talked about…
“Sorry,” a voice said in my ear as her arms slipped around me from behind. “Jenny needed fresh
towels. You guys set with snacks?”
I clasped her hands at my chest in relief. It was sort of sweet, sort of scary that she felt free to
embrace me like that in front of her father and brother, but I wasn’t complaining. “I’m okay.”
“Good.” She pulled away and went to kiss her father’s cheek. “Daddy, I didn’t actually say hi to
you yet. Merry Christmas.”
He beamed at her. “Merry Christmas, honey. Goodness, I hardly see you anymore! I think it’s been
almost two weeks since you were over.”
“I know.” She nibbled those cherry lips that were still a little puffy from kissing in the truck. “I’ve
been spending a lot of time at the barn.”
“And with Marshall, I bet,” Kevin snickered as he popped some peanuts into his mouth.
“Well, yeah,” she admitted as she slid her dark eyes to me. I quirked my eyebrow suggestively, and
she blushed. “Guess I’ve had to divide my time up a little more.”
“So, come on, tell me about your horse. Let’s go sit down.” Her father waved us into the living
room, all except for Kevin, who had to peel off to catch a naked kid who was streaking down the hall.
That would keep him busy for a bit. I waited for Kelli to sit and eased onto the couch beside her.
“Well? How is Joy? Setting any new speed records yet?”
Kelli laughed modestly and shook her head. “Just taking my time with her. She’s doing great! She’s
a natural on the barrels.”
I felt her knee twitch against mine, and I hurried to pipe up. I’d be in hot water with my woman if I
didn’t. “Yes, yes, she sure is.”
“That’s wonderful! No trouble with her, then? I know how you love riding, but horses always
scared me. I don’t bounce like I used to. You haven’t come off her at all?”
I narrowed my eyes. “You didn’t tell him?”
Kelli gave me a swift “stop talking” glare and sucked in a breath. “Oh! It was no big deal. I just
slipped off when something surprised her.”
“No big deal?” I shouldn’t have butted in, but darn it, she took all this too lightly. “You had to go to
the hospital!”
Her father’s forehead creased. “The hospital? Were you hurt?”
“No! Okay, a little, but it was hardly worth a trip to the ER. Just a twisted ankle.”
“And a concussion,” I muttered.
“Really?” Mr. Mason’s face was pinched, and he looked at me. “I thought the horse was safe. I
remember when we talked at the auction, you said she was.”
I stifled a groan. “I didn’t say that. Besides, no horse is perfectly safe, and just because one
situation is manageable doesn’t mean a different one will be. They’re animals, not machines.”
“Well, I hope you would have said something if the horse was dangerous. Were you with her when
she came off?”
“Daddy—” Kelli sighed.
“No, really, honey, this is important. I know you’re an adult, but if Marshall here wants to date my
daughter, I’d like to know that he’s going to take care of you.”
Kelli buried her face in her hands. “Oh, Dad.”
But I was beyond embarrassment. I was getting offended. What right did he have to insinuate that
I’d just stand back and let Kelli get hurt? That I wasn’t the kind of guy who would watch out for his
lady like she was his treasure? “For the record,” I said, bristling, “your daughter has a mind of her
own. She listens to me when and if she feels like it, but you can be assured, sir, that I have done and I
will continue to do everything in my power to keep her safe.”
Kelli clapped her hand down on my knee, her lips thinned and her eyes glittering at both of us.
“And that’s enough of that. Daddy, there’s nothing to worry about, either from the horse or this
cowboy here, who’s usually a gentleman.” She shot me a look, but I wasn’t quite ready to back down.
I wasn’t going to let her dad cast doubts on my manhood when his own son was a blockheaded boob.
“It’s a fair question, honey,” her father replied. “Marshall, what exactly happened? Is this horse
from your ranch safe, or is she not?”
I ground my teeth. There was no clean way to answer this—I’d catch it from either end, no matter
what I said. If I claimed the horse was safe, Kelli would never let go of that wild thing. But if I said
she wasn’t, then her father would accuse our ranch of producing a bronc and blame me for letting his
daughter ride it. “The fault isn’t with the horse,” I growled.
Kelli stiffened beside me and slowly turned her head to glare at me. “Exactly where is the fault?”
she asked in a low voice.
“Oh, for crying out… are we really doing this?” I whispered back.
Her chin tightened in swift anger, and the faintest sheen appeared in her eyes. Her father’s gaze was
darting between the two of us, but by the grace of God, that was when her mother called us all in to
eat.
I couldn’t look at him without sending fiery glares that would make everyone stop and stare at
me. So, I didn’t look at Marshall at all.
It wasn’t that hard, since Mom had seated him beside me. No one really expected me to turn in my
chair so I could gaze into his eyes, but I think he put it together pretty quickly when he asked me to
pass the gravy and I just slid it his way without letting him touch my hand. Ugh, the arrogance of that
cowboy!
Did he really say that whatever problems Joy had—and they were huge, according to him—they
were caused by me? That was what he had implied, wasn’t it, when he said I didn’t listen to him?
And it must be me, because who else could it be? Certainly not the all-wonderful Walker Ranch
cowboys, who gave up on Joy because they couldn’t handle her! And definitely not him, because he
knew everything and had the ever-ready power of “I-told-you-so” whenever something went wrong!
I didn’t even taste my mom’s delicious roast duck, and I lost track of how many lumpia rolls I did
or didn’t eat. Marshall had given up on trying to get me to look his way, but he was making a very
obvious show of how much of my mom’s cooking he sampled this time. Some of it went untouched
after the first bite, and he kept having to refill his water glass because he was choking other things
down without tasting them. He sampled everything, though; I think he even had seconds of the pancit.
But if he thought that was going to soften me up after he’d just thrown down the worst insult possible,
he was going to be one disappointed cowboy on the ride home.
Mercifully, Dad let the subject of my horse drop, but I was still hot under the collar about the
whole thing. Who was he to think he could grill my boyfriend about needing to protect me? He’d
never, in all my life, tried to do something like that. I never even thought he was that type of dad!
And what gave Marshall the right to throw the challenge back like he was the one being insulted?
Men! I’d been taking care of myself for years now, and I certainly didn’t need them snorting and
bellowing at each other about whose job it was to look out for me. What was I, some pampered
china-doll? I was tough! I owned my own business, I’d bought my own house, I could change my own
tires and drive a horse trailer in the snow. And even Kaycee Whitmore would say I was doing a heck-
fire job of turning a failed ranch horse into a truly great barrel racer. I did not need to be coddled.
So why was Marshall always trying to do just that? It had to be because he thought I couldn’t hack
it. Wasn’t tough enough or experienced enough. What was it going to take to convince him that I was
worthy of his respect as an equal? Was I?
My thoughts started dark and kept spiraling as dinner went on. One stupid remark after another
came to mind—stuff that hardly registered when it happened but taken together, and in such a mood as
I was in, only made me bitter and furious. Times in my life, all the way back to grade school, when I
couldn’t hack it, or someone told me I couldn’t. Times when everyone—not just Marshall Walker—
had given the impression that somehow, I wasn’t enough.
I speared my vegetables and tried to enjoy them, but they tasted like sawdust. So, I poured gravy all
over them, and I heard my mom clicking her tongue. Next thing I knew, she’d be on me about my
eating habits and how I’d never hold on to my man unless I lost ten pounds. Oh, yes, I’d heard it all
before. Kelli Mason, cute, curvy Kelli Mason, the city girl who reached for the moon but was left
spitting out stardust. I pushed my plate away.
Marshall’s head snapped around. “You okay?”
I clenched my jaw. “Fine.”
“Fine,” he muttered. “Awesome. Wanna tell me about it?”
I curled my lip and shook my head. The last thing I needed was a whispered argument with my
boyfriend at my parent’s table over Christmas dinner. No way that would end well.
“So, Marshall,” Dad said from the head of the table, “I had a five-hundred-acre property come up
on the market this week. I meant to mention it to your dad because he was looking to expand a couple
of years ago. Is he still looking?”
Marshall pushed his own plate away and leaned back in his seat. “Always looking. Maybe not
always buying. We had a good year last year, though, so I’ll pass it on. Evan’s making most of the land
decisions these days.”
“Oh, really? How does it work with you guys? Do you divide up jobs on the ranch, or do you each
sort of have your own flat percentage?”
“Daddy,” I said, shaking my head. Even I hadn’t felt bold enough to nose into the Walker family’s
business. It had seemed… I don’t know, presumptuous.
“What? I’m just making conversation.” Dad folded his hands and raised his eyebrows at Marshall.
My dad might not have been a rugged cowboy, but he was sharp in business and finances. I could see
what he was doing. He was back to that thing of quizzing my boyfriend to see if he passed muster, and
I wanted to crawl under the table.
Marshall must have picked up on it, too, and he liked it less than I did. “Equal partnerships,” he
replied shortly. “We all pitch in wherever.”
Dad nodded. “I see. So, none of you can ever sell his share?”
Marshall started twisting his glass around on the table. “It’s possible, just not probable.” He didn’t
look up after he said that, and his Adam’s apple bobbed. Was he thinking of Dusty?
“Hey, Mom,” I said before Dad could come up with another humiliating question, “why don’t we
bring out the pie now?”
Marshall’s chest rose and fell with a sigh. He cast me a grateful look, then reached for his pocket
because his phone was buzzing. Well, that would give him an excuse not to talk for a minute. I
followed my mom into the kitchen.
“Your cowboy is grumpy,” she said as she pulled the whipped cream from the fridge.
“No, he’s not. Dad was asking rude questions.”
“That’s not rude. Just business. Why he doesn’t like talking?”
“Not everyone wants to share all their business, Mom. Just leave him be.”
She sighed something about how Marshall had better sweeten up because her daughter deserved
only the best, and in the next breath, she mentioned that my sweater was looking a little tight. I
stopped answering.
We gathered the dessert plates, and I stacked them up while she carried the pies into the dining
room. I’d counted out the forks and piled them loosely on the dishes, and I was just headed for the
next room when Marshall appeared in the doorway. He was already wearing his coat, and he had his
hat in one hand.
I stiffened. I wouldn’t let my parents pick on him, but I hadn’t forgiven him yet. “Yes?”
He held up his phone. “I can’t stay.”
My arms sagged, and the forks clattered to the floor. “What?”
“Another tree came down and knocked out the power.”
“So?” I shrugged. “Doesn’t that happen all the time?”
“Yeah.”
“And don’t you have a backup generator?”
“Two of them. One at the house and one at the barn.”
“So, wait it out and let the linemen fix it. That’s literally what they do.”
Marshall shook his head. “It’s not that. The well pump in the lower eighty will freeze if we don’t
keep the water flowing. The backup isn’t connected to the generator power. It’s just a hand pump.”
I went over to the counter and shoved the dessert plates onto it. “So, what? You have to go pump
that thing all night until they get the power on? That’s crazy.”
“No, not exactly.” He stuffed his phone back in his pocket and hooked his thumbs in his belt loops.
“But there are other things I need to check, too.”
“And aren’t there four other guys there, plus your ranch hands? Why do you have to be the one to
race off?”
His jaw hardened. “You don’t shirk your work off on someone else, sweetheart. Everyone pitches
in. That’s what living on a ranch is all about.”
“Don’t—” I poked him in the chest. “Don’t call me ‘sweetheart’ if you’re going to talk to me like
that.”
His mouth worked, and his nostrils flared a little. “Well, all right. Have it your way. You want a
ride home?”
I leaned a hand on the counter and blew my bangs out of my face. “You know what? Just go. I’ll be
fine.”
His brow lifted. “‘Fine,’ again, huh?”
“Yeah, that’s right. Go on, cowboy. Ride off in the sunset and go be the hero.”
His face darkened, and he put his hat on. He didn’t even say goodbye.
Chapter 18
“I told you we had this. You didn’t have to race up here.” Luke grunted as he heaved the last
shovel of snow out of the way of the pump house door.
“I’m not going to sit there eating when everyone else is working. That’s not how Dad raised us.
Here, take the top.” I passed him the heating element and stepped back over the snow pile to grab the
propane bottle. We’d run the heater in the well house for short spells, just enough to keep the air in
there above freezing so the pipes wouldn’t bust before the heat tape had power again. We’d have to
come down here every hour or so at these temperatures. Still, as unglamorous as it was to defrost the
well house and pump water by hand, it was a lot nicer having water down here this year than having
to truck it all the way from the main well every day.
“How was dinner?”
My stomach hadn’t stopped churning since I’d left Kelli’s folks’ house. It was a good thing I usually
kept some Tums on hand, because the heartburn I’d get after eating Luke’s stew had nothing on how I
was feeling now. “Fine.”
“How come you didn’t bring Kelli up to the house when you came home? Gonna be a late night,
and I was hoping she’d make us some of her coffee later.”
I took the heating element from Luke and twisted it on the top of the propane bottle. “Dinner wasn’t
over yet. She stayed there.”
“You left her? Dude. Bad move.”
I hit the igniter and stood back as the heater flared to life. “Why? She knows stuff happens. She
wanted to stay.”
Luke shrugged and started working the hand pump to refill the tank while I held the flashlight. “If
you say so. Bet you guys had better plans for this evening than this.”
I didn’t answer. Yeah, I’d had much better plans. For starters, I hadn’t given Kelli her gift yet. The
box had only come that morning and I’d been starting to worry that it wouldn’t make it. I almost gave
it to her when she gave me that new beaver felt hat, but there’d been something I wanted to say when
she opened the box. Running off to dinner—late—wasn’t the time.
And with the way she’d been looking at me when I left, I wasn’t sure when the right time would be.
Even if I could make it back to her place tonight, I doubted she’d be in the mood for talking. I’d call
her. Maybe slip over there in the morning so she could open her gift with the Christmas sunrise, like
normal people did.
“Well, that does it,” Luke huffed as he gave the handle one last pump. He took the flashlight from
me to shine it at the gauge. “Tank pressure looks good. Let’s just let this run a while. Want to see how
Dad and Dusty are doing with the barn water?”
“Sure.” We closed the door and hopped in the gator to buzz up the hill. We passed Evan on the way,
but all we could see of him were the lights of his ranch truck as the darkness closed in for the night.
Even over the noise of the gator’s engine, the snowy world seemed muffled and still. I never could
understand how something simple like a power outage could render the whole landscape somehow
more peaceful, but it always did in my mind. I guess I felt like a pioneer, battling the elements and
throwing in my all with my family, just so we could survive to look one more morning in the eye.
But at the moment, all I felt like looking in the eye was Kelli.
She’d been furious when I left. She might not have said as much, but she didn’t need to. I hadn’t
seen her that angry with me since last summer, when I’d left her at the White Pines booth at the County
Fair. It had been ranch business that time, too. I was supposed to help her for a few hours while
Morgan and Cody had an evening off, but Mitch texted and said a hay field sprinkler had broken and it
was flooding half an acre of alfalfa. Sure, Evan could have picked up the parts and gone to fix it, but
he was actually out of the house for a change and talking to some of his friends. He hardly ever did
that anymore, since he lost his wife. So, I left Kelli—we were already bickering anyway—and fixed
it myself.
Maybe that time was a mistake. Maybe leaving her tonight was a mistake, too. Still, it was the life,
and I couldn’t always avoid it. She said she understood that, but when it actually happened, she acted
like I was deliberately choosing the ranch over her. But what I needed her to understand was that the
ranch was for her, the way I saw it. At least, it was my future, and I’d started to feel like it was her
future, too.
It was after midnight before the power came back on. We all made the rounds once more, putting
away propane heaters and buttoning things up for the night, and we finally crashed about two.
Christmas morning came early, but we stumbled out of bed at the same time as usual. And like every
Christmas morning since we were little, a row of fat red stockings graced the fireplace mantel. Dad
always acted surprised and claimed it wasn’t him, no matter how old we were. We each got a new
pair of work gloves, new wool socks, and something special. Mine was a new coffee mug.
“That should come in handy,” Dad said with a sly gleam in his eye. “You’ll probably be using it all
the time before long.”
I set the mug gently back in the box. “Why do you say that?”
“Oh, no reason.”
“Dad.”
He cracked a grin. “I figure one of these days, you’ll have someone in your life who makes you the
best coffee this side of the divide every single morning. I’m kind of jealous.”
“Why are you in a hurry to push me down the aisle? What, did you make a bet or something?”
He frowned and examined the callouses on his palms. “Me? I wouldn’t do something like that. But
since we’re on the subject, you two seem pretty serious. Things going okay?”
I shrugged. “Fine.”
Dad puckered his mouth. “Well, don’t sit here like a bump on a log. I imagine you need to get out to
see your gal this morning.”
“I guess so. I’ll head over there after I finish chores.”
Dad waved. “I’ll take over for you today. It’s Christmas. She’s coming for our cookout tonight,
isn’t she?”
“Said she was. I’ll bring her back with me. Merry Christmas, Dad, and thanks.”
He clapped me on the shoulder. “Merry Christmas, son. Don’t hurry back.”
I went out to my truck and set the candy-cane-wrapped box on the passenger seat. I’d been in a rush
to get out of the house before Dusty could rope me into helping cook breakfast, but I realized when I
started the engine that I hadn’t called Kelli yet.
Well, that was fine. It was Christmas morning, and she was my girlfriend, right? I could go over
without calling first, even if she wasn’t happy with me last night. There was more to us than fighting.
We’d proved that, and now it was time for me to make it up to her.
I peeked through the blinds when I saw the flash of headlights turning into my driveway.
Marshall. Even earlier than I’d expected. He must have known how low he was in my books right
about then. I let the blinds snap together and returned to rinsing my cup in the sink. A moment later, he
knocked.
I didn’t hurry to the front door, but I didn’t stall, either. Part of me wanted to hear his gushing
apology. How wrong he’d been to say those things at dinner last night, then rush off because the ranch
was more important. I wanted to know that he hadn’t meant to slice my heart out right in front of my
family, then leave me at their mercy for the rest of a holiday dinner where he was supposed to be my
champion. I needed to know where I ranked in his priorities.
But another part of me just wanted to tell him off until his ears sizzled.
He was standing on the porch, wearing the denim coat I never could get enough of, the new cream-
colored hat I’d given him, and a smile that looked yummier than sugar plums. He held a red-and-
white-wrapped box wedged against his hip, and he tipped that hat with a look that would have
caramelized my insides if I hadn’t woken up thinking dirty words about him.
“Merry Christmas, baby.”
I arched a brow and pushed the screen door open so he could come all the way in, then stood back
with my arms crossed and my head tilted.
He was already taking his hat off, but he stopped mid-way. “Oh. So, this is how it’s going to be.”
“If you mean that I’m going to need some explanations before I melt in your arms and kiss you like
we’re in some cheesy movie, then yes.”
He finished removing his hat and dropped it on the back of my old rocking chair. “Look, I told you
what happened. Sorry about the timing, but things don’t stop just because we land on a certain number
on the calendar.”
“I know that. It’s the way you left.”
“I offered to give you a ride home. Heck, you could have even come up with me if you wanted to
help. It’s not like it’s fun, but—”
“Yeah, right. What exactly would you have let me do?”
His forehead dropped into a scowl. “Well, it’s not rocket science. Hold the flashlight, set the
heaters. Break ice in the troughs, make sure the generators are all running. There’s always room for
one more pair of hands.”
“Which means they probably could have made do with one less.” I tipped my chin until my hair
slid behind my ears and stared him down.
He set the gift box down on my entry table and headed for the living room. “Well, sure, but…” He
dropped onto the sofa, leaning his forearms on his thighs, and stared at my carpet. “It just ain’t right.
That’s all. You don’t sit on your thumbs while everyone else is working.”
My eyes slid to the empty cushion beside him, but I didn’t sit. “Is that what your brothers said?
Your dad?”
He squirmed. “Yes. Well… no. Usually.”
“English, please.”
Marshall sighed and raked his hands through his hair, and I almost melted right then. Ooh, but I
wanted to nestle up to his chest and play with his hair, bury my face in his neck and have him hold me!
He even glanced at the seat next to him like he wanted that, too, but when I didn’t move, he set his
teeth and frowned at the floor again.
“Okay, it’s true. Last night, they told me I didn’t need to race home. We’d have been checking things
most of the night anyway, so it wasn’t an emergency, but it was probably easier to set the heaters with
another person there.”
“And you saw your chance to ditch my mom’s dinner.”
His head snapped up. “What?”
“Admit it. You don’t like my family.”
He blinked like a raccoon caught in the porch light. “I don’t dislike them.”
“That’s not true. I saw you rolling your eyes and holding your breath for half the evening. You can’t
stand Kevin and his family.”
His gaze hardened. “Do you like spending time with them? You said yourself his kids were
hellions, and your brother’s so shallow that if he was a pothole, nobody’d even trip over him.”
“That doesn’t give you the right to insult him. He’s my twin brother!”
“You got all the brains of the gene pool,” he said with a sour face.
If I hadn’t been so hot under the collar, I might have laughed. But I wasn’t laughing now. I wanted
to scorch him.
He had been tapping his fingers together and avoiding my face, but then he looked up and went
pale. “Hey, I didn’t mean it that way.”
I bared my teeth and stalked toward him. “Exactly how did you mean it? I suppose my mom is just
a tad too different for you. She cooks more than steaks and potatoes, and she’s been around the world.
And my dad can speak in complete sentences and he has one of those ‘useless’ college degrees, so
obviously, he’s wasted his life on stuff that doesn’t matter!”
He put out a hand. “Kelli, come on. Your folks are good people and I like them just fine. You’re
making way too big of a deal—”
“Am I? Oh! I’m sorry. When should it be a big deal that my boyfriend and my dad almost come to
blows over whose job it is to cluck over me like a mother hen? I’ve never been so humiliated!”
He stiffened. “Well, I’m sorry, sweetheart, but I’ll tell you straight. That was the most respect I’ve
had for your dad since I met him. He’s clueless about half the stuff he threw at me, but at least he’s
trying to make sure you’re okay.”
That pricked a deep nerve, and I didn’t even know which argument to take down first. I just knee-
jerked to the first thing that came to mind. “I don’t need him to ‘make sure I’m okay,’! I don’t need
him fussing over my choices, and I don’t need you poking him back like a couple of dogs fighting over
a bone! I’m twenty-six years old, for crying out loud!”
“It wouldn’t matter if you were six or fifty-six. He’s your dad. That’s his job, if he’s any kind of
man. I hope to heaven I’ll do the same thing if I ever have a daughter, but I’ll tell you one thing. I sure
as heck won’t sit there and let him test me like that without showing some spine. That’s what he was
looking for and I gave it to him.”
I smacked my face with both hands and let out a frustrated growl. “You guys are so… I’m my own
person! And I think I’ve done pretty well so far, but you act like I can’t fry bacon without you standing
there to make sure I do it right! When am I going to be good enough for you, Marshall Walker?”
He shot to his feet. “Where did you get a crazy idea like that?”
“Oh, don’t you play innocent this time. It’s behind every conversation we’ve ever had, all the way
back to prom night. Fine! I didn’t grow up punching cows and starting colts. I didn’t have to feed
stock for two hours every morning before school started, and I didn’t learn to change a tire or fix a
broken pipe or doctor a sick cow when I was six. But you know what I did learn?”
His chin was trembling, and his chest muscles flexed under his shirt in growing anger. “Sass. You
got plenty of that.”
I clenched my fists. Oh, how I wanted to belt him for that one! But I wouldn’t give him the
satisfaction of seeing me lose my temper. I wasn’t a kid anymore.
“I learned how to fight for what I want,” I hissed through my teeth. “I learned to put the work in and
take the risks, stand on my own two feet and not to depend on anybody to do it for me. Not my dad,
and definitely not you!”
Marshall’s cheek twitched, and his brown eyes glittered to black. “Fine. You like going it on your
own? Go ahead. You don’t need anybody? That’s alright with me. Reckon you don’t want to come
back with me, then. Merry stinking Christmas.”
He spun on his heel and marched toward the door. He reached for the hat he’d hooked over my
rocking chair, then his hand stopped and he clenched a fist. My stomach dropped into my feet as I
watched him yank the door open.
“See you around,” he growled. Then he was gone.
Chapter 19
H er car was parked by the Coffee Wagon the next morning. That would explain why she wasn’t
home and wasn’t picking up the phone. Kelli worked like a machine, and she didn’t stop for
anybody. Not even me.
I shouldn’t have stomped out like that, and on Christmas, too. I knew it by the time I shoved the key
in my truck ignition, but I was too amped up to turn myself around and apologize. No, like a moron, I
drove home and had to put up with everyone’s questions about where Kelli was. I didn’t offer much.
Just said she didn’t feel like coming after all.
I think Morgan knew. She gave me one or two somber looks but didn’t say anything. I was glad,
because I already felt like everyone was staring at me. It was a miserable way to spend Christmas
Day. My steak didn’t even taste good. So, here I was, hat in hand.
I was three cars back in line at the Coffee Wagon and drumming my thumbs on the steering wheel.
How was I supposed to start? “Morning, sweetheart. Sorry for yesterday. How about a ride?” No, she
probably wouldn’t go for that. Not till after she was done working. I let the truck roll forward as the
cars in front of me moved up one space.
I could go talk to her dad. She’d like that. Maybe. Actually, I’d probably get farther by taking
flowers to her mom. I rubbed my eyes and groaned. Knowing Kelli’s mom as well as I did—which,
admittedly, was not all that well—that would amount to a declaration that I planned on marrying her
daughter.
Well, didn’t I?
I sure didn’t plan on letting her get away again, but if we were going to talk marriage, I figured it
should start with Kelli and me, not her parents. I didn’t need her mom hitting me up for wedding plans
or her dad lecturing me about keeping her off of wild horses long enough to give him some decent
grandkids.
But we weren’t ready for that kind of talk yet. For now, I just wanted to kiss and make up and get
back to where things were a couple of days ago. My truck inched forward another space.
Her head popped out of the window right then, and I saw the quick arch of her eyebrow when she
locked gazes with me through the windshield. That was not her come-hither look. My palms started to
sweat.
The cars in front of me seemed to take forever. I don’t know if it was me just sitting on pins and
needles or if Kelli had slowed down her usual breakneck pace at the espresso machine, but the
minutes dragged by like molasses. By the time I rolled up to the window, my stomach felt like it was
full of barbed wire.
She leaned out and puckered her lips. “What’ll it be, cowboy?”
My hackles went up. Something about her tone was just a bit too close to the old days, when she
held me in contempt for being… well, me, I guess. I swallowed the knot in my throat.
“Mornin’ sw…” I closed my mouth when her cheek twitched. “Uh. Hi.”
“You forgot your hat.”
My hand went automatically to my passenger seat, where my old brown hat was perched. Then I
realized what she was talking about. I put my truck in park. “Didn’t think I should take it. Seemed like
maybe you’d regret giving it to me.”
Her eyes narrowed. “That depends. Did you come to pick up the box you left on my table?”
“Did you open it?”
“Nope.”
Something inside my ribs collapsed. “I wish you would have.”
“Why? So, you can show me up? What is it, a helmet? A pull-string inflatable body suit in case I
fall?”
I clamped down on my lower lip and stared forward. “Why do you always assume I’m out to defeat
you?”
She rested her chin on her fist and shrugged. “Call it experience. Why’d you leave me yesterday?
Again? ”
I swung my head around to look her full in the eyes. “Didn’t look like you wanted to come with
me.”
Kelli’s lips quivered, and she sniffed with a hint of disdain. “I didn’t.”
“Well, then, what are you mad about?”
She pushed away from the window with a toss of her hand. “If you don’t know, I’m not going to
explain.”
“What kind of sense does that make?” I called at her back. “How am I supposed to know what’s
bugging you if you don’t say it?”
“Because you should! I thought we knew each other by now.”
What was that supposed to mean? I craned my neck to see inside the little coffee hut, but she’d
disappeared in the back. “Well, I guess I’m ignorant because I don’t have a clue.”
Boxes and containers were slamming around inside, and she didn’t answer right away.
“Kelli! Come on, talk to me.”
She came back to the window. “Are you going to order something?”
I hissed in exasperation and tried to think of the most complicated drink I could imagine—anything
to make her stay there for a minute or two. Behind me, a horn honked, and my brain fritzed.
“Give me a triple shot,” I mumbled.
She rolled her eyes and reached for a cup.
“With whip,” I added. “And a cherry.”
She didn’t crack a smile like I’d hoped she would. She didn’t even look up again. Just pressed the
coffee grounds and started up her espresso machine, careful not to let her eyes stray in my direction.
This… this could be serious. I tugged at my collar. “So, hey. I was wondering if we could… What
time do you get off today?”
She returned a stony look as she dumped out the used coffee grounds. “I own the place, Marshall. I
don’t ‘get off.’”
“What time are you free?” I asked through gritted teeth. “I’d really like to talk to you.”
Her brow lifted serenely, and she upended a can of whip. “I’m not sure. Things can come up at the
last minute, you know.”
My head dropped back onto the seat. “Kelli, I said I was sorry. What do you want me to do, go
back in time? Tell the guys to stop the weather when I’m out with you?”
The guy behind me honked again, and I turned in annoyance to wave him off. He didn’t like that and
made a rude gesture back. I bit off the angry retort I’d have liked to make and looked at Kelli. She
was just putting the lid on my drink and she came up to the window; her face as blank as someone
with her vibrant features could make it.
“That will be $5.50.”
My mouth dropped open. She was charging me? I hadn’t really even come for coffee. I just wanted
her. Numbly, I dug in my pocket. “I don’t believe this.”
“Business is business, Marshall Walker. You know how it is. Personal feelings aside, I gotta keep
things running.”
“Business! So now you’re jealous of the ranch? My family? What?” I shoved the money in her
hand.
She pushed her lip out and casually counted out six ones. “Jealous? No.” She slammed the register
closed. “But it’s nice to know where I stand. Thanks for the tip. Have a nice day.”
I blinked like a stupid cow. She held my cup out the window, and I couldn’t make my hand reach
for it until the car behind me honked again. Twice.
“Kelli…”
“Move the line along, please.”
I snarled and jerked the gearshift. “Whatever. I don’t care.” I sped out to the highway, and I never
did drink that overpriced coffee.
I couldn’t even remember the last time I ugly cried over anything. It just wasn’t me. I was the
tough girl, the one who walloped everyone into cheerfulness, whether or not they felt like it. I was
the girl who could grin through a tax audit and find the bright side of a traffic jam. I was the one who
showed up when other people were in the dumps and made them smile through their tears. I was the
one…
The one who was presently curled up in her bathtub, fully dressed, with her knees pulled up to her
chin, and sobbing until she couldn’t breathe. I was that girl today. And yesterday. And tomorrow
wasn’t looking good, either.
The thing I feared the most had happened. I’d lost the last crumbs of my heart to Marshall Walker
and there was no getting them back. But he didn't seem to want them.
A few days ago, I’d been trying to put into words what I wanted and how I felt about our future, and
now I could write it all out and set it to music. He was sun and light and air to me. He was the earth
beneath my feet and the sky I wanted to reach for, and he was every dream, every hope and every
good thing that could ever come into my life. And he’d chosen the ranch over me.
Why was I so surprised? It was all he’d ever wanted, the cowboy life his family had built. He saw
the work as his way of proving his worth to the world, and if you couldn’t hack it, weren’t part of it…
well, you couldn’t be part of him. I leaned my head on my arms and snort-sniffed, long and hideously.
Then I gagged and sobbed all over again.
Why would Marshall want me, anyway? I wasn’t cut out for that type of living. I liked lazy bubble
baths by candlelight and corny romance movies over giant bowls of popcorn, and I drove an old
Civic instead of a pickup. And my horse, the inspiration that had made me think I could relive my
childhood fantasies… well, that was a pipe dream, wasn’t it? Joy didn’t fit in on the ranch, and
neither could I.
I’m not sure why it seemed like a good idea, but I plugged the drain and reached for the water
faucet. A bubble bath sounded great right about now, fully clothed or not. If I got my jeans soaked, I’d
just have an excuse for how pathetic I felt.
“Might want to rethink that.”
I picked up my head just in time for Morgan’s arms to slide around me. She seated herself on the
edge of the tub and pulled me into her side, then just stroked my sweaty hair, as only a hard-core
heart-sister could.
I choked on a sob. “How did you get in here?”
“Spare key. I was watering your house plants last year, remember?”
I sniffed and swiped at my cheek. “Oh, yeah. You heard?”
“No, but it doesn’t take a genius to figure it out. Marshall looked like he was going to vomit all day
yesterday.”
“He did?”
Morgan sighed and leaned over to reach me a tissue from the counter. “Green to the gills. How are
you?”
“Worse.”
“Yeah? Anything you want to talk about?”
I shook my head and blew my nose. Hard. “I think I need another tissue.”
She passed me the whole box. “Jess is worried about you. She called me because you didn’t show
up to feed Joy this evening.”
I smacked my face. “Oh, cripes. Did she feed?”
“Yeah.”
I pulled out a couple more tissues and fiddled with them, wadding them and then shaking them out
to crinkle them all over again. “It’s over, Morgan.”
“Hey, you don’t know that. Every couple has growing pains. One argument doesn’t mean—”
“I wasn’t talking about Marshall and me. Well, maybe that, too. I don’t know.”
She sank to the floor and framed her arms on the side of the tub so she could look me in the eye.
“What’s going on?”
I sucked my upper lip between my teeth, trying to shut down the squeaky sob growing in my throat.
“I can’t do this. I never should’ve tried. It’s not who I am.”
She squinted. “What exactly are you talking about?”
I closed my eyes and swallowed and let the awful confession out. “Everything. Marshall, Joy,
faking my way into the cowgirl life and thinking I could become something I’ll never be.”
“Faking! You listen to me, Kelli Mason. There’s no one-size-fits-all. There isn’t a formula. All you
need is the guts to take the chances and see things through, and I don’t know anyone with more guts
and daring than you have.”
I rested my cheek on my shoulder. “It doesn’t matter. None of it matters without him.”
“What makes you think you’ve lost him?”
I blew my nose and snorted a bitter laugh. “He’d be crazy to stay. I let my temper get the better of
me again. I always blow my top with him! He’s so… so impossible and so annoying and yet so
much…” I sighed and tossed the tissue at the trash can. I missed.
“So much what?”
“He’s everything I ever wanted—just him. I don’t even care about the ranch or the horses or how
mouth-watering he looks in a cowboy hat. He could wear a suit or a mechanic’s uniform or nothing,
for all it matters.”
Morgan raised a brow. “That could be interesting.”
“You know what I mean. He’s surly and overprotective and a workaholic, but he’s also just…
good.” I sighed miserably. “He always puts everyone else before himself and he’s always there when
someone needs him. He’s gentle and thoughtful and the best darned kisser this side of the Rockies,
and… and he was all mine for a little while.” I buried my face in my arms.
“Kel, I think he still is.”
I shook my head against my elbows. “It’s no good, Morgan. We’re too different. He’ll always
choose the ranch, and if I keep trying to shoe-horn myself into his life, we’ll both just get hurt more.”
“All right, enough.” Morgan got to her feet and extended a hand. “Get up. You’re terrible at this
whole self-pity thing.”
I let her tug my arm and slowly pulled my feet under me. “Where are we going?”
“To the kitchen. I’m fixing you dinner.”
“Wouldn’t it be safer if I just ordered a pizza?”
Morgan fisted a hand at her hip. “Hey! I’ve been learning. Cody’s still alive.”
I sagged and admitted defeat. She wasn’t going away unless I got up, so I could just fake a smile till
she left. Then I’d curl up in bed with a whole box full of chocolates and the remote control.
I waited forlornly in the dining room while Morgan rummaged in my fridge. She found the
Christmas Eve leftovers from my mom’s house and held them up with a questioning look. I shook my
head. I didn’t want to be reminded of that meal. She shrugged and dumped them out.
“Looks like your options are chicken fajitas or spaghetti. I’m good at both of those.”
“Just… don’t worry about it. I’m not hungry, anyway.” I sat down, and my stomach growled.
“Uh-huh. Let’s see… bag of lettuce, frozen chicken, some kind of cheese…” She sniffed it and
wrinkled her nose.
“Goat cheese. My mom gets it at this specialty imported foods place when she goes to the city.”
“Okay.” She put it back and parted the condiments. “Oh. Did you forget about these steaks in the
back? They’re… um. Defrosted. A while ago.”
I groaned. “I was going to have Marshall over after dinner at my mom’s. You know him, meat and
potatoes guy, and that’s not how my mom cooks. I thought he’d appreciate the gesture. I meant it to be
a surprise.” The tears stung again.
Morgan caught my eye and closed the fridge. “Hey.” She came over and put her arm around me.
I wiped the waterfall from my cheeks. I didn’t want her pity. I didn’t want anyone to have to pick
me up when I was down, and I didn’t want it to be said that Kelli Mason was just as big of a sucker
as the next person.
“Go talk to him.”
I shook my head. “We already tried that. We stink at it.” I sniffed and attempted a smile. “You know
us. Oil and water.”
She gave me a half-hearted squeeze. “More like baking soda and vinegar.”
“Gasoline and matches.”
Morgan laughed. “Okay, you win. But are you sure? It sounds like you guys have carried this torch
for years, and you can’t just wish that away. Hard as you try, Marshall Walker will always be that guy
for you. Don’t you want to give it another shot?”
I swallowed. “Yes. I’ll be honest. I want him like I want air. But it won’t work any better than it
ever has. Face it, Morgan. We just can’t bat for the same team.”
Chapter 20
N o matter how many times I slammed the tractor bucket down against the frozen pasture, I
couldn’t break the world. I sure wanted to keep trying to, though.
Why did she have to be so blasted stubborn? And why had I gone and hung my heart on her like an
idiot? All last summer, I’d kept myself back. I knew we’d end up here. I’d known for years why she
and I didn’t make sense, why we would only blow up all over each other. We were too much of the
same bull-headed characters but with opposite personalities. It was stupid to think we could get
along, and I’d left well enough alone for ten years.
Then she went and did something so crazy that I couldn’t help remembering why I’d fallen crown
over keister for her back in high school. Why there was only Kelli Mason for me, and why there
would only ever be her. She was hot-headed, she was contrary, she wouldn’t take no for an answer
and she would go to the mats for just about everything. And I loved her for it.
I jerked the lever of the tractor bucket and backed up, then rammed the pile again. It was just loose
hay; what was left of a round bale we’d dropped for the mama cows before we moved them back to
their regular field. Moving the hay should be an easy task with the tractor—ten minutes, tops—but I
couldn’t seem to do anything right today. And I was getting madder by the second.
How dare she accuse me of thinking she wasn’t “good enough” for me? What was that even
supposed to mean? Sure, she chafed a bit when I told her to take it easy, and I could sort of understand
that, but that wasn’t any kind of criticism of her. Why did she have to take everything so darned
personally?
I hit the throttle and gave the hay pile a last shove with the forked tractor bucket. This time I got
most of it to hold together, so I raised the bucket and turned for the next field to dump it. One more
trip and it was as done as it was going to get. I pulled into the barnyard and Evan was leaning on the
fence, chewing on a bit of straw.
“You’re fixing that thing next time it breaks.”
I parked it and hopped down. “If it can’t handle scraping some snow, maybe we should get a
different tractor.”
“You looked like you were trying to split logs, not rake hay. You know the hydraulics won’t handle
that kind of abuse, to say nothing for my attachments.”
I stopped and adjusted my hat, then looked my oldest brother in the eye. “Sorry.”
“I’m not looking for an apology. What’s going on with you? You’ve been mad as a hornet for three
or four days.”
I looked down and shook my head.
“Oh. Like that, is it?” Evan turned his side against the fence, shifting his gaze into the mountains.
“Well? You probably don’t want to talk about it.”
“How’d you guess?”
“I’m a genius. Thought you knew that.”
I rolled my eyes. “Okay, but you’re not exactly Mr. Talkative, either.”
He grunted. “Lots of things just aren’t worth saying. But some things are. So, what? Are you guys
over?”
“I have no idea. I don’t think so. Maybe. We just had a fight, that’s all.”
“What was it about?”
I stared at him. “Why do you want to know?”
“Because if it’s over petty stuff…”
“Yeah, yeah. We could work it out. I don’t need a lecture on compromising.”
“Actually, that’s not what I was about to say. People who fight over stupid stuff have their sights set
way too low.”
I snorted. “So, we should be high achievers with our fights? Hey, why not reach for the stars,
right?”
He shifted the straw around in his mouth. “If you want the kind of relationship that lasts, maybe.
Maybe you need to duke it out over the important stuff.”
I was shaking my head. “Wow. That makes zero sense.”
“Were you fighting over money? What to eat for dinner or how to spend a Friday night?”
“Not even close.”
“So, it was something more personal. Christmas? Family?”
“Getting warmer.”
“Oh. Hmm.” Evan rubbed his chin. “Seems a bit early for that. You guys were dating what, about
two months?”
“Just over one, but you could say we’ve been dancing around it since we were kids. And what do
you mean, ‘seems a bit early?’ Early for what?”
“Well, unless I miss my guess, you both loved becoming one new thing together, but then you
realized you’re still you. And you is a mess.”
“Thanks a lot.”
He shrugged. “Here to help.” He threw the bit of straw down on the ground and walked off.
“Wait up. What did you mean by that?”
Evan turned back and squinted against the low afternoon sun. “No two people can just drop who
they are and dive in headfirst. You’ve got to grind off the sharp edges a bit, rub along some until you
find how to fit together.”
“But we did… fit. We get along great!”
“When you’re getting along. How often is that?”
I shrugged. “Depends on the day.”
Evan snorted. “You’re too stubborn, that’s what it is. Both of you.”
I kicked a little snow with the toe of my boot. That was exactly what Cody had told me. I didn’t
think I was all that stubborn. I didn’t pick fights for the sheer joy of fighting. There was just a right
and a wrong way to do most things, and I didn’t like being wrong. And Kelli liked her independence.
That was one of the things I loved about her.
“Well… how long does that take? The ‘rubbing along’ part. Are we gonna just click after like a
year?”
He cocked an eyebrow. “Not exactly how it works.”
“Fine, then. How does it work? I’ve got to figure something out, or I’m going to bust. Like
yesterday.”
Evan got this funny smile—the one he sometimes got when thinking of Anne. His gaze drifted to the
mountains and his chin twitched a few times, like he was trying to control his voice when it came out.
“You just gotta figure out what’s important to you. It’s that simple and that difficult.” He sniffed and
cleared his throat, then pushed away from the fence. “I’m off to feed the yearlings.”
I sagged and let my shoulders fall against the fence as I watched him go. Figure out what’s
important, he said. Well, heck, I knew that. Easy. Kelli was important to me. My family was
important, and the ranch that kept us all fed. I scrunched my face as I thought more.
My horse. I liked my horse, and my truck, too. The guys—Mitch and Brandon and the rest of the
hands that were like extended family. Good weather, that was important, and water to irrigate the
fields and keeping things fixed so we could do our jobs. And kissing… kissing was very important, if
it was with Kelli.
Was I supposed to pick one? Give something up to get something better? Or could it all fit in my
life?
I blew out a heavy breath and wandered toward the barn. Cody would be in there finishing up with
his futurity horses for the day. I could talk to him, maybe get some more ideas. I slid the door open,
but the minute my eyes hit the golden sand inside, where it had all started over for us, my heart
slammed into my ribs.
And just like that, she was in my arms again, her cheeks deliciously cold and her lips warm against
mine. She was pushing my hat off my head and drawing me closer, her hands locking into my coat
collar and her body pressed into me. I don’t remember thinking of anything else when I was holding
