her—just how right it was, and how long I’d wanted her.
“What’s up?”
I blinked at Cody’s voice, and my vision came back into focus. He was just passing his colt off to
Emily to unsaddle and groom, and he started toward me. “Need something?”
I couldn’t make words. Half a second ago, Kelli had been almost there with me, so vividly that I
could practically feel her curves in the ache of my fingers. I’d never be right until I could hold her for
real.
Cody stopped and scanned my face. “Hey, you don’t look good. You ok?”
I pressed my mouth closed. “Not really.”
He put a hand on my shoulder. “Let’s go for a drive.”
“S ee you Saturday, Kelli!” Ashley, one of my teen employees, hung up her apron and jingled
her car keys.
I smiled as I closed the till we’d just balanced. “I won’t be in on Saturday. I’m volunteering up at
the new White Pines facility all day.”
“Wow, on New Year’s Eve?”
“Yeah. It was the only day everyone could get off work to come. I’m sure Morgan will make it
something special, regardless.”
“Are they almost ready to open? I’ve heard so much about it!”
“Not quite, but after the work party, they’ll be a lot closer. You have a good weekend. I probably
won’t see you until Monday.”
Ashley reached for the door. “Then I guess it’s Happy New Year, too.”
I looked down. I didn’t want to think about another holiday right now, especially one that
traditionally involved a kiss with that one special person who makes your world go round. This year,
I’d been hoping to do just that—wrap up my year by wrapping up in my cowboy’s arms—but things
weren’t looking good. Still, I forced a smile for Ashley. “Right. Happy New Year.”
She waved at me through the window as she walked out into the parking lot. I waved back, then
bent down to count my stock of cups. I’d have to bring some more over from the house before the
weekend, but I didn’t need to make another order yet. The syrups looked good, except the peppermint.
Everyone had been craving it over Christmas. Plenty of chai tea on hand. Lids, straws, chocolate-
covered coffee beans…
Someone knocked on my window and I jerked upright, slamming the back of my head on the shelf.
“Ow…”
Marshall was outside, leaning up to the glass with a worried look on his face when I popped up,
holding my scalp. “Sorry,” came his muffled voice. “I didn’t mean to scare you.”
I slid the window open. “It’s alright, I enjoy pain.”
He winced. “I don’t.” He stuck his hands in the pockets of his jacket and his eyes dropped to the
menu painted on the front of my cart. “How are you? Besides the head.”
Honestly? I still wanted to deck him, but once I had him down, I’d pin him to the ground and kiss
him till I was dizzy and he ran out of air. “I’ve been better,” was all I managed.
His eyes flickered up to mine. “Me too. We never got a chance to talk about stuff.”
I leaned on the counter and blew the hair out of my eyes. “I guess we didn’t.”
He rubbed the back of his neck, the way he always did when he was searching for something to say.
“I probably should have called.”
“Probably.” Although, my phone worked just fine, too.
“Yeah. I just…” He scuffled his boots on the pavement. “Figured we do better face to face. Less
chance for misunderstandings.”
I was idly stacking and re-stacking the cups beside the register. It kept my eyes off him, because if I
looked too long and hard, I wasn’t sure what I’d do. Probably dive out the window into his arms.
“You really think that’s going to make a difference?”
“I was hoping.”
Yeah. I’d been hoping that, too, but maybe he hadn’t thought things through the way I had. “We’ll
just fight again.”
“We don’t have to.”
I rolled my neck and stared up at the roof of the little hut. “Marshall, we’ve tried this, and it never
works.”
He stepped closer to the window. “Maybe not, but we’re getting better. Aren’t we? You…” His
gaze softened, and he attempted a smile. “You never even slapped me this time.”
My lips twitched, but I looked away. I thought of a hundred little smart-aleck retorts, and sassy
comments to make him smile, but I clamped my teeth down on them. I knew what made sense, how I
needed to end things before we both got hurt so bad, we wouldn’t even be able to live in the same
town. But he wasn’t making this easy.
He rested his hand on the credit card ledge. “There was something I never got to tell you.”
I froze and let my gaze drift to him. “What?”
Hopeful brown eyes locked with mine, and his throat bobbed. “You said before… Can you come
out of there?”
Should I? Because if I did, I’d just crumble. I’d jump right into whatever he asked because I’d
never learned what caution was. I was crazy about him, but we’d wind up hurting each other more,
and maybe next time, we couldn’t even be friends with the same people when it all blew over.
But if I didn’t go out there, I might be forever letting go of what I wanted more than anything—a
chance to see if I was the one who could love him like he should be loved.
“I… uh… I don’t know, Marshall. Seems like…”
I broke off. A car was pulling in behind him. We both watched as the passenger door opened and
Kaycee Whitmore stepped out. She raised her sunglasses, and Marshall backed away from the
window.
“Looks like a line. Are you doing walk-up service instead of cars now?”
I put on a smile. “Cars, two feet or four legs. If you can get to the window, I’ll pour the coffee.”
Kaycee laughed. “I’m glad I caught you. Ben’s driving me to the airport in Boise this afternoon, and
I was hoping for some caffeine to keep me fueled up. You’re not closed, are you?”
“I was just about to lock up, but I have cold brew on hand. What would you like?”
“Something to keep me awake! Mocha frap?”
“You got it.” I reached into the fridge for the coffee and got my blender back out. “Sorry to hear
you’re leaving so soon, but it was nice seeing you again.”
“Really nice,” she agreed. “And Marshall, I’m glad to hear your family’s doing well. Your dad was
always a good friend to me. Blake sold me my very first horse, did you know that? You tell him to
take care of himself.”
I was bent over the blender, pouring cream and cold brew, but I heard Marshall’s casual grunt. “I’ll
tell him. I think he’s sold horses to just about everyone within a hundred miles at some point.”
“Present company included,” she said, tilting her head toward me. “Kelli, are you going to race
your mare next season? You should. She’ll be ready.”
I gathered my bottles back up for the fridge and stopped at the window, my lower lip caught in my
teeth. Joy had done great when we practiced with Kaycee, but as high as I’d been afterward, it didn’t
settle the ache inside me. Shouldn’t that have blown the top off the glass ceiling I was always trying
to bust?
But a week later, I could truly say that it hadn’t. I didn’t feel any different, I didn’t feel like the
world was any wider or my future any brighter because a top professional had declared my horse and
me to be a great team. It was fun, but that was all. Maybe the most fun I’d ever had on a horse, but…
Well, no. The most fun I’d ever had on a horse was that trail ride with Marshall and our friends.
Before I fell off.
There it was. It had been staring me in the face all along, and I’d been too headstrong to admit it.
I’d bought Joy because part of me had never given up on that school-girl crush on a cowboy. Instead
of trying a new hairstyle or a cute outfit to get attention, I’d gone and bought a whole horse. And boy,
did it ever work. Just not the way I’d expected it to.
The bottles started to slip, so I shifted my grip and gave Kaycee my best cheerful grin. “I guess
we’ll see. Excuse me.”
Kaycee and Marshall were still talking—something about her plans for the circuit this year and a
new horse she was training. I closed the fridge and returned with a scoop of ice. Once I hit the switch
on the blender, I didn’t hear anything else for a minute. A glance showed his face turned down, his
eyes hooded, and his arms crossed over his chest. Kaycee looked serious, too, and she was talking
with her hands like something was important. Finally, she held them up in a shrug just as the blender
stopped. “… just not meant to be sometimes. I hate to say it, but… Oh! Coffee.”
She pulled some cash out of her pocket as I put the lid on her cup. “Thanks, Kelli. Stay in touch,
you hear?”
I nodded. “I’ll do that.”
She blew me a kiss, then gave Marshall a short, friendly wave. “Well, I have a flight to catch. I’ll
be back in the spring. Hope to see you both then!”
We watched as she jumped in the passenger seat of the car and sped off. Marshall’s hands were in
his pockets and he was slow to turn back around. “Guess I should probably be going, too.”
My chest squeezed. I hadn’t been sure how to answer him before, but the longer he’d stood there,
the easier it would have been to convince me to talk to him. Or go to dinner. Or anything, really. I just
wasn’t ready for him to go yet. “I thought you came for a reason.”
His jaw flexed, and he stared back at the ground. “I did. Just… maybe you’re right. I got some
figuring to do. No sense in trying to say what doesn’t mean anything.” He pulled his keys out of his
pocket and flashed a single, somber glance at me. “I’ll see you.”
I almost burst through the door and chased him down as I watched him get in his truck. What did he
mean by coming there and acting all heartbroken, like all he wanted in the world was to work things
out, and then leaving before he said what he’d come to say?
Unless he’d never meant it. Maybe he’d cooked up some beautiful speech in that chivalrous heart
of his, but then realized that it was a losing fight.
Because fighting was all we ever did.
So, that was it, then. We finally found something we both agreed on, and we didn’t even have to
have one last argument to settle it. We were just done. A failed experiment.
I held it together long enough to go to the barn that night to take care of Joy. I trailed my fingers
down her velvety nose as a sheet of tears blinded me, but I didn’t cry out loud. “Hey, beautiful,” I
whispered.
She nudged my hand, then just stood there like a stone. She didn’t push me, didn’t demand attention,
but she was right there. Strong. Faithful. A stout shoulder when I leaned on her to sigh out all my
cares. I poured her grain, but she didn’t dive in at once like she used to. She touched my fingers with
the edge of her nostril and blew a warm breath of air over my skin. I guess it was her way of showing
she was happy.
But I wasn’t.
When I got home, I tossed my keys on the table and dropped onto the couch with my phone. I was
kidding myself. Sure, Joy and I were really starting to click, but to what end? What did I think I was
going to do, just go to all the barrel races next summer alone? If I couldn’t have Marshall there to
cheer me on, anything I achieved would be hollow. A waste of time and heart. I couldn’t even go
riding with Morgan because that would involve Cody, meaning I’d be faced with… him.
I loved Joy. Sweet agony, I loved her so much my chest hurt when I thought of her. But I loved
Marshall more, and I had to let him go. It would only make the pain of losing him worse if I tried to
hang on to her.
I sniffed back a few tears, then picked up the phone and dialed. Cody answered. I tried to smile
when I talked, because people can always tell when you’ve been crying. “Hey, Cody, I have a favor
to ask.”
“Sure, Kelli. What’s up?”
“There was this guy at the auction when I bought Joy. He really wanted her, but he stopped fifty
dollars short of my top bid. I heard people call him Bud. Do you have any idea who he is or how to
get hold of him?”
Cody’s phone muffled for a second like he was covering the receiver. He was probably putting it
on speaker and playing the whole thing for Morgan. But it didn’t matter, because she’d find out,
eventually.
“Bud Wilkins. Yeah, I know him. He’s supposed to stop by here Saturday to drop off a retired rope
horse he’s donating. We had to wait till there was a stall for it.”
I sucked in a deep breath and closed my eyes. Was I really doing this? But it was for the best. It
would be like peeling off a Band-aid. Awful at first, but less painful in the long run. “Do you think
he’d still be interested in Joy?”
Cody hesitated. “Are you sure you want to do that?”
I nodded shakily. “Yes.”
He sighed into the phone. “I’ll call him for you.”
Chapter 21
“W ait up, Marshall.”
I was shifting the truck into gear when I heard Dusty call from the driveway. I kept my foot
on the brake and rolled the window down. “What?”
He slammed the door of his own truck and walked over with a pair of leather gloves in his hand.
“Mind if I tag along to the work party?”
I shrugged and put my truck in park to wait for him. “Thought you were busy today,” I said as he got
in.
“When are we not busy? But I wanted to come to help Cody and Morgan.”
“They’ll be happy to have you. What’s that?”
Dusty was swiping something on his phone screen, and his forehead creased as he read whatever it
was. His shoulders sagged, then he shook his head and put his phone away. “Nothing.”
“Didn’t look like nothing. You’ve been weird lately.”
He took his hat off and hung it on his knee. “Weird?”
“Yeah. Secretive and grumpy and a pain in the sirloin.”
“You’ve got a lot of room to talk. Evan was bent about you slamming on the tractor the other day.”
I shifted my mouth. “I heard. Guess I oughta say I’m sorry.”
“For what? You didn’t do anything to me.”
“It’s more what I didn’t do.”
“And that is?”
My hands tightened on the wheel. “Let go. Of my temper, of the things I can’t control, all of it.
Sorry if I was a jerk.”
Dusty put his elbow on the door ledge and faced the window. “It’s okay. I’ve been none too
sociable myself.”
I squinted under the visor. Might as well just ask what I was worried about. I’d rather know the
worst than wonder about it. “Are you thinking of leaving?”
His head snapped around. “Leaving?”
“The ranch. Home. Is that what’s been bugging you?”
“No. Why would you ask something like that?”
A pain moved around in my chest. “Because I am.”
He just stared at me.
“Come on, don’t look at me like that.”
“I’m trying to decide if you’re just messing with me or if you’re serious.”
“Dead serious. I’m not thinking forever. Just… a while. Long enough to… I don’t know what.”
His face screwed up in confusion. “Are you okay?”
I shook my head. “But I will be. Once I get everything figured out.”
C ody was walking around outside the barn when we got to their new property. He was packing a
shovel in one hand and a horse halter in the other, and he stepped over to open the gate for us.
“Thanks for coming early,” he said when we got out.
“No problem.” I glanced around, disappointed that Kelli’s car wasn’t there yet. “What time is
everyone showing up?”
“Nine. Here, help me bring a horse in.”
Dusty reached out for the halter. “What’s the shovel for?”
“Huh? Oh, nothing. Just bringing it up from the house. It’s the bay over there. He has Cushings; it's a
metabolic disorder, so he has to have his medicine every day. Morgan put his feed in a small run
behind the barn so the other horses can’t steal it from him.”
“Okay. So, the bay with the blaze or the one with the star?”
Cody looked at the corral again. There were about eight horses in it and two of them were bays.
“Sorry. The blaze. Marshall, want to come inside and see what we’re working on today?”
Dusty split off to catch the bay, and I followed Cody into the new barn. It didn’t look like they had
done much to the office since last week, but there was a stack of drywall sheets, boxes of screws,
buckets of drywall mud, and a few rolls of insulation.
“Hoping to get all this up today,” he said. “Morgan would like to move operations here by spring
break.”
“What’s left after the office?”
“Follow me.” He pushed the door open into what was eventually going to be the therapy program’s
saddling area. Right now, this section was still just a pole barn frame. “Stalls are done so we can get
the rest of the horses moved at a moment’s notice. We’ll get the cedar planks up in the tack room
today and eventually—” he gestured down a wide aisle—“we’ll get the boards up around the riding
ring. It’s only sixty by eighty, but you wouldn’t believe how hard it is to get the lumber truck up here
in the middle of winter. Sand, too. I’ll be lucky to get that before April.”
I rested my hands on my hips and surveyed the lined sheet metal roof and the new lights Cody had
hung. “I can’t believe you guys have gotten this far already.”
“That’s only because Morgan’s a charmer. She talked the truss company into bumping our order up
the list. Otherwise, we wouldn’t have even been able to start for months yet. This will all be tongue-
and-groove pine siding, and then…” Cody flipped a light on into the row of horse stalls. “Oh.” His
face got tight, and he turned to me, pushing on my shoulder. “Just a second.”
“What?” I couldn’t help looking past him, and then I saw. Blinking in the florescent light flooding
the new row of stalls was a single horse. A handsome chestnut mare with a blaze face and a pink
halter.
“What’s Kelli’s horse doing here? Are she and Morgan going riding later?”
Cody shook his head. “I wish. I picked her up yesterday because Kelli’s selling her to Bud Wilkins.
He’s coming for her this afternoon.”
My face grew cold. “What? ”
“She didn’t want you to know yet. I’m sorry, I forgot in our barn tour that I wasn’t planning to bring
you back here.”
“But why? For criminy’s sake, how many times did I try to talk her into selling that mare? She’d
never do it! Why now?”
Cody heaved a sigh. “You’ll have to ask her, I suppose.”
“I’m going to ask her right now,” I growled, yanking my phone out of my pocket. “What was she
thinking? She loves this horse! Why didn’t she talk to…” I lowered the phone.
“She didn’t want to talk to you about it,” Cody said gently.
I nodded and swallowed. It was a few seconds before I found my voice. “I know. Guess I’ve never
really given her a reason to. She probably figured I’d say, ‘I told you so’ again.”
“The way I understand it, it had nothing to do with the horse being a problem. Sounded like
personal stuff.”
Oh. I met his eyes. “I think I know why.”
“Sorry, Marshall. Morgan tried to talk her out of it, but she was set on it. Said it was a nice dream
while it lasted, and that was all I heard before Morgan packed her off for a private talk.”
I studied the darkened screen of my phone. Should I call her now? Or wait for her to show up and
tell her everything that had been on my heart, all the things I needed her to know? Somehow, I had to
find a way to fix this. It couldn’t be too late!
“Bud won’t be here for the horse till mid-afternoon,” Cody said as if he could hear my thoughts.
“Meanwhile, should we go get the tools out of your truck? I hope Luke sent his finish nailer. Did your
dad throw in that extra set of kneepads he had? We could sure use them.”
He was trying to distract me, but it wasn’t working. If anything, I was more focused on what I had
to do. Yes, I’d work today. I’d give this project my all, because it was important to people who were
important to me. But somewhere in the middle of all of it, I’d make sure Kelli knew I wanted to build
something with her, too. And hopefully, I could talk her into not giving up on that dream of hers quite
yet.
I put my phone away, my hands suddenly itching for work. “What time is Kelli getting here today?”
Cody hesitated. “I don’t think she’s coming.”
My stomach sank like a rock. I hadn’t even considered that she wouldn’t show up. She was
Morgan’s best friend, the program’s volunteer coordinator. She was probably the one who’d
scheduled this whole thing! “Not… not coming?”
“She felt like it would be too much, watching the horse get loaded up and everything. She may have
had other reasons, too. I don’t know. Morgan asked if she could be a runner—you know, standing by
for errands to the hardware store or picking up pizzas for us at lunch. So, you might catch her then, if
you’re quick.”
My eyes glazed as my brain scrambled to hatch a new plan. Was she avoiding me? I hadn’t exactly
given her much lately to look for. But I was going to change all that, and if it meant I had to chase her
car down the road to make her hear me, so be it.
We returned to the main office room and found Morgan talking to Dusty, plus a few others who
were trickling in. Jess had just arrived with Austen Conrad. There was also a pack of 4-H kids
needing to put in service hours and Doc Burns, the local vet. Cody introduced me to an older guy
named Walter who had an artificial leg, but I’d never have known it if he hadn’t pulled up his pant leg
and knocked on it.
People started showing up all at once, and before long, I was standing alone in the middle of a
crowded room. Everyone was excited to get to work, talking about the plans for the day and what this
project meant for the future. All the usual volunteers and a few of the regular clients and their families
showed up. They all had something to say and someone to say it to… except for me. I turned my back
on the crowd and strapped on my tool belt.
Someone tugged on my sleeve, and I looked around at Morgan. I braced myself for some apology
about Kelli’s absence, but she was nodding toward the door at someone who was just arriving.
“Marshall, can I ask a favor? You see that kid coming in the door?”
I glanced up. He was a gangly kid wearing glasses and no coat, despite the fact that it was snowing
again outside. He looked like he was about twelve, and he was hanging back behind his mother,
scanning the room with a guarded expression. “Yeah.”
“His name is Dustin, and he’s not going to handle this crowd well. Meg—that’s his mom—really
wanted to help today, so I told her we’d find something quiet and focused for Dustin. He’s crazy smart
and really detail oriented.”
I made a face. “Listen, I’m not that good with kids. Did you ask Dusty?”
“He’s already gotten started on cutting cedar planks for the tack room and Dustin doesn’t like the
noise from the saws. I thought he’d like mudding the drywall joints, but we won’t be ready for that till
later. Any ideas?”
I shrugged and shook my head as I slid my hammer into my tool belt. “He’s one of your clients,
isn’t he? Why not just bring one of the horses in the barn and let him hang out for a while?”
She tapped my shoulder in grateful inspiration. “Marshall, you’re a genius. We brought Biz over
from the other house for the weekend because he gets in too much trouble if he’s bored. He’s out in
the big corral with the rescue geldings. I’ll go get him. Thank you!”
“Anytime.” I grabbed my impact driver and a box of drywall screws and sighed. Only part of me
was there. The rest of me—all that mattered—was waiting till Kelli arrived to come to life. Until
then, I’d just be a robot, staring out the window more than I should and wondering if the worst of
today’s snow would hold off until her skittish old car made it up the mountain road.
T hat did it. I smacked my hands together as I snapped the lid of the storage bin. All my
Christmas decorations were safely put up for the next year.
I didn’t have many, and it wasn’t like I had a house full of kids to fill stockings or buy special
goodies for, but I’d just never felt like my house was a home at Christmas unless I put a few things
out. Every year, I’d stop at the craft store after stuff went on clearance and pick up a few more little
knick-knacks, and now the new decorations were packed up with the old ones for another Christmas.
I shouldn’t have been doing this today, but I had to do something with my time. I checked my
watch. Morgan’s work crew would be in full swing now, and I was supposed to have been there with
them. But I just couldn’t. I couldn’t watch Joy take those last steps onto the trailer, and I couldn’t
work side-by-side with Marshall without breaking. And bless my friend, she understood.
I did promise to pick up the pizzas for her crew at lunchtime. That would give me a chance to
satisfy my curiosity about what was going on, but I wouldn’t be obligated to stay. But lunch was a
way off. I might as well do something else with myself, like clean the cobwebs from my ceiling.
Something really important.
I grabbed the broom and headed for the front door, and my eyes landed on the candy-cane-wrapped
box. It was still sitting on my entry table, right where Marshall had left it. I dropped the broom and
stared at it, frowning. I’d have to do something about that thing. I hadn’t felt right about opening it, but
it sure couldn’t sit there another year.
The box wasn’t large, but it was heavy. I turned it over in my hands and drifted toward the couch to
sit and ponder the thing. What should I do with it? He’d obviously wanted me to open it, even after
we fought. Would he still want me to? Should I just return it?
That seemed like a smack in the face to a guy who didn’t deserve it. Maybe we were terrible as a
couple, but it didn’t mean I didn’t care for him. Or he for me. And I was dying to know what he’d
been so eager to give me. My fingers slid under the tape.
Ten seconds later, I died. Or came alive. Maybe both. My hands trembled, and I plucked a heavy
chunk of steel out of its packing paper. It was a spur. Two, actually. Custom made black iron with
silver, brass and rhinestone accents. Just like the big-time barrel racers wore.
I turned it over and ran my fingers along the side. KM, my initials, shone next to a silhouette of a
horse turning a barrel. The filigree detailing was stunning, and when I hefted them, the balance was
just right. He’d even included a pair of silver-buckled straps with leather cut-outs and a turquoise
background peeking through. I didn’t even want to think about what they cost.
I was starting to hyperventilate. Why would Marshall give me something like this? Didn’t he
always tell me I was too reckless? Didn’t slow down? Why give me something so symbolic of grit
and speed? Traditionally, cowboys didn’t “earn their spurs” until they’d proven themselves as tough
and skilled, and able to wield them wisely. And he thought that was me?
I couldn’t keep these. They were just too much! I dropped them back in the box like I was scalded,
but I couldn’t stop staring at them. And then I saw the note peeking out from the bottom of the box
through the spurs. I swallowed. What fresh shock could that be? But I had to know, so I reached in.

p.emphasis: Merry Christmas to the toughest cowgirl I know. Hope these will help you
chase your dreams. You’ll always have my heart.

section.p: So simple. And yet, it said everything. My heart pounding, I read it about a thousand times in just a
few seconds. And I knew exactly what I had to do.
M y car almost slid off the road twice. I turned up the windshield wiper and backed off the
accelerator. Fresh snow was melting as it hit the pavement, then re-freezing almost
immediately. I wanted to gun the engine and streak up the hillside to find Marshall, but I could almost
hear his voice in my ear to take it easy. He’d still be there five minutes later.
Shoot! I screeched the brakes on that empty country road and my car skittered sideways on the
black ice. How could I have forgotten lunch for the crew? I glanced at my dash clock. It was past
eleven already, and I was supposed to pick the pizzas up in twenty minutes. I muttered a few
indelicate things under my breath, but I backed the car up in a wide spot on the shoulder and turned
back down the hill. As eager as I was to see Marshall, it wouldn’t be fair of me to forget everyone’s
lunch in my race to see him.
The kid at the pizza place took forever to get our order up to the counter. It was like he didn’t
understand what “I’m in a hurry” meant and went slower instead of speeding up. By the time I
slammed the car door and glanced at the clock again, it was almost noon. I jerked the gear shifter and
sped off.
But not for long. I was halfway up the county road when I heard the siren, and I groaned when I
caught the reflection of red and blue lights in my rearview. Perfect. I pulled over, gritting my teeth
and hissing in frustration.
Sheriff Wyatt sauntered up to my window and leaned down to peer at me through his dark glasses.
“License and registration, please.”
“Wyatt, come on. You buy coffee from me like three times a day. Heck, I’ve always told first
responders it’s on the house!”
“License and registration, please.”
I bit back a spicy retort and grabbed my wallet. “I wasn’t going that fast. I was only doing—”
“Sixty-five in a school zone.”
I whirled around to look at the side of the highway. “You can’t even see the school! It’s like half a
mile away! And it's on Saturday during Christmas break!”
“It’s the law, ma’am.”
“Look, I’m sorry,” I said, making my voice as apologetic as possible. Might as well try wheedling
him. “I’m trying to get a hungry work crew fed and I was in a hurry. There’s no one around and it’s not
even a school day. Please…” I glanced at the boxes. “I’m sure you’re hungry, too.”
Wyatt narrowed his eyes and wrote something else on the ticket. “Bribing an officer.”
“I wasn’t! I was only saying how you must understand what it’s like to—”
“Where’d you get the men’s hat?”
I craned my neck around. Marshall’s hat, the one I’d given him for Christmas and he’d left at my
house, was still in my back seat. I’d put it in my car hoping I’d see him in town or something so I
could give it back to him, because I sure didn’t have a use for it and I couldn’t return it.
“It belongs to someone else.”
Sheriff Wyatt kept writing. “Theft of property.”
“Oh, for Pete’s sake! It’s a gift for Marshall Walker, and I just happen to have it in my car! That’s
not a crime, Wyatt!”
His pen stopped, and he glanced over the rim of his glasses. “Did you say Walker? That one of
Blake’s boys?”
I seized on that. “Yes! He sure is.”
“The one who used to break into fire hydrants and flood the parking lot at the grocery store?”
“Uh… no. You’re probably thinking of Luke. He was wild back in high school, I heard.”
“Then it’s the one who drives ATVs on the highway without a blinker.”
“Probably Luke again. Why does it matter which one he is?”
Sheriff Wyatt’s pen hovered over the ticket, and his mouth screwed up into a frown. “You’re sure
it’s not the one who used to break into the fire hydrants?”
My head dropped back onto the seat. “One hundred percent.”
“Well…” Wyatt’s voice stretched to a drawl, and he tucked his pen in his pocket. “That’s good,
ma’am, or I’d be questioning your judgment, hanging around a hooligan.”
“I don’t see what that has to do with my driving.”
“How big is that work crew?”
I tilted my head. “Why?”
He pointed to the boxes in my passenger seat. “You don’t have enough to spare one piece, do you?”
“I can probably spare a whole pizza. Here, take a piece.”
Wyatt ripped the ticket out of his book and tore it up. He took the slice of pepperoni I offered with
a brief nod. “You drive carefully, now.”
I pulled back out on the highway a little slower than before, watching my mirror to make sure Wyatt
was well behind me. I still wanted to speed, but that little delay had cost me almost ten minutes.
Besides, the roads were getting worse, and the last thing I needed was to have to call for someone to
pull my car out of the ditch.
I took a few deep breaths and tried to slow myself down by thinking through what I’d say when I
saw Marshall. He’d be working, probably distracted and maybe not in the mood to talk. We hadn’t
exactly parted well the other day. How would I get him alone? Make him feel something close to what
I was feeling?
My fingers flexed on the steering wheel as I chewed in thought. One sweet Christmas gift didn’t
change the world. For any of it to really mean anything, for it to last, there had to be something
already there. How could we get past the things that always got us stuck? Did I even know what they
were?
Priorities, that was a big one. I needed to know where I ranked in his. And I’d really like to be by
his side as a partner, working toward the same goals, not pulling in separate directions like I felt we
had been. Could we ever do that? I was sure willing to try again, and from what he’d said in his note,
maybe he was, too. And this time, I knew something I didn’t know before. I knew how good we could
be together, if we let ourselves.
The snow was really coming down by the time I pulled into the new White Pines driveway. I
stepped out of the car for one long drink of the winter air, cleansing my senses. In just a few minutes,
I’d have some answers. But first, I needed to haul in the pizzas.
Chapter 22
“H ey, kid. Are you thirsty? There’s hot chocolate and cider in the office.”
He acted like he didn’t hear me, but I was pretty sure he had. Dustin, the kid Morgan had
pointed out to me, was in the middle of the new stall aisle, just brushing a furry buckskin gelding.
He’d been in there for over two hours, not making a peep. His mother had gone back once to check on
him and said he’d be happy there until there was a job for him to do. I was almost ready for help, but
I was more ready for lunch.
“Dustin, can you hear me? Hot chocolate? Morgan has donuts, too.”
He was brushing Biz’s mane with exacting care, and he didn’t look up. But after a few seconds, he
said, “I don’t like donuts.”
“What about pizza? It’s supposed to be here in a little while.”
He kept brushing. “I like pizza.”
“Everyone likes pizza.” I rattled the screws in my tool belt. “I just finished hanging dry wall in the
hallway. Morgan said you’d like to tape and mud the joints. Wanna get started before lunch? It will go
fast with both of us.”
More brushing. “Morgan said I should put Biz away when I was done.”
“Well, are you ready?”
Dustin finally lowered his brush and turned around. “Not yet.” I watched as he carefully placed the
brush back in the grooming tote, arranging the other brushes around it so you could see them all
clearly. Then he carried it into what was going to be the tack area and looked at the wall, as if
expecting a shelf that hadn’t been hung yet. After a few seconds, he set the tote on the floor and came
back to unsnap Biz from the crossties. “Now I’m ready.”
“Okay, do you need help?”
“I know where he goes.”
I nodded. “I bet you do. You got a coat? It’s snowing pretty hard out there.”
“I don’t like coats.”
“Well, that’s up to you. Come on back here when you’re done and I’ll give you a job.”
He straightened and lifted his chin. “Okay.” He walked Biz through the big sliding back door of the
barn and I helped him close it. Then I looked at my watch.
Another half hour before Kelli would be up there. And I planned to be waiting for her when she got
out of the car, which meant I had to time my work so I could stop right when she was due. There was
just too much I wanted to tell her—no, show her—and I wanted to start by proving that she was what
mattered most. So, if it was carrying pizzas for her or talking her out of selling her horse or telling her
the plan that had been hatching in my head since that day at the coffee shop, I’d make sure I was ready.
It was really Kaycee Whitmore who’d kicked the last clue in place for me. When Kelli was making
her drink, she’d said something to me about retiring from the rodeo circuit. I about fainted when she
said that. She had a few up-and-coming young horses, and she was at the top of her game, had all the
best pro sponsors and her own line of saddles with a big label and everything.
But her husband back home was getting tired of having her gone all the time. He’d wanted kids, but
she’d put that off for so long it might be almost too late. She wanted him to follow the circuit with her,
but he was home trying to get a growing business off the ground. “I love him to death but we can’t
keep playing tug-of-war” was one thing she’d said, and it stuck with me. It all sounded just a bit too
familiar and by the time she’d left that day, my ears were on fire with guilt.
And so, the minute I got Kelli to myself, I was going to tell her I planned to leave the ranch.
Not to move to the big city or anything crazy like that. But I couldn’t very well marry her and move
her into the ranch house with four bachelors living there, could I? If she would have me—and it might
take some groveling, but I’d happily give up what I thought I'd always wanted to have her—we could
live in her house for a while. The house she had worked for, something she was proud of. I could
drive out and get my work done every day like Cody did, or… or get a real job, if that was what I
needed to do. We could work on becoming partners with the same goals, rather than coming from two
different worlds and trying to make them fit.
After that, we’d figure the rest out. Together.
But first, she had to get there. I checked my watch. Only about ten more minutes and she’d be
skidding into the driveway. Driving too fast, like she always did. I had to chuckle at that thought.
Then something pricked my awareness, and I looked at my watch again. Dustin hadn’t come back.
He probably got distracted by the hot chocolate, but I’d planned things out so we could get the joint
tape up before lunch. His not coming back changed my schedule a little. I put my screw gun down and
went into the office. “Hey, has anyone seen Dustin?”
A few people turned around, most looking blank-faced as they tacked up insulation or stood by
with more drywall. Morgan had her hands full with a driver and a level, and she spat some screws
out of her mouth. “I thought he was still in with Biz.”
“He went to put him away about fifteen minutes ago, but he hasn’t come back. I figured he just got
distracted somehow.”
“Not Dustin. He would put the horse away and finish his job if it was the last thing he did. Hang
on.” She walked to the back of the office where the utility room and bathrooms were going to go.
“Meg? We’re looking for Dustin.”
I leaned down to look out the window. Still no gray Civic peeling into the driveway. I sighed.
Soon.
A second later, Morgan and Meg raced past me for the front door. “How long ago did you say,
Marshall?” she called over her shoulder.
They looked worried, so I trotted after them. The kid couldn’t have gone far, but I guessed his mom
knew when to worry and when not to by this time. “Fifteen minutes ago. He wasn’t wearing a coat,
but it’s only about a hundred feet to the—” I stopped dead.
The corral was empty. No Dustin, no Biz, no herd of woolly winter geldings, and a gate swinging
wide open. And all the tracks led up the mountain.
“W here is everybody?” I stepped inside the office, my arms loaded with seven pizza boxes
and a mountain of breadsticks. The only person I saw was Walter, and he was leaning out the
window, his face pinched.
He straightened when he saw me and came over to help with the pizzas. “They’re all out looking
for Dustin Truman. He’s lost out there somewhere. I’m no good in the snow, so I said I’d stay here in
case he wandered back inside.” Walter’s big shoulders sagged. “Wish I could keep up out there and
help!”
“Dustin, lost? What happened? Where do they think he went?”
“I guess he was grooming Biz until they could give him a job. You know Dustin, he doesn’t like all
the loud noises from the tools or lots of people. One of the Walkers—let’s see, the taller one, I think it
was Marshall—he sent Dustin to put the horse away and the kid never came back. Cody said it looks
like he had some trouble at the gate and the horses got out, but that doesn’t explain where Dustin
went.”
“Good heavens! What are they doing to look for him?”
“Cody and that other Walker brother took the two ATVs up the mountain after the tracks. Everyone
else is just combing the woods around here, figure he’s probably on foot. And since he doesn’t wear
coats, they’re all pretty worried about hypothermia.”
“Oh, I bet! Well… what about Marshall?” I couldn’t imagine him wandering around on foot when
Cody and his brother were zipping up the mountain.
“Huh. I guess they’d brought a horse in this morning to take his medicine, so he was the only one of
the geldings still here when the others took off. All the saddles are still down at the old facility, so
that guy hopped on the horse bareback and galloped after the others.”
“Bareback? ” I was already scrambling for the door. I had another hat in the car I could give to
Dustin to warm him up when we found him, and I usually kept an extra coat and gloves in the trunk for
when I went to the barn…
The barn.
I whirled around. “How long has Dustin been missing?”
“Twenty, thirty minutes or so. Poor kid, I bet he’s so cold!”
“I bet he is. I’m going looking for him, too.” I went to my car and bundled the extra warm clothes I
had under my arm, then raced around to the side of the barn where Morgan’s horse trailer was parked.
There was one saddle here, though Marshall wouldn’t have known about it. My saddle, still in Cody
and Morgan’s trailer because I’d given it to the program when he came to pick her up.
I dumped my stuff off by the trailer and ran for the big sliding door of the barn. I tugged it open, and
inside I found my Joy. The horse I was giving up.
She neighed when she saw me.
“Hey, beautiful. How about one last ride?”
Chapter 23
C ody and Dusty were ahead of me, but I was hot on their heels. An ATV is a good tool in the
mountains in the snow because it’s fast with great traction, but for rough country, you really
can’t beat a horse. I just wished I had my saddle. And maybe a horse I knew. I grappled for the bay’s
mane and stuck to the middle of him. I wasn’t sure if he was one of the new rescues or if he belonged
to the therapy program, but he seemed pretty gentle. Hopefully, he didn’t buck.
The group of geldings had taken off all helter-skelter, but the tracks straightened out after about a
hundred yards. Cody and Dusty had screwed up some of the tracks with their ATVs, but I could still
read most of them. And it looked to me like about five or six horses were goofing off, bucking and
playing as they ran, and one was holding a straight line.
If I had to guess, I’d say the horses were jostling Dustin at the gate when he tried to put Biz away
by himself. I shouldn’t have let him go out alone! They’d probably gotten past him, but instead of
getting safely out of the way and letting them go, he’d followed. I’d seen some scuffled tracks next to
the corral fence, so my bet was that Dustin had just scooted Biz over, climbed on his back from the
fence rail, and set out after the horses. Why would he do such a fool thing?
But even as I growled the question, I knew the answer. Because it was his job to put the horses
away. A job I’d told him to do, so I was at least partially responsible for what happened. And Dustin
was the kind who would make darned sure he did his job, no matter what it cost him. Kinda like some
other confounded fool I knew. I leaned forward and asked the bay to gallop a little faster.
On ahead, Cody and Dusty had stopped, and I pulled up beside them. “What is it?”
Cody was squinting at the snow and drawing circles in the air as he traced the path. “Looks like
they split up. Some scuffling here. If you’re right and Dustin was trying to catch them, maybe he tried
to get a rope on someone. And then two horses took off for the river while the others went uphill.”
“Why would they do that?”
Cody shook his head. “With the snow coming on, most horses head back down.”
“Unless they were still feeling full of themselves,” Dusty offered. “They could have sniffed out that
bunch of ranch colts we have pastured on the other slope. Which group do you think Dustin
followed?”
“Hard to say. But we can’t take the ATVs down the river trail, so you and I will follow the bigger
group. Marshall, you good to go down the other way?”
“Yep.” I turned the bay and squeezed my calves to send him down the trail at a gallop. It was a lot
harder riding bareback these days than it used to be.
M y teeth were chattering with raw nerves as I trotted through the barnyard gate. I’d never
ridden out alone, and certainly never when the stakes were so high. I found the trail made by
the ATVs and maybe a dozen horses and followed it. How hard could that part be? I gave Joy her
head and asked her to go. That was her favorite thing in the world to do, and we took off.
I saw some people walking through the trees a few hundred yards later. I recognized Meg, Jess, and
some teens, and I thought I could see Morgan beyond them. They were calling Dustin’s name every so
often. I almost stopped to join them, but if they hadn’t found him there, I wasn’t going to be much help.
I’d go out a little farther.
“Okay, Joy,” I stammered breathlessly. “Let’s get moving.”
She flicked an ear at me and put those long legs of hers to work. We were galloping up the snowy
slope in an instant, her neck levering up and down as her haunches thrust us forward. Her strides
were so driving and powerful it felt like she was jumping with every step and snorting because she
loved it. I, however, had to hang on for dear life. Had Marshall really run up this hill bareback? I felt
like I was flopping all over the place… at first. It took me a minute or three, but I figured out how to
sit forward and let her reach without getting my bum bruised.
And for the guiltiest of seconds, I forgot all about Dustin. It was horrible of me! But there was an
instant there, climbing that big snowy mountain with my horse when I felt like I really could tackle
anything. We were one machine, thinking and breathing together, and it was just like that thrill when
we shot around the barrel pattern. No matter what happened, no matter how crazy or hard life got, this
was my heart horse, and I’d be out of my mind to let her go.
In fact, she wasn’t the only thing I should be hanging on to instead of giving up on.
We galloped over a rise to an area that flattened out, and I slowed Joy down to catch her breath.
This was the future building site for the house. Off to our right was a stand of trees, and down to the
left, I could hear the frozen creek bubbling through the ice. I could still see the tracks from the others,
so I started her off after them again.
But I was starting to wonder if I was wasting my time. What was I really doing on that mountain?
The guys would surely have found Dustin already, if he were up here to be found. The tracks made it
pretty obvious which way they… I stopped Joy.
The tracks split. The ATVs had gone off to the right, but there were other hoof prints going left.
As I sat there, pondering which way to go, I heard the faint hum of their engines. The noise got
louder, and after a minute, they popped out of the trees. I squinted. As they got closer, I could see one
rider was leading a horse, and the other had a short passenger. They saw me and waved, heading
straight for me.
“You found him?” I called as they drew near.
Cody pulled up first. Dustin was riding behind him, clinging to Cody’s middle. Poor kid, his lips
looked like they were turning blue! I untied the extra coat from behind my saddle and hopped off to
wrap it around him.
“He’s not hurt,” Cody assured me. “Just having a little too much fun, huh, bud? Hey, next time you
want to round up some horses, we’ll do it in better weather, ok?”
Dustin shivered and nodded. He’d never let me touch him before, but today he let me zip the coat
up to his chin and tuck the hat over his ears. “C-can w-we g-go back n-now?”
“Sure thing. That hot chocolate’s probably sounding pretty good right about now.”
Dusty pulled up on the other side of Cody, leading Biz. “Looks like the other geldings are running
with some of our ranch colts. They’ve got good forage and water where they’re at, so we’ll ride up
later and round everyone up. Marshall and I need to come back with our own horses. And then we’ll
have to fix some fence.”
Cody groaned. “Story of my life. Did Marshall send you out to look for us?”
“I haven’t seen Marshall. I thought he was with you.”
“We split up right about here. He was going to go down and check the river. He’s probably back
down the other side now, waiting for us.”
I shook my head. “He can’t be. When Morgan and I were here before, you couldn’t get through
without coming back up.”
Cody shrugged and started his engine. “Well, he can take care of himself alright. I need to get
Dustin back down.”
“But what about Marshall?” I shouted over the ATV.
“If we don’t see him down there, we’ll come back up,” he called back. “Stay safe. See you at the
bottom!”
See you at the bottom. I dug my teeth into my lower lip. I knew good and well that Marshall
couldn’t be down there already. He wasn’t there when I left, and I hadn’t passed him. He had to still
be searching the riverbed, and maybe panicking that he couldn’t find the lost boy.
I turned my horse down toward the river and gave her a nudge with the side of my let. “Come on,
Joy. Let’s go tell him everything’s okay.”
I t was a lot harder hanging on to a fat horse on a mountain trail bareback than I remembered. I’d
ridden my share of broncy colts and hot-shot cutters; horses that were nigh impossible to stay in
the middle of without a heap of skill and a darn good saddle. I honestly wouldn’t have thought it
would be that hard not to slide off the back of a pudgy old pensioner walking down a trail. But make
that trail an almost forty-degree slope on ice and rocks, and things got a bit dicier.
“Whoa, there,” I steadied him. He stopped, and I looked around. I’d lost the tracks over the rocks,
so my only hope was to pick the trail up somewhere on flatter ground. I was almost at the water’s
edge. Ahead there was nothing but ice and a short trickling fall where it broke through the cut in the
gorge.
If Dustin was on one of the horses that had gotten down here, how far could he have gone? Did he
turn around or find another place to climb back out? And just how deep was that water? I could hear
it moving, so it wasn’t all frozen. That meant it was deep or fast, and neither one was good.
My legs bumped against the bay and urged him forward, scanning the area for any signs of hoof
prints. I probably wouldn’t find anything down here, because what little snow had covered these
rocks before was rapidly falling under a fresh blanket. But there were no horses in sight, so they had
to have gotten through somewhere. I stopped the horse and put my hands around my mouth. “Dustin?
Hallooo…” Not a sound.
I picked up the reins and started forward, but about ten paces later, I had to stop again. A huge rock
had rolled down into the middle of the trail, probably in that last ice storm we’d had. Beyond it,
nothing was level. Maybe if everything wasn’t frozen, I could have found another path, but as it was, I
couldn’t get over it or around the rock, and there was no place to get back out. Was this the spot
where Kelli had to flip her horse around on a dime? If so, I was impressed. And I didn’t think the
overweight old gelding I was on could even attempt it.
“Dustin?” I called again. I could see the deepest part of the river from where I sat, and the ice in
the center hadn’t been broken. There just wasn’t anywhere for the kid to hide, even if he’d fallen off
and gotten hurt. He’d have left some kind of trail. “Are you out here?”
Even the birds were silent in the falling snow. He couldn’t be down here. Why, the ride down the
slope alone would be beyond the kid’s ability. I’d just have to go back up and see if Cody and Dusty
had had any better luck. If I could get back up the slope. I looked around and thought for a minute.
“Okay, horse. You gotta help me out here.” I knotted my fist in his mane so I’d have a decent
handle, then turned his nose to the uphill side of the trail. The horse stepped around, but he didn’t pick
his feet up very well. He stumbled a bit, but caught himself and tried again. Another half-step and
we’d be clear. But as his right hoof reached for sure footing, his toe caught on a loose, rounded
boulder and he staggered.
It happened so fast that I didn’t even have time to blink. The bay went to his knees for a split
second, throwing me forward onto his neck. While he was trying to recover his front feet, his back
feet splayed apart and skittered on yet another rock in the path. He managed to recover his footing, but
he lost me in the process.
Well, sort of. His shoulder slammed into a bigger boulder, and while he bounced off and got
himself upright again, I felt all the pain of whiplash coupled with a thousand pounds slamming me into
a rock. And I might have said a few words that my dad would’ve taken me to the woodshed for when
I was younger.
By the time all the stars cleared out of my head, I was grinding my teeth and holding my right arm
pinned against my side. I wasn’t sure if it was just my arm or if my collarbone had gotten into the
action too, but something wasn’t feeling right. And I still had to climb back out of that snowy gorge.
T hat was weird. The trail just vanished at the tree line.
I frowned and turned Joy back for another look, but there wasn’t anything to see. The snow
broke up into random little patches, and what was underneath was mostly frozen. I squinted and tried
to follow on from the last hoof prints I could find, but the ground didn’t look disturbed to me. I was
hardly an experienced tracker, but I wasn’t blind, either.
But Marshall should still be ahead of me somewhere. Maybe he was already turning back, and he
could hear me by now. I tried calling his name, but all I heard in reply was a bird flapping from one
tree to another.
Well, what now? I sat and frowned for a minute. I could turn back down, but I was convinced that
Marshall had to be up here somewhere. Not that he needed any help from me, but I could at least tell
him Dustin was safe. I should just wait for him where I was. It was a good safe spot up on top here,
and if he came up out of the gorge in a different place, I’d still see him coming.
But Joy made the decision for me. She bowed her neck and rooted at the bit—something she hadn’t
done to me lately—and pressed forward. I should have stopped her. Terrible manners! But maybe up
here, she had some instinct I didn’t possess. Maybe she knew somehow which way we needed to go. I
cringed and let her have her head. I just wouldn’t tell anyone I’d let my horse get away with that one.
Right before the ground was about to drop out from under her feet into a rocky descent, something
whizzed by my head. I jumped, but Joy jumped higher. It was a good thing I’d latched on to the saddle
horn at the last second, because she split sideways so hard that my furry hat flew off. She grunted and
whipped her head up and down like she was thinking about bucking. Then her whole body trembled
until her legs splayed, and she was staring and snorting, ready to bolt.
“Whoa, Joy,” I begged, forcing myself to let go of the saddle horn to stroke her neck. “Whoa, baby!
Whoa, whoa…” I just kept repeating it, even if I didn’t know what I expected her to do. What else
could I do? If I’d pulled the reins right then, I was sure she would have panicked even more. All I
could think of was what Marshall and I had done with her—using my voice and staying calm.
Then a miracle happened. She tilted her head, listening to my voice and cocking a suspicious ear at
the little bird that had flown by her head, now hopping innocently on the ground in search of
something to eat. Joy gave one more snort as I kept talking to her. It took almost a full minute, but
gradually, she relaxed enough to play with the bit.
“Good girl,” I soothed. My hands were shaking, but somehow, I kept my voice even. “My beautiful,
good girl. Are we okay now?”
She swished her tail and sneezed, then reached for a brown tuft of grass poking out of the snow.
“Well, that’s good enough for me. Let’s go find Marshall.”
Joy picked up her head and started down the slope, her feet steady and sure. And even though she’d
just spooked at a bird, I’d never felt safer on her because we’d finally learned to be confident in each
other. It didn’t matter if Bud Wilkins showed up and doubled his offer. Joy was staying with me.
The rest of it? I’d figure it out. Just as soon as I got to talk to my cowboy.
Chapter 24
I hate broken bones. It’s not only the pain, which is bad enough, but the inconvenience of having an
arm in a sling for a couple of months. You can’t work, you can’t shower, and you have to have
help just to put on your socks. And from the looks of things, I was going to be sporting some plaster
for a while.
Right about now, though, a heavy cast sounded pretty good. I leaned against the rock where the bay
horse had smashed me and closed my eyes, trying not to let the dizzy sickness take over as my
forehead beaded with sweat. I probably needed to find somewhere to put my feet up until the shock
wore off, but down there in that frozen gorge, the only way to put my feet up was to lie on the rock
and hang my head down. No way was I doing that, not when I was trying to keep my horse from
running off.
“Easy, boy,” I hissed through gritted teeth when the gelding whinnied again. He’d been quiet
earlier, but just after I came off, he started acting antsy. I was hoping it was because he heard Cody
and Dusty somewhere up there on the ATVs, and I was praying they’d already found Dustin. They’d
get around to looking for me when they could. The kid was more important.
I might be able to walk out. Without a busted arm, it would be no problem. But I was shaking all
over and none too steady on my feet. With my luck, I’d slip and have nothing to catch myself with, and
then maybe get stepped on by the horse I was trying to lead. If it were life and death, I’d make it up
the hill. But it wasn’t. Someone would come looking for me, and it wouldn’t be all that long.
I wedged myself farther up on the rock by levering my weight back and pushing with my boots.
Then I got an idea. Maybe I wasn’t helpless, after all. “Hold still, you,” I told the horse. Turning
loose of my broken right arm was torture, and I hollered in pain—couldn’t really help it—but I made
myself grab his mane and lunge for his withers. If I could jump far enough and then clutch my arm
again after I got on…
I missed.
Actually, the horse took off, darting out from under me like a bottle rocket and trotting away. And
I’d rather not mention my landing. Cowboys do cry, if it hurts bad enough. They pass out, too, but
don’t tell anyone.
So, there I was, bruised with a bloody lip, a knot on my head and at least one whole side of me
broken up. I didn’t know where my hat had gotten to and the cold was seeping through my coat until
my guts shivered. And my horse was gone. I grumbled a few choice words and wished all over again
I had my good Bay Rum there, but it didn’t solve anything. I’d made a stupid call and now my options
were even more limited.
I knew I had to get up. I didn’t stand a chance in that cold, injured and everything, if I had to wait
for whenever someone could come after me. I tried to push my good shoulder off the ground, but my
ribs started screaming and my wrists didn’t work and that was the end of that. I couldn’t get up to my
knees. I couldn’t even kick the rocks under my toes. I dropped my face down into the snow and
groaned. Maybe. I might have blacked out again.
But then I heard my name. It was Kelli. I looked up, and there she was, wearing that sumptuous
wine-colored gown she'd worn to prom and reaching for me like she meant to kiss me silly.
“Marshall, I’ve been looking for you!”
I grinned and tried to say something, but I don’t remember what came out. It was enough just to see
her, laughing and looking as beautiful as ever and challenging me to a race on that magnificently crazy
horse of hers. Where did the horse come from? Her hair streaming behind her, her eyes flashing, she
leaned forward and called over her shoulder, “Catch me if you can, Marshall Walker!”
I reached out for her, chasing the wind until I caught her—five feet, three inches of fire and ice, and
I pulled her close to my aching ribs. My woman. She smelled so good. And she was warm. She was
saying something, but I didn’t care what it was. I just smiled and held on to her with my good arm,
because if Kelli was by my side, everything was just fine.
“Come on, cowboy,” she murmured in my ear. “I need you to do something for me now.”
I let her wrap my left arm over her shoulders and I gave her a cheesy smile, my eyes drooping and
unfocused. This was a good dream. “Anything for you, sweetheart.”
“Don’t call me sweetheart,” she whispered back. “Not yet.”
I let my head drop to her shoulder. It was too much work to hold it up, but it felt nice to rest it there.
“Why not?”
“Because this is going to hurt like the devil.”
She wrenched my arm from its socket, and my delirium snapped. For a blessed few seconds, pain
had been a memory, and all around her face was sunshine and peace. But then she pushed and pulled
and tugged me upright, apologizing each time I yelped but never stopping until I was up on my knees
and pleading for mercy.
“There,” she panted. “Just rest a second. I’ll get Joy.”
The icy world coalesced around me. I was back in that frozen gorge again, injured and shivering so
hard I was about to quake out of my boots, but no longer alone. My head was still hanging in agony,
and it took me a second to put together what she was talking about. “What? What are you… no way!
You’re not putting me on her.”
“It’s the only way I’m getting you out of here. Stand, Joy.” She came back to me, reaching for my
good arm. “We’ve been working on this. She’ll stay put for you.”
I’ll never know how she did it. I don’t want to remember because it involved a fair bit of
screaming on my part, and I probably looked like a giant wuss, but somehow, that slip of a woman
managed to push and shove my carcass up onto the back of the goofiest horse in the county. And that
mare never twitched a muscle until Kelli took the reins to lead her forward. If I’d been in less pain,
I’d have been honestly impressed.
The climb out of the gorge slipped by in something of a misery-stricken blur for me. My arm was
throbbing, my ribs shot with agony at each breath, and my head was loopy as the shock wore off and
the cold settled in. But one thing did manage to slip through the haze fogging my brain. That horse
never took a wrong step. She just walked quietly behind Kelli, putting her feet exactly where they
should go and never jostling me more than she needed to.
I was clutching my broken arm and breathing through my teeth when we reached the top. “Tha’s
good,” I slurred. “Cody cam cone fine me.”
“I don’t think so, cowboy. We’re getting you to the hospital.”
I shook my head, but slowly because it hurt. “I’m f-fun.”
“Uh-huh.”
The next thing I knew, Kelli had swung up behind the saddle. She wrapped her left arm under mine
to hold the reins and turned the horse down the hill toward civilization. “You can lean back a little if
it helps.”
She was tiny compared to me, but she was stronger than I was at the moment. I let myself rest
against her, my head sagging. It did help some, just having something solid and warm next to me to
stabilize my weight. “Kay.”
She hooked her arm around my chest, somehow avoiding the parts that hurt the most, and I felt her
lips on my neck. “You’re one tough cowboy, Marshall Walker.”
“Mmm.” I opened my eyes and watched the snowy landscape pass by us with detached amazement.
Then I closed them again. “Don’ sell this horrrse.”
“Not planning on it,” was her soft answer. “I’m keeping the horse and the cowboy.”
My head bobbed. “Good.” Then I drifted off, walking down the trail in my woman’s arms, and
happier than I’d been in a long time.
“B roken humerus, three cracked ribs, broken collarbone, sprained wrist—both of them.
Numerous contusions, borderline hypothermia…” I flipped the page on the doctor’s chart and
looked up. “And a knob on your skull the size of a golf ball. Good thing there was nothing important
in there to damage.”
“Haha. Give me that.”
I raised the chart out of Marshall’s reach. “How are you going to hold it? You’re casted up from
stem to stern.”
“Not all of me. Wanna see?” He gave me a sloppy, medicated grin and reached for the tail of his
hospital gown with the best hand he had at the moment.
“Yes.”
The word was out of my mouth before I thought better of it. Yes, I did want to see Marshall
Walker’s chiseled abs, muscular arms, and whatever other yummy surprises lurked beneath his
button-down shirts and jeans. But I probably shouldn’t have said that. Not right then. I never could
keep my mouth shut around him.
Marshall froze. His eyes widened, and I could see the exact second he registered all of what I’d
meant. His grin got crooked, the way it always did when he tried to flirt. “Well, now. Isn’t that
interesting.”
“You’ll think ‘interesting’ if you try to do something about it on a hospital gurney.”
“Then let’s get out of here. I’ve got my cast and about fifteen different braces and I'm so loaded up
with morphine I can't remember what year it is. I need some of the kissing-it-better kind of medicine.”
“Slow down, Butch. You’re still strapped into the IV thing, and the nurse is working up your
discharge papers. Cool your biscuits for a minute.”
He sighed and let his head drop back on the pillow. His jaw was twitching, and he started to drum
his fingers but quit because his wrist hurt. Then he turned his head and looked at me.
“I was waiting to talk to you, you know.”
I let my fingers graze the part of his arm that wasn’t broken or bandaged. “The doctor told me that.
He had me back here the whole time they were putting your cast on.”
“Not that. Before. Today.” He made a face and shook his head. “Earlier. I was waiting for you to
get to the work party so I could see you.”
That warmed me to my toes. “I wasn’t sure you wanted to see me. As a matter of fact, I wasn’t sure
I could bear seeing you.”
His head lifted slightly. “Why?”
I caressed his skin as I tried to form the words. “I… figured I had to let you go. Nothing’s ever hurt
worse than that.” I shrugged and fought back a few tears. Even now, it cut my heart out. “I just didn’t
think I could stand to be beside you, but not with you.”
His chest rose in a sigh, then he winced and hissed. “Gotta remember not to do that.” The pain
gradually eased from his face, and his eyes found mine again. “I shoulda talked to you sooner. I meant
to. Just didn’t know how to say it or if you’d listen.”
I tightened my hand on his arm. “I’m listening now.”
“Okay.” He drew a careful breath. “Okay. It’s like this. The ranch… It’s all I’ve ever known.”
“I know that. It’s who you are.”
“But it’s not. Not really.” His gaze grew intense, and he waited for me to say something.
My breath stilled in my chest. What was he trying to tell me? “Well, Marshall Walker. Who are
you?”
He tried to sit up more. “I’m the guy who’s gonna spend the rest of his life chasing you,
sweetheart.”
I chuckled. “What does that mean?”
“Whatever it needs to mean. I’ve had this idea for so long that a relationship would have to fit into
the life I already had. It never occurred to me I could do something new. Be someone new. But for you
“Marshall, I’m not asking you to change who you’ve always been.”
“But I was asking it of you.”
I blinked. “I hadn’t thought of it like that. Hey, you were! That was a jerk thing to do, cowboy. I
oughta—”
“Please don’t punch me for it this time. I’m hurt enough.”
“That was a slap. Are you ever going to let go of that one?”
He grinned. “Nope. I plan to bring it out every time I do something stupid, so I don’t compare quite
so bad.”
“Like this?” I gestured to his cast. “You’d better believe you’re not living this one down anytime
soon.”
“So, does that mean you’ll be there to give me a hard time?”
I reached for his hand, careful not to jostle his fingers too much in the wrist brace. “Just try to get
rid of me.”
“Naw.” He shook his head against the pillow and smiled tiredly. “You’re way too stubborn. I’ll
just put up with you.”
“Oh, now that’s just the most romantic thing I’ve ever heard. I mean, be still my heart!”
“How about this? Kelli Mason, I’ll follow you to rodeos all season long, or I’ll help you build a
coffee empire in the Rockies or strike out and go wherever you want to go. I’ll even babysit your
nephews if it means we’re in it together. As long as we’re chasing the same dream, it doesn’t matter
to me what we’re doing. I just can’t see a future for me without you being the heart and soul of it.”
I had to close my mouth, because it had dropped open. “M-Marshall… are you… s-serious?”
“Dead serious, sugar. If the choice is you or the ranch, I’ve made it. It’s you, every day and
forever.”
My heart was about to pound out of my chest and I was so short of breath I was seeing stars.
“You’re sure of that? This isn’t just morphine talking, is it?”
“I’m not saying I’d like starting over somewhere else, but I’ve thought it through. I’d rather lose my
past than my future.”
I arched my brow, and a smile tugged at my mouth. “And what if I want to move to the city and go
to college? I was thinking about being a teacher. What do you think? I’m sure the school would need a
janitor. You know, clean up the bathrooms, fix broken lockers. You’d be so good at it!”
He swallowed hard and his eyes got big for a second, then his face relaxed. “You’re such a tease,
Kelli Mason. It’s not nice to mess with me when I’m on painkillers.”
I laughed and leaned forward, close to his face. “You know what you’re getting into, right?”
He nodded gently. “Not really, but I’m game to find out.”
“Then let’s blow this joint, cowboy. We have us some planning to do.”
“Now you’re talkin’.”
I found the nurse and asked when Marshall could leave. It turned out they were just heading our
way with his discharge papers. I only had time to send off a few texts to everyone who wanted to
know how Marshall was doing, and then they had his IV removed and they were wheeling him out.
Blake and the brothers had each been offering to pick us up, but I told them all we didn’t need help. I
could drive just fine. It was getting him out of the truck and settled at home that would be the problem.
I pulled up at the hospital door as the nurses wheeled Marshall out, and he gave me a funny look.
“You drove my truck?”
I shrugged. “I figured you’d rather ride home in it than cram yourself in my car. Oh, but there’s one
thing.” I opened the back door and reached in. “You forgot your hat. Think it still fits?”
He grinned and let me set his new hat back on his head where it belonged. “Like a glove.”
It took some doing, and about four people, but we got Marshall settled in the passenger seat. We
closed the door, and I came around to the driver’s side. He was pale as a ghost, visibly trying to take
tiny, even breaths. He had to be in so much pain, but he just leaned back in the seat and gave me a
cocky smile.
I shook my head. Cowboys. I turned the ignition. “Ready?”
“Let’s go. Uh, by way of the wayside, if you can. There’s something I never got to do.”
“Marshall, it’s like eleven-thirty on New Year’s Eve, and you’re kind of broken. Can’t we do that
tomorrow?”
His eyes lit. “Tomorrow. We could. But let’s do it tonight, too.”
I sighed. “If you insist.”
The wayside was on the main highway out to the ranch. It overlooked the valley, and if you parked
just right behind the overhanging rock cliff, you couldn’t be seen from the road. Not that I’d know, or
anything. I pulled off the road and circled the truck. As I did, a few fireworks sparked off from the
valley behind us.
“Must be getting close to midnight,” he said. “Hey, park where we can watch.”
“What are we doing here? I’m a little old to be hiding from my parents with my boyfriend, and
you’re…” I looked him over. “In no shape for anything that involves being touched.”
“Who says? I figure you still owe me a kiss up here.”
I rolled my eyes. “You’re a sentimental old—”
“And you’re a tease who still owes me a kiss.”
“Fine.” I slid over beside him and raised his hat. “Just one problem, though. Your lip’s already
split, and this time, I didn’t do it.”
He laughed. “Just come here.”
I did.
He was sweeter than ever, asking and accepting and just letting me lead him. I’d never been
anything but deliriously out of control with Marshall, but when he let me take over, the thrill sent
shivers down my spine. It was a power trip, knowing I could make him shiver and beg for mercy, and
it made me want more. He moaned softly as I caught his swollen bottom lip between my own and
traced his scruffy jaw with my fingertips.
“Hey,” he whispered when I pulled back. “We finally did that right.”
“Practice makes perfect.” And then I practiced some more.
“I just had an idea,” he mumbled into my lips.
“Should I be worried?”
“Nah, you’ll love it. Well.” He frowned. “Maybe not at first.”
“Marshall Walker, what are you talking about?”
“About getting married.”
I tilted my head back to eye him suspiciously. “Yes? It’s not like we’ve settled anything.”
“Then let’s do it. Right now—tomorrow, I mean.”
I squinted. “Tomorrow is New Year’s Day.”
“Oh, right. How about Monday?”
“For what? What are we talking about?”
He gave me a sheepish grin and pointed at his broken arm with one of his sprained wrists. “I can’t
put my socks on. Guess that clinches it. I’m leaving the ranch for a while no matter what, huh?”
I shook my head and put my hand up. “You lost me. What is this about Monday and marriage, and…
and socks?”
“It’s crazy, but that’s kind of us, right? I mean, unless you wanted a big wedding.” He paused and
narrowed his eyes at me. “Did you?”
“It’s not like I’ve given it much thought!”
He looked hurt. “You haven’t?”
“Well, not… What exactly are we talking about?”
“Oh, just a courthouse wedding for now. Then we can really do a party up big come summer, when
you’re looking all tanned and gorgeous like you always do, and I have both arms again. Maybe a long
honeymoon in… well, you pick, since I picked the wedding date.”
My eyes were glazing over. “What? ”
“Of course, that only works if you’re good with that ‘sickness and health’ thing right off the bat.
Showering is going to be interesting for a while. Would be a whole lot more bearable if I had a sweet
nurse rather than Luke or Evan. Luke would probably hit my cast just for fun, and Evan—”
“So, you want to get married on Monday just so I can help you get dressed while you heal?”
“Admit it. You like me when I’m helpless,” he said with a wolfish smile.
I kneaded my eyes. “I can’t believe we’re talking about this. Weren’t we just fighting like
yesterday?”
“That’s ‘miscommunicating,’ sweetheart. Fighting is what we used to do.”
“But why would we just toss caution to the wind and get married in thirty-six hours?”
He gestured with his chin like he wanted me to come closer. So, I did. “Because,” he whispered,
“that’s what I love best about you. You’re not afraid of anything and you jump into life with both feet.
My mind’s made up. If yours is, too, and you wouldn’t object to, uh…” He looked down. “… helping
me for a while…”
I cupped his cheek and just stared at him while I started to laugh. I laughed until I had tears in my
eyes. “You know what, Marshall Walker? It just sounds so crazy. I don’t think I can resist.”
His grin split his face. “That’s my woman. I love you, sweetheart.”
I slowly lowered my lips to his. “You can call me that anytime. I love you more, cowboy.”
We were really doing this. Him and me, me and him, forever, the way it should have started ten
years ago. And to think we finally got engaged over a few broken bones and a wild idea at… “Hey,” I
said as I broke off kissing him. “It’s almost midnight. Happy New Year to us.”
Marshall nuzzled my cheek and gently cradled me with his least injured arm. “Happy New Year,
sugar. Wanna stay here and watch the fireworks for a minute? They’re starting to really pick up.”
I lifted my head and looked. “Marshall? Those red and blue flashes… aren’t fireworks.”
Something tapped on the driver’s window and I turned around. It was Sheriff Wyatt, and he shone
his flashlight in at us. “License and registration, you two.”
Chapter 25
“I ’ve heard crazier ideas, but not many. Are you both sure of this?” Dusty asked.
It was just us Walkers, hanging around the breakfast table. At least I had my two legs, but
getting out of bed and to the table with everything broken and cracked on me had been an exercise in
misery. But I wanted to do this right, and it started with sharing the news like a man.
“Yeah, we talked it all over. We’re both sure.”
Evan shook his head. “Seems awfully fast, Marshall.”
“Maybe if you look at it that way, but I’ve wanted her since we were kids. If anything, I took too
long coming around to the point.”
“Well, I’m all for it,” Luke announced.
I looked at him funny. “Why? It’s not because of her coffee, is it?”
He shrugged. “Maybe. Or maybe I just think it’s high time you did something spontaneous.”
“But getting married is serious,” Dusty argued. “You don’t make a lifetime commitment just
because you got hurt in an accident.”
Dad had been leaning on the table, cupping his chin in his hand and watching me through narrowed
eyes. He finally smiled. “They’ll do alright, Dusty. With most folks I'd say they had their heads put on
backwards, but Marshall and Kelli will be just dandy. Don’t think there’s any other woman for him.”
“There isn’t. I looked.”
He laughed and got up from his chair. “Well, that’s fine. You’ll both figure it out.” Dad started
toward the stairs, then straightened and came back. “Just one thing. What time last night did you two
settle this?”
“Why does that matter?”
He gestured impatiently. “Just curious is all. What time?”
I looked at Evan, who just shrugged. “You’re acting like you put some kind of bet on it.”
Dad laughed. “I wouldn’t do that. So? Was it before or after midnight?”
I groaned and reached for my wallet, but then I remembered I was wearing sweatpants. And I
couldn’t reach anything without help. “I think my wallet’s on the counter. You can check the timestamp
on the ticket we got last night.”
Luke howled with laughter, and he was the first to jump up and fetch my wallet. He fished around
inside until he found the ticket. “Eleven fifty-nine, baby!”
Dad got a big grin on his face. “Huh. Isn’t that funny? Well, congratulations, son. I’ll be upstairs.”
Dusty was shaking his head as Dad headed up. “You know he put a bet on you.”
I scowled. “Obviously. I just wonder who he was betting against.”
“Probably Hoss Jones or one of his other buddies. I wouldn’t worry about it.”
“Too late.”
“How are you feeling?” Luke asked. “Need more pain meds? Trip to the bathroom? Shower? I
hope you don’t need… you know. Anything too personal. I draw the line at birthing calves.”
“See?” I pointed at my brother. “This is why I’m getting married tomorrow. I don’t need any of you
ya-hoos making fun of me while I’m laid up.”
Kelli chose that moment to open the door. She’d stayed down at the bunkhouse again last night so
she could be close, and my brothers had just left her side-by-side to come up whenever she was
ready. She stopped midway through the door and smiled at me. And I knew deep down in my broken
bones that this was how it was supposed to be—the two of us, fighting our way through this business
called life. Together.
“Morning, everyone.”
My brothers all stood up to greet her. Kelli didn’t care about that kind of thing, but Dad had grilled
that into us when we were kids, and you just didn’t sit when a woman was standing. I struggled to my
feet, too, but a lot more slowly. “Morning, sweetheart.”
“Hey, Kelli, does this mean you’ll be making us coffee all the time?” Luke asked hopefully.
She grinned at him. “If you say please. I see you already heard the news.”
“Marshall doesn’t waste any time,” Dusty said as he grabbed his hat. “I gotta go feed. Catch you
later, Kelli.”
She watched him go, then turned a quizzical look on the rest of us after the door closed behind him.
“Is Dusty okay?”
“He’ll be fine,” Luke said, a little too quickly. “But he’s right. Time to start the chores. Good thing
Brandon’s back to work so he can fill in for Marshall for a while.”
