I almost laughed at the irony of that, but I stopped before my ribs could scream in protest. Funny
that Brandon had gotten hurt on the very horse that ended up saving me.
“I’m off, too,” Evan echoed. “You guys need anything?”
“We’ll be fine,” Kelli assured him.
Evan paused and winked at her. “Congratulations. See you later.”
“Well!” she said after we were alone. “Where to? The couch? Back to your bed? I’m not helping
you shower until at least tomorrow.”
I smiled. “None of those. I need to talk to your dad.”
“Y ou know, my dad isn’t the type to expect you to ask for my hand. We never did things that way
in my family.”
“Well, we did. And anyway, I figure he deserves at least an explanation, and maybe a personal
invite to the ceremony.”
“What ceremony? It’s just going to be Judge Baker signing the paperwork between traffic tickets.
Do you think he’ll waive our ticket, since we had mitigating circumstances?”
“One can only hope. I can’t open this door on my own.”
“Right.” She turned the truck off and came around to my side. “You probably can’t get down
without help, either.”
I grimaced. “No, but I’m not sure what you can do about it.”
We wrestled for a few minutes, with her probably hurting me more than she helped, and me yelping
and trying to tough it out as I tumbled down from the truck. By the time I was swaying on my own two
feet and trying to battle back the pain nausea, there were two faces staring out the front window at us.
“We’ve been spotted.”
“Yeah.” She bit her lower lip. “I’m not looking forward to this.”
“Come on, sweetheart. It’ll be fine.”
It wasn’t fine.
Her dad turned pale and her mom was so excited that she ran to the kitchen and started making
food. Lots of food. Kelli patted my knee in reassurance and followed her mom, but that left me facing
her dad alone.
“Are you two sure of this? It’s awfully fast.”
“Yes, sir. I’ve never been more sure of anything in my life.”
“But in such a hurry? Seems like it would be better to take some time working things out.”
“Mr. Mason, sir, respectfully, I’d like to begin as we mean to go on. I plan to love and care for your
daughter for the rest of my life. She is my first and last. Nothing is more important. I want to make a
life with her, and I don’t see any reason to sit on my pockets when we’re both of the same mind.”
“Well, where will you live?”
“We decided to live at her house for a while, ‘least till I’m on the mend. I have a share of the ranch
and a piece of land where we can build a house when we’re ready. We thought we’d take our time
and see if that was what we wanted before we did anything crazy.”
“‘Crazy’ is getting married tomorrow.”
I grinned. “No, sir. ‘Crazy’ would be doing anything that’s not for her. Kelli is all I need. The rest?
‘Long as she’s there, everything is right.”
“Aw, that’s sweet, baby.” Kelli had come back in behind me and she laid a hand on my shoulder.
“Daddy, really, we’ll be just fine. Don’t fret about us.”
Her father sighed as his gaze rested on her, and I saw the fear breaking in his eyes. He was just a
dad who wanted what was best for his little girl, and he wasn’t sure I was it.
“I’ll protect her with my life,” I promised him. “And you don’t need to worry about wild horses or
hard ranch living.” I looked up and smiled at her. “This woman can handle anything she sets her mind
to.”
He bowed his head in defeat. “Well. I just want to make sure she’s happy.”
“I am, Daddy. And now that we’ve got that settled, Ma says it’s time to eat.” She leaned down and
gave me a “don’t blow this” look. “Hope you’re hungry.”
I eased myself to my feet. “Lead the way, sweetheart. Can’t let your mom’s cooking get cold.”
T hat was the fastest wedding I’d ever heard of. Marshall and I were man and wife in less time
than it took to dismiss our traffic ticket. It seemed like it, anyway. Our families had crowded
into the back of the courthouse and they’d barely sat down before they had to file out again.
But I got what I came for. A new last name and the promise of a lifetime with my best friend.
We celebrated ranch-style, with platters of Marshall’s favorite grilled steaks served up by his dad,
Dusty’s mashed potatoes, Evan’s famous hot barbecue sauce, and some mystery stew cooked up by
Luke. Marshall warned me not to try it, but my mom loved it and asked for his recipe. There was
laughter and teasing, well-wishes and bets placed on how long it would be before we had six kids.
And then it was just us.
“Remind me not to try to go anywhere tomorrow,” Marshall groaned as he gimped his way to the
couch after we got home. Our home now.
I sank down beside him. “When I’m done with you, you won’t be able to go anywhere.”
“That sounds promising.” He turned to press a kiss to my forehead. “Don’t break me too hard,
though. You’re the one who has to help me get up in the morning.”
“Who said anything about getting up?” I asked with an evil grin as I slid my hand over his thigh.
“Well, when you put it that way…” He reached for me—carefully—and I kissed my cowboy for
hours.
G ray dawn was slow to creep through the window the next morning. Another heavy snow had
fallen overnight, and all the world was muffled and quiet. Except for Marshall’s snoring.
I ran my fingers affectionately through his thick, wavy hair, thinking of all those years I’d longed to
be able to do that. He shifted in his sleep but didn’t stop snoring. I kissed him softly on the cheek. He
needed his healing rest, and I was wide awake for the day, so I went to the kitchen to put on the
coffee.
I peered out the window as a garbage truck rattled by. The snow plow had come through a while
ago, waking me up, of course. Now the street was filling with cars and buses and people, all headed
to work or school. It sure wasn’t as peaceful as the ranch.
It was sweet of Marshall to think of us living here. Really, it was the ultimate gesture—starting our
life together on my terms and in my world. But this wasn’t where I’d always pictured myself, all
those years I’d fantasized about being his. I saw us in the mountains, cows grazing outside the
windows, and my horse waiting for me in the barn.
And when my husband worked, no matter when or what, I’d just put on my gloves and boots and
work by his side. It would take some learning on my part and maybe some patience from him, but that
was how it was supposed to be. I’d married a rancher. I’d talk to him about it when he got up.
The kettle whistled, so I poured it over the French Press. While I waited for it, an arm slid around
me, and warm lips caressed my neck. “Mornin’, angel.”
I chuckled and turned around to kiss him back. “Angel, huh? That’s a new one.”
“After the dreams I had all night, I’m pretty sure you’re heaven sent. Boy, howdy! Speaking of
heavenly…” He sniffed the air. “Starting off spoiling me, eh?” He kissed my neck again, which was
becoming my new favorite thing. Well… second favorite. “Smells delicious.”
“Uh-uh. No coffee for you until you get off the painkillers.”
“Aw, come on. You wouldn’t deny a wounded man his only comfort, would you?”
I put a fist on my hip. “You used that same line last night, sir.”
“It worked, didn’t it?”
“Not this time. You get decaf tea.”
He wrinkled his nose. “I’d rather drink dish water.”
“That can be arranged.”
“You’re a cruel woman, Kelli Walker.” He leaned in and nuzzled my ear, and I nearly melted. “If I
can’t have coffee, I’ll just limp back to bed. I’d sure hate to go alone.”
“Well, you’re going to have to.” I picked my phone off the counter to show him a text that had come
earlier. “Cody dropped Joy off at the ranch for me. I have some chores to do.”
His face sagged. “You’re kidding. You’re leaving me to go feed your horse? Can’t the guys take
care of her just this once?”
“Well, a girl’s got to have her priorities.”
He shook his head, and a slow grin smoldered on his face. “If that’s how it is, let’s go. I’ll follow
you.”
I tilted my head and put the phone down, ringer off. “Actually, no. I changed my mind. Everything I
need for now is right here.”
I slipped my arm around his least injured side and whispered something in his ear. Something that
would be a lot of fun without ever leaving the house. He smiled and turned about three shades of red.
“What do you think of that idea?” I murmured.
“Sweetheart, I’m your cowboy. Lead the way.”
Epilogue
A stick dropped on my foot.
I pushed back from my desk to see Daisy, our youngest Border Collie, wagging her tail
hopefully. “Not now.” I kicked the stick off my toe.
Quick as lightning, she plopped it back on top of my foot. And stared.
“No.” I picked the stick up—of course, it had to be slimy—and dropped it in the garbage can. She
knew better than to fetch it from there. I wiped my hand on my jeans and bent over my desk again. My
pen danced in the air as I chewed on my words. It had to be just…
Another stick landed on my foot.
Duke was in my room now, too, and both dogs were focused on the stick with such intensity you
would’ve thought they were trying to ignite it with just their eyes.
“Oh, for crying out loud, you two. I’m busy!” I dropped the second stick into the garbage and gave
both dogs a stern look. “Keep it up, and I’ll boot you out.”
Both tails wagged, and Daisy gave the faintest whine.
“That’ll do,” I said sternly.
They dropped to the floor and heaved gloomy sighs. Finally. I turned back to my work.
My work. It wasn’t exactly mine, though, was it? At least, no one could know it was mine. But that
was the way I’d always wanted it, so why should now be any different?
Because this time, it was for her. And since she barely knew I was alive, it was just as well that
my name wasn’t attached to it. That was how I consoled myself. I groaned, and my head plopped onto
the desk. How had I gotten into this fix? It had all started innocently enough.
I gulped when I saw the email pop up. Stockman’s Magazine had sent me a reply! It had been so
long since I submitted I wasn’t expecting an answer anymore. My fingers trembled as I tapped the
message.
This was how rejection letters started. I’d seen enough of them to know. But I kept reading anyway,
and… and it wasn’t a rejection.
We will be proud to feature your poem, The Cowboy’s Call, in our December
I almost dropped my phone. I was barely awake anyway, and I thought at first it was a dream. I’d
hardly slept all night—it was nothing, just… a stupid brother. Really stupid. Dumb enough not to
know when he was outclassed. Anyway, the notification lit up my room till I picked the thing up to
turn it off.
But this was worth stumbling out of bed early for. I felt flushed all over. Published, at last! I
wanted to whoop for joy, throw my hat in the air, and bark at the moon. I was going to be published! A
real cowboy poet! I was no Baxter Black, but I’d cut my teeth on his writing, and I always hoped that
someday, someone would smile over my words the way I’d basked in his.
I should tell someone. I’d kept my poems under my hat for two years because in my family, you
didn’t waste working hours on stuff that didn’t put beef on someone’s table. I solved that by writing at
night, but I didn’t expect anyone to really get it. I couldn’t stand the teasing from Luke or the long,
heavy look from my dad when he thought I wasn’t pulling my weight. So, I’d kept quiet.
I read the email about ten times more, then celebrated by turning on my desk light and trying to
capture the moment in a few lines. Once I was done, I pulled my clothes on and rolled out into the
frozen dawn. I was about to bust inside, but stock still needed hay, and now I was running late.
I got the yearlings on the lower eighty fed and headed back up to the barn to grab alfalfa for the
broodmares. Marshall was already there when I pulled up. Hmm. Marshall might be the one I could
talk to. Less stoic than Evan, more serious than Luke. Should I tell him? Maybe on a full stomach,
because I was so nervous, I thought I was going to be sick.
“You’re getting back later than usual this morning,” he said.
I shivered and pulled my bandanna back over my mouth. Today wasn’t the day. “Didn’t get up till
five.”
“What’s the matter? Trouble sleeping?”
Trouble? I’d tossed and turned all night, but it wasn’t Marshall’s fault. It was something Luke had
started yesterday when he asked her out, and she’d been bored enough to say yes. I’d been queasy
ever since. “I guess. I’m late getting feed to the broodmares. Are you busy?”
“Always,” he said, but he came to help me, anyway. “So, what kept you up last night?”
I threw a hay bale and shook my head. “Nothing. Too much TV, I guess.”
“You never watch TV. Last I heard, your TV doesn’t even work.”
“I have my phone. What’s your excuse?”
“My excuse?” he asked as we tugged a couple of bales together. “What do you mean?”
We chucked the last bales at the same time. “You were up, too. I heard you in the kitchen a few
times.”
“That’s because that salad you made for dinner wouldn’t keep a gopher full for more than half an
hour. I was starved.”
“At least the salad was edible. Someone needs to tell Luke the truth about his stew.”
“I’ll let you do it. You all set? I’d better get out to the colts.”
“Yeah.” I started for my truck, then stopped when an idea struck me. I might not have the guts to tell
Marshall about my writing, but I might have enough gumption to stop Luke from doing something even
stupider than he was already doing. “Hey, do you have plans on Saturday?”
“What plans would I have?”
I shrugged, trying to seem nonchalant. “I don’t know. I’m just tired of not doing anything. Luke…”
that idiot… “says he has a date, and Evan never goes anywhere. Wanna go to town?”
“Sure. Been a while since someone else cooked my steak.”
We both hopped down from the haystack, and I got back into my truck. There was one of the day’s
issues managed, if not resolved. As for the other… I paused for just a minute before I put the truck in
reverse to look at that email one more time. Just to make sure it was real.
O kay, so I have two brothers who are idiots. Luke wouldn’t know what to do with a nice girl if
she gave him an instruction manual, and Marshall couldn’t even force himself to be polite to a
woman he finally confessed feelings for. The whole night was humiliating, just watching my older
brothers make fools of themselves.
At least Marshall talked to Kelli Mason at the end of the evening. Actually, they looked like they
were arguing, but that’s a form of talking. Luke, though… I couldn’t believe his date didn’t call
someone else to pick her up. I stuck close all night, just trying to make sure she was okay.
She was too sweet for him. Too innocent and gentle and far too intelligent. Not that he could see
that. Luke was my favorite brother in a lot of ways, but he’d gone too far this time, asking out the
prettiest girl in town and thinking she was no different from all the rest.
The only good thing to come out of the whole evening was that Marshall introduced me to a new
friend. I’d seen Austen Conrad around. Evan had bumped into him once or twice at the sales and
everyone had been talking about the new guy in town. I had just figured he was like all the other green
horns who came out here with too much money and not enough sense, but Austen seemed like he had a
good head for business. And he was a nice guy, too.
We were hunched over a paper napkin, our meals half-eaten and getting cold, as Austen quizzed me
about herd genetics and conversion ratios. I was trying to write out some thoughts for him with a
stubby Keno pencil, and he was nodding and taking it all in like a sponge. “What about hybrid vigor?”
he asked. “Do you ever dabble in outcrosses?”
“Yeah, about every three generations. Look at—”
“Hang on, just a second,” he stopped me. “Isn’t that another one of your brothers over there?”
I looked over my shoulder and rolled my eyes as Luke tossed a peanut in the air and tried to catch it
with his mouth. He missed. “That’s Luke. He’s not housebroken.”
Austen laughed. “I’ve heard he’s a good guy, though.”
I swirled the pencil over the napkin, accidentally tearing it. Now I’d have to start over. “He is,
really. I just wish he would get serious about life.”
Austen crossed his arms and nodded toward my brother. “Looks to me like his date is thinking the
same thing.”
I risked another glance over my shoulder. At her. She was shielding her face with her hand and
nursing her drink, so she didn’t have to talk to Luke.
“She’s not really his type,” I said, my voice as flat as I could make it.
“Doesn’t look like it. I saw her at Cody Haskins’ wedding.”
Yeah. So I recalled. Austen and Luke had both been in the long line of guys trying to dance with her
all night, and I hadn’t even managed to ask once.
“She was there,” was all I said.
“What was her name again?” Austen wasn’t even pretending to look at me anymore. He was
leaning half out of his chair, admiring the golden cascade of hair streaming down her back. You
couldn’t not stare at her hair.
“Jess Thompkins.”
“As in Thompkins Auto Body?”
I nodded.
“Huh.” He shifted back to the middle of his seat and looked down at the torn napkin. “Hey, I didn’t
mean to take up your whole night. I’d really like to pick your brain some more, though. Do you have
any time next week?”
I shrugged. “I can make time.”
“Sweet. I still have some chores to do, so I’ll take off. Dinner’s on me. I really appreciate your
help.”
“No problem.”
I watched him pay the tab and leave, and then there was nothing more for me to do. I’d ridden with
Marshall, and he was still arguing with Kelli Mason over at her table. I sat back to finish my soda and
waited. That was when Jess passed by me on her way to the restrooms.
Luke went the other way, leaving their coats and even Jess’s purse still hanging on the backs of
their chairs. It was a small town. People did that. But it gave me an idea.
I fingered the printout of the poem in my pocket. The poem, the one that was going to be published.
I wasn’t sure what had made me print it out and bring it tonight, as if I could find a chance to show it
to someone. There was only one person in the room who would probably like it, and her coat was
hanging right there.
I didn’t need to claim it as mine. She could just read it and hopefully enjoy it. Would she find it
creepy, having a poem show up in her pocket? I prayed not. I had never worked up the courage to ask
her out, but maybe I could do this one small thing. Before I could talk myself out of it, I was on my
feet, the slip of paper concealed in my hand.
And it was done.
“G ood night, Jerry! ‘Night, Tucker!” I waved at the guys in the paint booth as I walked out of
the shop, keys jingling in my hand. They both stopped cleaning their sprayers long enough to
wave back. My dad’s office was next, and I found him at his desk, squinting over his glasses and
typing up the day’s invoices.
“I can do those in the morning if you want,” I offered.
He looked up and took off his glasses. “Oh, hey, sweetie. That would be nice, but I have to get Jed
Watts’s total sent over to his insurance company this evening. You heading home?”
“Yeah. I need to get chores done early and get cleaned up. I sort of have a date.”
“Oh? Who’s the lucky guy?”
I laughed and shook my head. “Luke Walker. I can’t believe I agreed to go out with him.”
Dad replaced his glasses. “Neither can I. I wouldn’t have thought he’d be your type.”
“Not really, but he’s not a bad guy. I’ve certainly been out with bigger pigs than Luke Walker.”
He snorted. “That’s not saying much. Have you ever thought of going out with Will? I think he likes
you.”
“Our Will? Dad, I’m not dating a co-worker. It would make things too weird.”
“But you don’t exactly work with him. You have the whole back mechanic shop to yourself, and
he’s up front in body repair.”
“Still too close. Don’t worry about it! I’m not getting serious about Luke Walker. He flirts with
everything that walks, and that’s not for me, but he’s fun to dance with. You’re always after me to get
my nose out of the book and get out more.”
He sighed. “Well, have a good time, then. See you later.”
I walked over to kiss him on the cheek. “Bye, Dad.”
The motion lights came to life when I pulled into the darkened driveway. I parked in front of the
barn and zipped my coat as I got out. I only had one horse now, but Kelli had just moved her new
horse in a couple of weeks ago, so old Nash had a friend again. Two horses made for a little more
work, but Nash was actually less messy now that he had another horse to talk to over the bars. It was
good for everyone.
I switched into my mud boots in the tack room and walked out to the stalls. Both horses were
already fed, with fresh bedding and topped-off water buckets. I shouldn’t have been surprised. Kelli
had been beating me here lately, and I had hardly picked a stall since she’d brought Joy home. I just
reached through the door frame and stroked Nash’s forelock. “Good night, sweet boy. See you in the
morning.” He nudged my hand, then went back to his hay.
I got back in my car and drove it around to the house. It was weird, living back at home after being
on my own for five years. Even weirder with Mom gone. But Dad needed me more than he’d admit,
and it was just the change I’d needed from Oklahoma.
I sent a text to Kelli, thanking her for doing my chores again. It meant I’d have more time for a
proper shower now, which was definitely in order. I wasn’t exactly trying to impress Luke, but a girl
just didn’t go out to dinner with automotive grease under her fingernails. I untangled the grimy
handkerchief from my hair, grabbed the bottle of heavy-duty hand soap, and got to work.
If I hurried, I’d have time to pick up my book for a few minutes before Luke showed up.
“H ey there, sweet thing.” Luke leaned against the porch railing, giving me a cheesy wink when I
opened the door. And suddenly, I wasn’t so sure this had been a good idea.
“Let me get my coat,” was all I said.
He wanted to hold hands. It was only twenty feet to his truck, but for some reason, he thought it was
appropriate to grab my hand while I was still trying to put on my gloves and lead me to the door. Then
he just left me standing there until he went around to the driver’s side and hit the unlock button.
Things went downhill from there.
“Where do you wanna go?” he asked. We were already in town by this point.
I gave him a confused look. “We got this far. I thought you had something planned.”
“Oh, I did, but it just came to mind that maybe you’d like somethin’ fancy.” He threw me another
wink.
“Like the steakhouse? That would be fine. Were you thinking of something else?”
“Nachos are on special at the pub.”
I blew out a sigh. At least at the pub, dinner would be over quickly. “Fine.”
We sat down and made our order, and then we were just looking across the table at each other.
“So,” I said after an awkward silence.
He nodded. “Yup.” And then he looked over my head at the football replay from the TV in the bar.
“What do you like to do in your spare time?”
“What do you mean?” His eyes didn’t leave the TV.
“You know. Movies, books. Do you have a workshop you like to tinker in?”
“Workshop? Heck yeah, we have a workshop. We all have to weld and stuff like when the
mower…” His forehead wrinkled and his gaze tightened when something exciting happened in the
game. “Get him! Run, you butterfingers! Aw, man. What’s wrong with you? Is that ref blind?”
When it went to a commercial break, he looked at me again. “Yeah, we fix stuff. Me and Dusty had
to put a new head on my truck a while back. Didn’t it sound good?”
“Oh. Um, yes, it did,” I said, as politely as possible. “I was thinking more like a wood shop.
Crafts, projects, that kind of thing. Do you have any hobbies?”
He grabbed a handful of peanuts and stuffed them in his mouth. Then he talked around them. “What
for?”
“Just something to do. Everyone has to have some kind of outlet.”
“Not us Walkers. We work.” He grabbed another handful of peanuts.
“What about when you take a break? You’re a pretty good dancer,” I remembered.
He grinned. “That’s right, I am. I’m a better roper, though. Pretty good at tyin’ things up.”
It took me a second to realize he was making some kind of innuendo. I probably turned about six
shades of scarlet, but Luke didn’t notice. The game was back on.
“Go! Catch him, you filthy varmint! What the heck are they paying you guys so much for if you can’t
take down—”
“Luke… Luke! You know they can’t hear you, right?”
“Half the fun of watching the game, yelling at the quarterback. So, where were we? Oh! Nachos are
here.”
I spent the rest of the meal with my forehead resting on my fingertips and my eyes hidden.
Hopefully, if someone I knew saw me, they’d think I had a headache and wouldn’t try to talk to me. I
was frankly too embarrassed to look around and see who else was there. What was I thinking,
agreeing to go out with this oaf? Never again.
“Wanna dance?”
I scarcely looked up. No way did I want to make a bigger scene than we were already making.
“There’s no music.”
“I can fix that.” Luke pulled a handful of quarters out of his pocket. “I was looking forward to
trotting around the dance floor tonight. What do you like, two-step or line dance?”
“You know, actually, I’m not feeling all that great. Refried beans and I don’t get along.”
“Oh. Well, I’ve got a stomach tube and some drench in the truck. Need a hit?”
I just stared at him. “What are you talking about?”
“It was just a cattleman’s joke, sweet thing. Calm down.”
I shook my head and got up. “I’m not your ‘sweet thing,’ Luke. I’m going to the powder room.”
He made a face. “What’s a powder room?”
I didn’t even bother answering.
He drove me home after that. I don’t think he realized just how over it I was, because he tried to
reach his arm around me when he walked me to my door. That was not happening, but no matter how
wrong for me he was, I didn’t want to hurt his feelings.
“Sorry,” I apologized as I ducked his embrace. “Still feeling a little sick.”
“Does that mean no necking by the porch light?”
My stomach rolled in earnest. “Most definitely not. Thanks for dinner, though.”
“Sure. Hey, Friday night is Karaoke. You in?”
“Sorry. I can’t carry a tune in a bucket.”
“Well, catch you next time, I guess? ‘Night.”
“Good night.”
I let myself in the door and pressed it closed, leaning against it and sagging in relief. Worst date of
my life.
It wasn’t like I’d had high expectations, but I didn’t think it could go that badly. It was like he was
still twelve or something! Were all men overgrown adolescents, or just the ones I’d gone out with? I
ticked off a mental list.
There was Colby, whose mother still ironed his drawers. Porter, who thought the way to woo a girl
was to put his hands everywhere. Tanner… Tanner didn’t count because I’d turned him down when he
spat out a stream of tobacco immediately after asking me out. Rafe, Ryan, Robert, Sam, Shep, Stu…
all good-looking ne’er-do-wells who thought primarily with their little brains.
Was it really so much to ask? There had to be a few left who didn’t paw a girl for attention and
who could act like a grown-up. Maybe even some who thought about more than football and how
good his truck sounded. Just not in this town.
I sighed and pulled off my gloves to stuff them in my coat pockets. And that was when I found it. A
sheet of paper, crisply folded into perfect quarters. That hadn’t been there earlier, had it? I unfolded
and scanned it. It was a poem, but no poem I’d ever read. And it was really good.
I turned the paper over a couple of times. No author, no address. Just a folded poem stuck in my
pocket. Had I picked it up somewhere? Surely, I’d have remembered. I only wore my nice coat for
going to town, but I’d worn it to church on Sunday. I couldn’t have forgotten something like this in that
little time.
I read the poem again, more slowly. Whoever wrote it had a gentle way with words and an
expressive turn of mind. The way they described a sunrise—a tender challenge issued each day to
live the moment one was given—it made me choke up a bit. A Cowboy’s Call, it was titled. I couldn’t
know who had written it, of course, but it seemed to speak into my soul with the voice of a man. A
man who knew honor and love and beauty and sacrifice.
And, apparently, a man who had to be from around here. I carried the page to my room to spread it
on my dresser, where I could read it over and over again. And I wondered who he was.
Dusty and Jess's story continues in An Angel for the Cowboy

emphasis.strong: A Winter Surprise for the Cowboy

section.p: Blake Walker has built a legacy in his family and his ranch. His five boys are starting to find love
and build their own lives, and he's beginning to wonder what adventures are left for him.
Meryl Justice has raised everyone's kids but her own, and now she's looking forward to retirement
from the job she's had for thirty years. She loves her home and her farm animals, but is that all there
is?
Find out when these two hearts set out to discover if wintertime might not just be the best time of
all to fall in love!
Click HERE to get your story.
About The Author
Tess Thornton writes inspirational western romances from her Oregon horse farm. She has a science
degree she never uses because she's better at daydreaming than keeping schedules. Her authentic
western stories are inspired by the real ranching life and people she loves.
Tess's days are filled with country kids, stubborn dogs, and one special guy in a cowboy hat. She
spends more time cleaning the barn than the house, and she should never be left unsupervised with
power tools. When she's not fantasizing about training a champion reining horse, she likes to head to
the beach with her family and her book. And don't forget the snacks.
If you enjoy stories about strong, imperfect people who just happen to be perfect for each other,
families pulling together through thick and thin, and heart-pounding horseback adventures, then you
might enjoy Tess's books.

p.strong: To learn about special deals and new releases, please follow Tess on Book Bub!
